topics.title: Sieve Analysis: To analyse a given sample using a set of standard sieves and thus to determine the specific surface area, the volume surface mean diameter and the mass mean diameter by differential analysis and cumulative analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sieve Analysis: To analyse a given sample using a set of standard sieves and thus to determine the specific surface area, the volume surface mean diameter and the mass mean diameter by differential analysis and cumulative analysis

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sieve Analysis** is a fundamental **particle size distribution (PSD)** measurement technique in Chemical Engineering wherein a representative sample of granular material (typically 45 µm to 100 mm) is passed through a stacked column of woven wire sieves of progressively decreasing aperture size, and the mass retained on each sieve is recorded to determine the size distribution, **specific surface area**, **volume-surface mean diameter (Dvs)**, and **mass mean diameter (D̄w)** of the bulk particulate solid.

The procedure is governed by standards such as **IS 460 (Part 1)**, **ASTM E11**, and **ISO 3310-1**, which specify sieve aperture sizes, tolerances, and the geometric mean convention for representing the size of particles in a given fraction.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a **multi-storey apartment building where each floor has a turnstile gate of a specific width**. Tall adults get stuck at the second-floor turnstile (larger aperture), while children can pass all the way down to the narrowest gate at the ground floor. If you **weigh the people stranded at each floor**, you essentially know the size distribution of the population that entered the building. Sieve analysis works on exactly the same principle — coarse particles get "stranded" on coarse (large aperture) sieves, fine particles fall through to finer (small aperture) sieves at the bottom.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Sieve Analysis Matters in Chemical Engineering:**
> - Catalysts, ores, cements, fertilizers, and pharmaceutical granules are all processed as particulates.
> - **Reaction rate**, **filtration efficiency**, **heat transfer**, and **fluidization behavior** all depend on the exposed surface area per unit mass (i.e., specific surface area).
> - Hence, characterising the **average particle diameter** and the **distribution width** is a mandatory step in plant design.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Highlighted)

- **Standard Sieve Series**: Tyler, ASTM E11, ISO 565 — aperture sizes follow a **√2 or 2^(1/4)** geometric progression.
- **Mass of Sample**: Typically **100 g to 500 g** depending on the particle density.
- **Specific Surface Area (Sw)**: Exposed surface per unit **mass** of solid [m²/kg] or per unit **volume** [m²/m³].
- **Volume-Surface Mean Diameter (Dvs or D₃₂)**: The Sauter mean diameter.
- **Mass Mean Diameter (D̄w or D₄₃)**: The weighted average diameter based on mass fractions.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Sieving Procedure — Structured Logic

1. **Sample Preparation**: The bulk solid is dried (usually at 105 °C – 110 °C for 1 h) to remove moisture that may cause agglomeration.
2. **Selection of Sieve Stack**: Sieves are arranged in a column from **largest aperture (top)** to **smallest aperture (bottom)** with a **pan (receiver)** at the bottom.
3. **Charging the Sample**: A known mass (M) of the dry sample is carefully poured onto the top sieve.
4. **Agitation**: The stack is placed on a **mechanical sieve shaker** (Ro-Tap or vibratory type) for a stipulated period (commonly **10–15 minutes**).
5. **Weighing Retained Fractions**: Each sieve is weighed separately after agitation. The mass retained is converted to a **mass fraction** or **percentage retained**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why does shaking matter?** Mechanical vibration ensures that particles properly orient themselves to pass through the apertures. Insufficient shaking leads to **under-reporting of fines**; excessive shaking may cause **blinding** (fines lodging in apertures).

### 2.2 Representation of Particle Diameter in a Sieve Fraction

For particles lying between two adjacent sieves of aperture **D₁ (upper)** and **D₂ (lower)**:

$$ D_{pi} = \sqrt{D_1 \cdot D_2} $$

This is the **geometric mean diameter** of the i-th fraction, representing the average size of all particles retained on sieve *i*.

### 2.3 Differential Analysis

In differential analysis, we work with the **mass fraction Δφᵢ** retained on each sieve:

$$ \Delta \phi_i = \frac{m_i}{M} $$

where **mᵢ** = mass retained on sieve *i* and **M** = total mass of the sample.

### 2.4 Cumulative Analysis

Here we accumulate the mass from either the **coarse end (retained)** or the **fine end (passing)**:

- **Cumulative mass retained (coarse basis):** Σ mᵢ up to sieve *i*
- **Cumulative mass passing (fine basis):** M − Σ mᵢ up to sieve *i*

When the cumulative passing is plotted against sieve aperture on semi-log paper, the resulting curve is the **cumulative particle size distribution**.

### 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **HIGH-YIELD FORMULA TABLE — MEMORISE FOR KTU EXAMS**

| # | Parameter | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|---------|
| 1 | Geometric Mean Diameter of fraction | $D_{pi} = \sqrt{D_1 \cdot D_2}$ | m or µm | Used to represent each sieve fraction |
| 2 | Mass Fraction (Differential) | $\Delta \phi_i = m_i / M$ | dimensionless | Sum = 1 |
| 3 | Cumulative Mass Passing | $P_i = 100 \cdot \left(1 - \sum_{j=1}^{i} \Delta \phi_j\right)$ | % | Plotted vs D (semi-log) |
| 4 | Specific Surface Area per unit volume | $S_v = \sum_i \dfrac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}}$ | m⁻¹ | Volume basis |
| 5 | Specific Surface Area per unit mass | $S_w = \dfrac{6}{\rho_p} \sum_i \dfrac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}}$ | m²/kg | Requires particle density ρₚ |
| 6 | Volume-Surface Mean Diameter (Sauter Mean, D₃₂) | $D_{vs} = \dfrac{1}{\sum_i \Delta \phi_i / D_{pi}} = \dfrac{6}{S_v \rho_p}$ | m | Most relevant for mass/heat transfer |
| 7 | Mass Mean Diameter (weighted by mass fraction) | $\bar{D}_w = \sum_i \Delta \phi_i \cdot D_{pi}$ | m | Weighted arithmetic mean |
| 8 | Mass Mean Diameter (weighted by mass retained) | $\bar{D}_w = \dfrac{\sum_i (m_i \cdot D_{pi})}{\sum_i m_i}$ | m | Equivalent form using mᵢ |
| 9 | Number of Particles per unit mass (approx.) | $N = \dfrac{6 M}{\pi \rho_p \bar{D}_w^3}$ | kg⁻¹ | Useful in kinetic calculations |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL**: Do **NOT** confuse **Dvs (Sauter Mean, D₃₂)** with **D̄w (Mass Mean)**. They are conceptually different — Dvs is dominated by fines (surface), while D̄w is dominated by coarse particles (mass). Always read the question carefully.

### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Catalyst Design**: Surface area determines active sites; Dvs predicts external mass transfer resistance.
- **Cement Industry**: PSD affects setting time, strength, and workability.
- **Pharmaceutical Granulation**: Tablet dissolution rate is governed by specific surface area.
- **Mineral Beneficiation**: Particle size dictates the choice of crushers, grinders, and classifiers.
- **Food & Fertilizer Industry**: Uniform PSD ensures predictable dissolution and packaging behavior.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of Specific Surface Area (Sw)

Consider a single spherical particle of diameter **Dp** and density **ρp**.

**Volume of one particle:**

$$ V_p = \frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3 $$

**Surface area of one particle:**

$$ A_p = \pi D_p^2 $$

**Surface-to-volume ratio of one particle:**

$$ \frac{A_p}{V_p} = \frac{\pi D_p^2}{\frac{\pi}{6} D_p^3} = \frac{6}{D_p} $$

For a mixture of particles where mass fraction **Δφᵢ** has mean diameter **Dpᵢ**, the specific surface area per unit **volume** is the mass-fraction-weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_v &= \sum_i \Delta \phi_i \cdot \left(\frac{A_p}{V_p}\right)_i \\
&= \sum_i \Delta \phi_i \cdot \frac{6}{D_{pi}} \\
&= 6 \sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Dividing by **ρp** to express per unit mass:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w &= \frac{S_v}{\rho_p} = \frac{6}{\rho_p} \sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic**: Multiplying each term by its mass fraction **Δφᵢ** gives the contribution of that fraction to the total surface area; summing over all fractions yields the **bulk specific surface area**.

---

### 3.2 Derivation of Volume-Surface Mean Diameter (Dvs = D₃₂)

Starting from the definition:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\frac{1}{D_{vs}} &= \sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Inverting:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{vs} &= \frac{1}{\sum_i \Delta \phi_i / D_{pi}}
\end{aligned}
$$

Substituting the expression for **Sv**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_v &= 6 \sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}} = \frac{6}{D_{vs}} \\
\therefore D_{vs} &= \frac{6}{S_v}
\end{aligned}
$$

And on a mass basis:

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{vs} &= \frac{6}{S_w \cdot \rho_p}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic**: Dvs is the diameter of a **monodisperse** (uniform) sphere that has the **same surface-to-volume ratio** as the entire polydisperse sample. This is why it is also called the **Sauter Mean Diameter**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation of Mass Mean Diameter (D̄w)

The **mass mean diameter** is the **mass-fraction-weighted arithmetic mean** of all sieve-fraction diameters:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{D}_w &= \frac{\sum_i (m_i \cdot D_{pi})}{\sum_i m_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since $\sum_i m_i = M$ (total mass), and $\Delta \phi_i = m_i / M$:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{D}_w &= \frac{\sum_i (m_i \cdot D_{pi})}{M} = \sum_i \Delta \phi_i \cdot D_{pi}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conversion Logic**: Each fraction's diameter is weighted by **its share of the total mass**. Coarse particles (high mᵢ) pull the mean upward; fine particles have a small effect on D̄w even if numerous.

---

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard)

**Problem:** A 200 g sample of sand is sieved through a stack. The retained masses are:

| Sieve No. | Aperture (µm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| 1 | 1180 | 12 |
| 2 | 600 | 28 |
| 3 | 425 | 36 |
| 4 | 300 | 48 |
| 5 | 212 | 40 |
| 6 | 150 | 24 |
| 7 | 75 | 10 |
| Pan | < 75 | 2 |

**Compute: (a) Specific surface area (Sw), given ρp = 2650 kg/m³ (b) Volume-surface mean diameter (Dvs) (c) Mass mean diameter (D̄w).**

---

**Step 1: Compute geometric mean diameter (Dpᵢ) for each fraction.**

Sieve 1: Dp₁ = √(1180 × 600) = √(708000) ≈ 841.4 µm
Sieve 2: Dp₂ = √(600 × 425) = √(255000) ≈ 505.0 µm
Sieve 3: Dp₃ = √(425 × 300) = √(127500) ≈ 357.1 µm
Sieve 4: Dp₄ = √(300 × 212) = √(63600) ≈ 252.2 µm
Sieve 5: Dp₅ = √(212 × 150) = √(31800) ≈ 178.3 µm
Sieve 6: Dp₆ = √(150 × 75) = √(11250) ≈ 106.1 µm
Pan:    Dp₇ = (75/2) = 37.5 µm (assume mid-pan aperture)

---

**Step 2: Compute Δφᵢ = mᵢ/M = mᵢ/200.**

| Sieve | Dpᵢ (µm) | mᵢ (g) | Δφᵢ | Δφᵢ / Dpᵢ (µm⁻¹) | Δφᵢ × Dpᵢ (µm) |
|-------|----------|--------|-----|------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | 841.4 | 12 | 0.060 | 7.131 × 10⁻⁵ | 50.48 |
| 2 | 505.0 | 28 | 0.140 | 2.772 × 10⁻⁴ | 70.70 |
| 3 | 357.1 | 36 | 0.180 | 5.040 × 10⁻⁴ | 64.28 |
| 4 | 252.2 | 48 | 0.240 | 9.516 × 10⁻⁴ | 60.52 |
| 5 | 178.3 | 40 | 0.200 | 1.122 × 10⁻³ | 35.66 |
| 6 | 106.1 | 24 | 0.120 | 1.131 × 10⁻³ | 12.73 |
| 7 | 37.5 | 2 | 0.010 | 2.667 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.375 |
| **Σ** | — | 200 | 1.000 | **Σ ≈ 3.732 × 10⁻³** | **Σ ≈ 294.7** |

---

**Step 3: Compute Dvs (volume-surface mean diameter).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{vs} &= \frac{1}{\sum_i \Delta \phi_i / D_{pi}} \\
&= \frac{1}{3.732 \times 10^{-3}} \text{ µm}^{-1} \\
&= 267.95 \text{ µm} \\
&\approx 268 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 4: Compute D̄w (mass mean diameter).**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{D}_w &= \sum_i \Delta \phi_i \cdot D_{pi} \\
&= 294.7 \text{ µm} \\
&\approx 295 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

**Step 5: Compute Specific Surface Area Sw (mass basis).**

Convert units carefully: Dpᵢ in metres → Dpᵢ (m) = Dpᵢ (µm) × 10⁻⁶.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}} &= 3.732 \times 10^{-3} \text{ µm}^{-1} \\
&= 3.732 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{6} \text{ m}^{-1} \\
&= 3732 \text{ m}^{-1}
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w &= \frac{6}{\rho_p} \sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}} \\
&= \frac{6}{2650 \text{ kg/m}^3} \times 3732 \text{ m}^{-1} \\
&= 8.45 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Sanity Check**: This value (≈ 8.45 m²/kg) is typical for natural sand, confirming the calculation.

---

### 3.5 Cumulative Analysis — Numerical Sketch

Using the same data, compute **Cumulative Mass Passing (%)**:

| Sieve | Dp (µm) | Δφᵢ | Cum. Retained | Cum. Passing (%) |
|-------|---------|-----|---------------|------------------|
| 1 | 1180 | 0.060 | 0.060 | 94.0 |
| 2 | 600 | 0.140 | 0.200 | 80.0 |
| 3 | 425 | 0.180 | 0.380 | 62.0 |
| 4 | 300 | 0.240 | 0.620 | 38.0 |
| 5 | 212 | 0.200 | 0.820 | 18.0 |
| 6 | 150 | 0.120 | 0.940 | 6.0 |
| 7 | 75 | 0.010 | 0.950 | 5.0 |
| Pan | < 75 | 0.010 | 1.000 | 0.0 |

Plotting **Cum. Passing (%)** vs **Dpᵢ** on semi-log graph paper yields the **S-shaped cumulative PSD curve**, from which **d₁₀, d₅₀, d₉₀** can be read off (size at 10%, 50%, 90% passing respectively).

> [!NOTE]
> **d₅₀** (median diameter) and the spread (d₉₀ − d₁₀) are widely reported industrial specifications for any particulate product.

---

### 3.6 Mass Mean Diameter from Cumulative Analysis

Using cumulative fractions φᵢ (cumulative passing) and their associated diameters Dpᵢ:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\bar{D}_w &= \frac{\sum_i (m_i \cdot D_{pi})}{\sum_i m_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

But in cumulative form, mass retained between successive sieves is computed as the **difference between successive cumulative fractions** (multiplied by M). This is **mathematically identical** to the differential form shown earlier.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Sieve Stack Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Representative Sample m grams] --> B[Sieve Column Top Largest Aperture]
    B --> C[Sieve 2 Next Smaller Aperture]
    C --> D[Sieve 3 Smaller Aperture]
    D --> E[Sieve 4 Smaller Aperture]
    E --> F[Sieve 5 Smaller Aperture]
    F --> G[Sieve 6 Smallest Aperture]
    G --> H[Receiver Pan for Fines]
    H --> I[Mechanical Sieve Shaker]
    I --> J[Weigh Each Sieve plus Retained Mass]
    J --> K[Tabulate Retained Mass versus Aperture]
    K --> L[Compute Mass Fractions delta phi i]
    L --> M[Compute Dpi geometric mean]
    L --> N[Compute Specific Surface Area Sw]
    L --> O[Compute Sauter Mean Dvs]
    L --> P[Compute Mass Mean Diameter Dw]
    L --> Q[Plot Cumulative Passing Curve on semi log paper]
```

### 4.2 PSD Computation Workflow Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    step1[Raw Data: m sub i and Aperture D sub i] --> step2[Compute Dp sub i = sqrt D sub i times D sub i plus 1]
    step2 --> step3[Compute Mass Fraction delta phi sub i = m sub i over M]
    step3 --> step4A[Differential Analysis Branch]
    step3 --> step4B[Cumulative Analysis Branch]
    step4A --> step5A[Tabulate delta phi sub i over Dp sub i]
    step4A --> step6A[Tabulate delta phi sub i times Dp sub i]
    step5A --> step7A[Dvs = 1 over sum delta phi sub i over Dp sub i]
    step6A --> step8A[Mass Mean Dw = sum delta phi sub i times Dp sub i]
    step4B --> step5B[Compute Cum Retained and Cum Passing]
    step5B --> step6B[Plot Cum Passing versus Dp sub i]
    step6B --> step7B[Read d10 d50 d90 from graph]
    step5A --> step9A[Sw = 6 over rho sub p times sum delta phi sub i over Dp sub i]
```

### 4.3 Schematic of Cumulative vs Differential Distribution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    X[Particle Size Distribution Concept] --> Y1[Differential Curve nD versus D]
    X --> Y2[Cumulative Curve passing percent versus D]
    Y1 --> Z1[Bar or Histogram: height equals delta phi sub i]
    Y2 --> Z2[S shaped curve rising from 0 to 100 percent]
    Y1 --> W[Both curves are mathematically related: passing equals integral of differential]
    Y2 --> W
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Interpretation**: The steeper the cumulative curve, the **narrower** the distribution (i.e., more uniform particles). A gentle slope indicates a **wide** distribution (polydisperse sample). This visual cue is widely used in QC laboratories.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define **specific surface area** of a particulate solid and explain its significance in chemical engineering operations.

**Model Answer:**
Specific surface area **Sw** is defined as the total exposed surface area of particles per unit mass of the solid, expressed in m²/kg. For a polydisperse sample analysed by sieving, it is computed as:

$$ S_w = \frac{6}{\rho_p} \sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}} $$

**Significance**: It directly governs the **rate of heterogeneous reactions**, **dissolution kinetics**, **adsorption capacity**, **filtration resistance**, and **heat/mass transfer coefficients** between particles and the surrounding fluid. A high Sw (fine particles) implies greater reactivity but also higher pressure drop in packed beds and fluidization difficulties.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** What is the **volume-surface mean diameter (Sauter mean)**? How does it differ from the **mass mean diameter**?

**Model Answer:**
The **Sauter mean diameter (Dvs)** is defined as the diameter of a hypothetical uniform sphere having the **same surface-to-volume ratio** as the entire polydisperse sample:

$$ D_{vs} = \frac{1}{\sum_i \Delta \phi_i / D_{pi}} $$

The **mass mean diameter (D̄w)** is the mass-fraction-weighted arithmetic mean of all particle diameters:

$$ \bar{D}_w = \sum_i \Delta \phi_i \cdot D_{pi} $$

| Aspect | Dvs | D̄w |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Weighting | By surface (fines dominate) | By mass (coarse particles dominate) |
| Use | Mass/heat transfer, combustion | Bulk handling, cyclones |
| Value | Smaller than D̄w for wide PSD | Larger than Dvs for wide PSD |

---

### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – May 2023]**

A 500 g sample of crushed ore is subjected to sieve analysis. The data recorded is as follows:

| Sieve (mesh) | Aperture (µm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|--------------|---------------|-------------------|
| 4 | 4760 | 25 |
| 8 | 2360 | 75 |
| 14 | 1180 | 100 |
| 28 | 600 | 110 |
| 48 | 300 | 90 |
| 100 | 150 | 60 |
| 200 | 75 | 30 |
| Pan | < 75 | 10 |

Particle density ρp = 2700 kg/m³.

**(a)** Determine the **specific surface area** of the sample in m²/kg.
**(b)** Calculate the **volume-surface mean diameter (Dvs)** and the **mass mean diameter (D̄w)** using differential analysis.

**(c)** Plot the **cumulative percentage passing** curve and report the values of d₁₀, d₅₀, d₉₀.

---

##### **Model Solution (a) — Specific Surface Area [7 marks]**

**Step 1**: Compute geometric mean diameter Dpᵢ for each sieve.

Dpi values (µm): 3365, 1671, 836, 424, 212, 106, 37.5 (using √(Dᵢ × Dᵢ₊₁); pan = 75/2 = 37.5 µm).

Detailed:
- Dp₁ = √(4760 × 2360) = √11,233,600 ≈ 3351.7 µm
- Dp₂ = √(2360 × 1180) = √2,784,800 ≈ 1668.8 µm
- Dp₃ = √(1180 × 600) = √708,000 ≈ 841.4 µm
- Dp₄ = √(600 × 300) = √180,000 ≈ 424.3 µm
- Dp₅ = √(300 × 150) = √45,000 ≈ 212.1 µm
- Dp₆ = √(150 × 75) = √11,250 ≈ 106.1 µm
- Dp₇ = 75/2 = 37.5 µm

**Step 2**: Compute Δφᵢ = mᵢ/M and the terms Δφᵢ/Dpᵢ.

| Sieve | Dpᵢ (µm) | mᵢ (g) | Δφᵢ | Δφᵢ/Dpᵢ (µm⁻¹) |
|-------|----------|--------|-----|-----------------|
| 4 | 3351.7 | 25 | 0.050 | 1.4917 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 8 | 1668.8 | 75 | 0.150 | 8.988 × 10⁻⁵ |
| 14 | 841.4 | 100 | 0.200 | 2.377 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 28 | 424.3 | 110 | 0.220 | 5.184 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 48 | 212.1 | 90 | 0.180 | 8.486 × 10⁻⁴ |
| 100 | 106.1 | 60 | 0.120 | 1.131 × 10⁻³ |
| 200 | 37.5 | 30 | 0.060 | 1.600 × 10⁻³ |
| Pan | 37.5 | 10 | 0.020 | 5.333 × 10⁻⁴ |
| **Σ** | — | 500 | 1.000 | **Σ = 4.787 × 10⁻³ µm⁻¹** |

**Step 3**: Convert sum to m⁻¹.

$$ \sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}} = 4.787 \times 10^{-3} \text{ µm}^{-1} = 4.787 \times 10^{-3} \times 10^{6} = 4787 \text{ m}^{-1} $$

**Step 4**: Compute Sw.

$$
\begin{aligned}
S_w &= \frac{6}{\rho_p} \sum_i \frac{\Delta \phi_i}{D_{pi}} \\
&= \frac{6}{2700} \times 4787 \\
&= \frac{28722}{2700} \\
&= 10.64 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Specific Surface Area = 10.64 m²/kg**

---

##### **Model Solution (b) — Dvs and D̄w [4 marks]**

$$
\begin{aligned}
D_{vs} &= \frac{1}{\sum_i \Delta \phi_i / D_{pi}} = \frac{1}{4.787 \times 10^{-3} \text{ µm}^{-1}} \\
&= 208.9 \text{ µm}
\end{aligned}
$$

**∴ Dvs ≈ 209 µm**

For D̄w, compute Δφᵢ × Dpᵢ:

| Sieve | Δφᵢ | Dpᵢ (µm) | Δφᵢ × Dpᵢ (µm) |
|-------|-----|----------|-----------------|
| 4 | 0.050 | 3351.7 | 167.6 |
| 8 | 0.150 | 1668.8 | 250.3 |
| 14 | 0.200 | 841.4 | 168.3 |
| 28 | 0.220 | 424.3 | 93.3 |
| 48 | 0.180 | 212.1 | 38.2 |
| 100 | 0.120 | 106.1 | 12.7 |
| 200 | 0.060 | 37.5 | 2.25 |
| Pan | 0.020 | 37.5 | 0.75 |
| **Σ** | — | — | **733.5 µm** |

$$
\bar{D}_w = 733.5 \text{ µm} \approx 734 \text{ µm}
$$

**∴ Mass Mean Diameter ≈ 734 µm**

---

##### **Model Solution (c) — Cumulative Curve [3 marks]**

| Sieve | Dpᵢ (µm) | Cum. Passing (%) |
|-------|----------|------------------|
| 4 | 3351.7 | 95.0 |
| 8 | 1668.8 | 80.0 |
| 14 | 841.4 | 60.0 |
| 28 | 424.3 | 38.0 |
| 48 | 212.1 | 20.0 |
| 100 | 106.1 | 8.0 |
| 200 | 37.5 | 2.0 |
| Pan | < 37.5 | 0.0 |

Plotting on **semi-log graph** (x-axis = log Dpᵢ, y-axis = Cum. Passing %):

- **d₁₀** ≈ 180 µm
- **d₅₀** ≈ 410 µm
- **d₉₀** ≈ 1700 µm

(Values read off the graph; small variations acceptable.)

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**

**(a)** Describe the **principle, procedure, and applications** of sieve analysis. Explain why the **geometric mean** (and not arithmetic mean) is used to represent the diameter of a sieve fraction.

**(b)** The following data was obtained for a 250 g powder sample:

| Sieve | Aperture (µm) | Mass Retained (g) |
|-------|---------------|-------------------|
| A | 500 | 20 |
| B | 355 | 35 |
| C | 250 | 50 |
| D | 180 | 60 |
| E | 125 | 45 |
| F | 90 | 25 |
| G | 63 | 10 |
| Pan | — | 5 |

Given particle density ρp = 2400 kg/m³, compute by **differential analysis**: (i) **Dvs**, (ii) **D̄w**, and (iii) **Sw**.

---

##### **Model Solution (a) — Principle, Procedure, Geometric Mean [7 marks]**

**Principle**: A powder is classified by passing it through a stack of sieves of progressively smaller aperture. The mass retained on each sieve gives the size distribution of the bulk solid.

**Procedure** (6 steps):
1. Dry the sample at 105 °C for 1 h.
2. Weigh accurately (e.g., 250 g).
3. Arrange sieves in descending order of aperture (largest at top).
4. Place the sample on the top sieve, fit the lid and receiver pan.
5. Place stack on a mechanical sieve shaker for ~15 minutes.
6. Weigh each sieve + retained material, tabulate, compute fractions.

**Why Geometric Mean**:
Sieve apertures form a **geometric progression** (ratio = √2 or 2^(1/4) per ISO 565). For data on a logarithmic scale, the **geometric mean** correctly represents the central tendency:

$$ D_{pi} = \sqrt{D_i \cdot D_{i+1}} $$

The arithmetic mean would bias the value upward because the upper aperture Dᵢ is always larger than the lower aperture Dᵢ₊₁ by a multiplicative factor.

**Applications**: Cement industry, pharmaceuticals, mineral processing, ceramics, fertilizers, catalysts, food powders.

---

##### **Model Solution (b) — Numerical Computation [7 marks]**

**Step 1**: Dpᵢ = √(Dᵢ × Dᵢ₊₁):

- Dp_A = √(500 × 355) = √177500 ≈ 421.3 µm
- Dp_B = √(355 × 250) = √88750 ≈ 297.9 µm
- Dp_C = √(250 × 180) = √45000 ≈ 212.1 µm
- Dp_D = √(180 × 125) = √22500 = 150.0 µm
- Dp_E = √(125 × 90) = √11250 ≈ 106.1 µm
- Dp_F = √(90 × 63) = √5670 ≈ 75.3 µm
- Dp_G = √(63 × pan) ≈ √(63 × 31.5) = √1984.5 ≈ 44.6 µm (assuming pan mid = 31.5 µm)
- Dp_Pan = 31.5 µm

**Step 2**: Δφᵢ = mᵢ/250 and tabulate.

| Sieve | Dpᵢ (µm) | mᵢ (g) | Δφᵢ | Δφᵢ/Dpᵢ (µm⁻¹) | Δφᵢ·Dpᵢ (µm) |
|-------|----------|--------|-----|------------------|---------------|
| A | 421.3 | 20 | 0.080 | 1.899 × 10⁻⁴ | 33.7 |
| B | 297.9 | 35 | 0.140 | 4.700 × 10⁻⁴ | 41.7 |
| C | 212.1 | 50 | 0.200 | 9.429 × 10⁻⁴ | 42.4 |
| D | 150.0 | 60 | 0.240 | 1.600 × 10⁻³ | 36.0 |
| E | 106.1 | 45 | 0.180 | 1.696 × 10⁻³ | 19.1 |
| F | 75.3 | 25 | 0.100 | 1.328 × 10⁻³ | 7.5 |
| G | 44.6 | 10 | 0.040 | 8.969 × 10⁻⁴ | 1.78 |
| Pan | 31.5 | 5 | 0.020 | 6.349 × 10⁻⁴ | 0.63 |
| **Σ** | — | 250 | 1.000 | **Σ = 7.616 × 10⁻³ µm⁻¹** | **Σ = 182.8 µm** |

**(i) Dvs:**

$$
D_{vs} = \frac{1}{7.616 \times 10^{-3}} = 131.3 \text{ µm}
$$

**(ii) D̄w:**

$$
\bar{D}_w = 182.8 \text{ µm}
$$

**(iii) Sw:**

Convert sum: 7.616 × 10⁻³ µm⁻¹ = 7616 m⁻¹.

$$
S_w = \frac{6}{2400} \times 7616 = 19.04 \text{ m}^2/\text{kg}
$$

**Final Answers**: Dvs ≈ 131 µm, D̄w ≈ 183 µm, Sw ≈ 19.0 m²/kg.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Unit Inconsistency** — The most common mark-losing error. If Dpᵢ is in **µm**, the denominator must also be in µm to get Dvs in µm. For Sw in m²/kg, Dpᵢ **must be converted to metres**.
> 2. **Forgetting ρp** — Specific surface area Sw requires the particle density. Students often forget this and report Sw in m⁻¹ instead of m²/kg, losing 2 marks.
> 3. **Arithmetic mean instead of geometric mean** — Using (Dᵢ + Dᵢ₊₁)/2 is wrong; always use √(Dᵢ · Dᵢ₊₁).
> 4. **Not summing to unity** — Δφᵢ must sum to 1.0 (or mᵢ must sum to M). If not, an arithmetic error has occurred.
> 5. **Conflating Dvs with D̄w** — These are NOT the same. Always check the question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — SIEVE ANALYSIS**

- ✅ **Sieve analysis** classifies particles between **45 µm and 100 mm** using a stack of standard sieves (Tyler / ASTM E11 / ISO 3310).
- ✅ Each sieve fraction is represented by its **geometric mean diameter**: $D_{pi} = \sqrt{D_i \cdot D_{i+1}}$.
- ✅ **Mass fraction** $\Delta \phi_i = m_i / M$ must sum to **1.0**.
- ✅ **Specific Surface Area (mass basis)**: $S_w = (6 / \rho_p) \sum_i \Delta \phi_i / D_{pi}$ — **units: m²/kg**.
- ✅ **Specific Surface Area (volume basis)**: $S_v = 6 \sum_i \Delta \phi_i / D_{pi}$ — **units: m⁻¹**.
- ✅ **Volume-Surface (Sauter) Mean Diameter**: $D_{vs} = 1 / \sum_i \Delta \phi_i / D_{pi} = 6 / (S_v) = 6 / (S_w \cdot \rho_p)$ — **dominated by fines**.
- ✅ **Mass Mean Diameter**: $\bar{D}_w = \sum_i \Delta \phi_i \cdot D_{pi} = \sum_i m_i D_{pi} / M$ — **dominated by coarse particles**.
- ✅ **Cumulative analysis**: plot **% passing** vs **log Dpᵢ** on semi-log paper; read off **d₁₀, d₅₀, d₉₀**.
- ✅ **Differential analysis**: bar chart of **Δφᵢ vs Dpᵢ** — preserves the actual mass distribution shape.
- ✅ **Assumption**: Particles are **spherical** (so that $V_p = \pi D^3 / 6$ and $A_p = \pi D^2$). For non-spherical particles, results must be corrected using a **shape factor**.
- ✅ **Sources of error**: sieve **blinding**, **incomplete shaking**, **sample splitting bias**, **moisture agglomeration**, **electrostatic adhesion of fines**.
- ✅ **KTU-favourite comparison**: Always know that **Dvs < D̄w** for any polydisperse sample (proved by Jensen's inequality).
- ✅ **Always** check unit consistency: convert µm → m before plugging into Sw or Sv formulas.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of the laws of crushing in a ball mill and calculation of critical speed
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of the laws of crushing in a ball mill and calculation of critical speed

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module Experiment 2: Verification of the Laws of Crushing in a Ball Mill and Calculation of Critical Speed

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Ball Mill:** A cylindrical rotating vessel, operating horizontally on its own axis, partially filled with grinding media (balls made of steel, ceramic, or flint) of uniform size, used for fine grinding of solids in a wet or dry state. The mill rotates at a controlled speed such that the balls tumble, cascade, and impinge upon the feed material, producing comminution.

**Comminution** is the generic unit operation of mechanically reducing the size of solid particles. In the **ball mill experiment**, comminution is achieved by a combination of:
- **Impact** (high-energy collision of cascading balls)
- **Attrition** (rubbing/shearing action between balls and particles)
- **Compression** (squeezing of particles between ball-ball and ball-liner contacts)

### 1.2 Laws of Crushing — The Three Classical Laws

| Law | Proponent (Year) | Fundamental Assumption |
|---|---|---|
| **Kick's Law** | Friedrich Kick (1885) | Energy required is proportional to the **logarithm** of the reduction ratio |
| **Rittinger's Law** | Peter Rittinger (1867) | Energy required is proportional to the **new surface area** created |
| **Bond's Law** | Fred Bond (1952) | Energy required is proportional to the **square root** of the work required to produce infinitely fine particles from a feed of infinite size |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Examination Pointer:** The three laws are essentially special cases of a generalized differential equation:
> $$dE = -C \cdot \frac{dD}{D^n}$$
> where n = 1 (Kick), 1.5 (Bond), 2 (Rittinger).

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Kitchen Mortar" Model:**
> Imagine grinding spices in a heavy stone mortar. If you use a small number of heavy strikes (coarse crushing), most of your effort goes into *splitting large pieces roughly* — this is essentially what **Kick's law** describes (energy ∝ volume reduction).
> If, however, you use hundreds of light, gentle rubs (fine grinding), most of your effort creates *new exposed surface* (the aroma escapes because of new cell walls being broken) — this is exactly **Rittinger's law** (energy ∝ new surface area).
> **Bond's law** is the "practical middle ground" — the actual energy consumption of industrial mills lies between the two extremes.

### 1.4 Critical Speed — Intuitive Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Speed (Nc):** The rotational speed of the mill at which the centrifugal force on the outermost ball just equals the gravitational force, causing the ball to be pinned to the mill wall and stop cascading. **Above Nc, no grinding occurs.**

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy:** Spin a bucket of water in a vertical circle. At a "critical speed," the water does not fall out — it is pinned by centrifugal force. Similarly, above the mill's critical speed, the balls are "pinned" to the wall and cease to grind.

### 1.5 Standard Constants & Metrics Used in the Lab

- **Acceleration due to gravity:** $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$ (Bold metric — standard for Kerala lab measurements)
- **Mill radius R and ball radius r** in metres
- **Critical speed Nc** typically reported in **RPM** (revolutions per minute)
- **Standard ball charge** = 30–50% of mill volume
- **Standard work index (Wi)** values:
    * Limestone: 11.5 kWh/ton
    * Coal: 13.0 kWh/ton
    * Iron ore: 15.0 kWh/ton
    * Quartz: 14.0 kWh/ton
- **Standard mesh sizes (Tyler/BS):** +4, -4 +8, -8 +16, -16 +30, -30 +50, -50 +100, -100 +200, -200 (in microns)

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principles of a Ball Mill

When the mill rotates, the balls are carried up the rising side of the mill and then fall down (or are projected across) onto the lower portion of the charge. The mode of ball motion depends strictly on the **rotational speed (N)** relative to the **critical speed (Nc)**.

| Operating Regime | Speed (% of Nc) | Ball Motion | Grinding Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Cataracting** | 50 – 80% | Balls rise up the wall and break free, falling freely onto the charge | Predominantly **impact** |
| **Cascading** | 30 – 50% | Balls tumble down the rising slope of the charge | Predominantly **attrition/shear** |
| **Centrifuging** | > 100% (above Nc) | Balls pinned to wall, no relative motion | **No grinding** occurs |

> [!NOTE]
> **Optimal operating speed in practice:** 65–80% of critical speed for dry grinding; 50–80% for wet grinding. Below 50%, cascading is dominant; above 80%, centrifuging begins.

## 2.2 The Three Laws of Crushing — Detailed Derivation Logic

### 2.2.1 Kick's Law (1885) — Volume Proportionality

**Logic step-by-step:**
1. Kick assumed that the energy required to effect a small reduction in size is proportional to the **volume of the particle** (since volume scales with D³, and crack propagation energy scales similarly).
2. The differential form: energy required $\propto$ reduction in characteristic size.
3. Integration between feed size $D_1$ and product size $D_2$ yields the **logarithmic form**.
4. Result: $E = K_K \cdot \ln\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$, where $K_K$ = Kick's constant.

**Physical meaning:** Best applicable for **coarse crushing** (size range > 1 cm).

### 2.2.2 Rittinger's Law (1867) — Surface Area Proportionality

**Logic step-by-step:**
1. Rittinger assumed that the energy required is proportional to the **newly created surface area**, since a fresh surface must be formed across a freshly broken plane.
2. Surface area of a sphere $\propto D^2$, so the differential work $dE \propto d(\text{area}) \propto d(D^2)$.
3. Integrating for a mass of particles: $E = K_R \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$, where $K_R$ = Rittinger's constant (often called **Rittinger's number**).
4. Note: $D$ must be the **surface-mean (or equivalent) diameter**, not the sieve diameter.

**Physical meaning:** Best applicable for **fine grinding** (size range < 0.1 mm).

### 2.2.3 Bond's Law (1952) — Work Index Approach (The Engineering Compromise)

**Logic step-by-step:**
1. Bond recognized that neither Kick's nor Rittinger's law held across the full size range.
2. He proposed an intermediate exponent and defined the **Work Index (Wi)** — the energy (kWh) required to reduce **one short ton (2000 lb = 907.2 kg)** of material from **infinite theoretical size** to a state where 80% passes through a 100 µm screen (P80 = 100 µm).
3. The differential form: $dE = K_B \cdot \dfrac{dD}{D^{1.5}}$
4. Integration gives: $E = W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_2}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{D_1}}\right)$, where sizes are in microns and energy in kWh/ton.
5. Most commonly written using P80 and F80 (the 80% passing sizes): $W = W_i \cdot \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right) \cdot 10$$

**Physical meaning:** The **most widely used law in industry**, applicable across a broad range of sizes (coarse-to-fine).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Variables & Units | Applicable Regime |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $N_c = \dfrac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\dfrac{g}{R-r}}$ | $N_c$ in rev/s, $g = 9.81 \; m/s^2$, $R$ = mill radius (m), $r$ = ball radius (m) | General (theoretical) |
| 2 | $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D - d}}$ | $N_c$ in RPM, $D$ = mill **diameter** (m), $d$ = ball diameter (m) | **Industry shortcut** (most-used form) |
| 3 | $E = K_K \ln\left(\dfrac{D_1}{D_2}\right)$ | $K_K$ = Kick's constant (kWh/ton) | Coarse crushing |
| 4 | $E = K_R \left(\dfrac{1}{D_2} - \dfrac{1}{D_1}\right)$ | $K_R$ = Rittinger's constant, $D$ in **metres** | Fine grinding |
| 5 | $W = 10 \, W_i \left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right)$ | $W$ = work (kWh/ton), sizes in **microns** | **Bond's law (most-used industrial form)** |
| 6 | $W_i = \dfrac{E}{\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \dfrac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}}$ | Defines **experimental Wi** | Verification experiment |
| 7 | Operating speed = $0.65 \cdot N_c$ to $0.80 \cdot N_c$ | Empirical optimum | Lab practice |
| 8 | Power consumption $P = m \cdot g \cdot h \cdot N$ (approx) | Simplified power estimate | Lab estimate |

> [!WARNING]
> **Unit Mismatch Trap (Very Common in KTU Valuations):**
> - In **Rittinger's law**, the diameters $D_1, D_2$ are in **metres** (or any consistent length unit, but MUST be the same).
> - In **Bond's law formula #5**, the diameters are in **MICRONS** and the factor of 10 converts to kWh/ton.
> - Mixing units between Rittinger and Bond in a single problem will lead to **zero marks**.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Application Domain | Why This Lab Matters |
|---|---|
| **Cement Industry** | Raw material (limestone) and clinker are ground in ball mills; design power is computed via Bond's law using published Wi. |
| **Mineral Beneficiation** | Liberation of valuable mineral from ore requires controlled grinding — Wi dictates mill selection. |
| **Pharmaceutical Manufacturing** | Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients (APIs) are micronised; critical speed determines whether jet mill or ball mill is suitable. |
| **Coal-Fired Power Plants** | Pulverised coal (PFC) for boiler burners is ball-milled to ~75 µm; grinding law dictates bunker design. |
| **Ceramics & Pigments** | Fine grinding of kaolin, TiO₂ — Rittinger's law dominates since new surface area determines reactivity and tinting strength. |

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Critical Speed of a Ball Mill

### 3.1.1 Free-Body Analysis of an Outer Ball at the Top of the Mill

Consider the outermost ball (radius $r$) of mass $m$ stuck to the inside wall of the mill of radius $R$. The centre of the ball traces a circular path of radius $(R - r)$. At the **critical point**, the ball is at the topmost position and is on the verge of separation from the wall.

**Forces acting on the ball at the topmost position:**
1. Centrifugal force (outward, radially): $F_c = m \, \omega^2 \, (R - r)$
2. Weight (downward, inward radial): $W_g = m \, g$

For the ball to be **just held by the wall without falling**, the centrifugal force must equal the weight at the very point of departure from the wall (i.e., when the centripetal requirement is exactly balanced by gravity).

**Condition at the critical speed:**

$$m \, \omega_c^2 \, (R - r) = m \, g$$

Cancelling $m$ (which is non-zero):

$$\omega_c^2 = \frac{g}{(R - r)}$$

Taking the positive square root (since angular velocity is positive):

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{R - r}}$$

### 3.1.2 Conversion from rad/s to rev/s to RPM

Since $\omega_c$ is in rad/s, the **frequency in revolutions per second** is:

$$N_c = \frac{\omega_c}{2\pi} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{R - r}}$$

Converting to **revolutions per minute (RPM)** — the form used in the laboratory:

$$N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{R - r}} \times 60 = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{R - r}}$$

Substituting $g = 9.81 \, m/s^2$ and $\pi = 3.1416$:

$$\frac{60}{2\pi} = \frac{60}{6.2832} = 9.549$$

Therefore:

$$N_c = 9.549 \sqrt{\frac{g}{R - r}}$$

If we further substitute $g = 9.81$ and take its square root: $\sqrt{9.81} = 3.132$:

$$N_c = 9.549 \times 3.132 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{R - r}} = 29.91 \times \frac{1}{\sqrt{R - r}}$$

This is often approximated as $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D - d}}$ where $D = 2R$ is the **mill diameter** and $d = 2r$ is the **ball diameter** (both in metres).

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Bond's Work Index Formula

### 3.2.1 Starting Point — The Differential Work

Bond's hypothesis: the differential work required to reduce a particle of size $D$ by an infinitesimal amount $dD$ is:

$$dE = -C \cdot \frac{dD}{D^{1.5}}$$

where $C$ is a constant depending on the material. The negative sign indicates energy is consumed as size decreases.

### 3.2.2 Integration from Feed to Product

Integrating from feed size $F$ (larger) to product size $P$ (smaller):

$$E = -C \int_F^P \frac{dD}{D^{1.5}} = -C \left[ \frac{D^{-0.5}}{-0.5} \right]_F^P$$

$$E = 2C \left[ D^{-0.5} \right]_F^P = 2C \left( P^{-0.5} - F^{-0.5} \right)$$

$$E = 2C \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \right)$$

### 3.2.3 Definition of Work Index

By Bond's definition, the **Work Index $W_i$** is the total work required to reduce **one ton (2000 lb)** of material from **infinite theoretical size** to a product such that 80% passes through 100 µm (P80 = 100 µm). When $F \to \infty$, the term $1/\sqrt{F} \to 0$, so:

$$W_i = 2C \cdot \frac{1}{\sqrt{100 \, \mu m}}$$

The constant $2C$ is therefore numerically equal to $W_i \cdot \sqrt{100}$ when sizes are in microns. Substituting back:

$$E = W_i \cdot \frac{10}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} \quad \text{(with sizes in microns)}$$

For finite feed sizes, the full equation becomes:

$$\boxed{E = 10 \, W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right)}$$

## 3.3 Exhaustive Numerical Worked Example (KTU Pattern)

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example — KTU Pattern (Dec 2023-style):**
> A ball mill of internal diameter $D = 0.6 \; m$ is charged with steel balls of diameter $d = 0.025 \; m$. The mill is operated at 60 RPM with a feed of 500 g of limestone. The energy supplied by the motor during one minute of operation is 0.025 kWh. The feed F80 = 2000 µm and the product P80 = 250 µm. Calculate:
> (a) Theoretical critical speed and operating speed as % of Nc.
> (b) The Work Index of the limestone and identify the most appropriate law.
> (c) Rittinger's constant from the same data.

### 3.3.1 Part (a): Critical Speed

Using the industry shortcut formula:

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D - d}} = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{0.6 - 0.025}} = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{0.575}} = \frac{42.3}{0.7583} = 55.78 \; RPM$$

Operating speed as a percentage of critical speed:

$$\% N_c = \frac{60}{55.78} \times 100 = 107.6\%$$

> [!WARNING]
> **At 107.6% of Nc, the mill is in the centrifuging regime** — balls are pinned to the wall, and grinding is inefficient. This is a common lab-observation question in KTU.

### 3.3.2 Part (b): Work Index

Energy supplied per ton of feed:

$$E = 0.025 \; kWh \quad \text{for} \quad 500 \; g \implies E = \frac{0.025 \, kWh}{0.5 \, kg} \times 1000 \; kg = 50 \; kWh/ton$$

Substituting into Bond's law:

$$50 = 10 \cdot W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{250}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2000}} \right)$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{250}} = \frac{1}{15.81} = 0.0632 \; \mu m^{-1/2}$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{2000}} = \frac{1}{44.72} = 0.02236 \; \mu m^{-1/2}$$

$$\left(\frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}}\right) = 0.0632 - 0.02236 = 0.0409 \; \mu m^{-1/2}$$

$$50 = 10 \cdot W_i \cdot 0.0409 = 0.409 \cdot W_i$$

$$W_i = \frac{50}{0.409} = 122.2 \; kWh/ton$$

Since the experimental $W_i$ is significantly higher than the literature value (11.5 kWh/ton for limestone) — it indicates the mill is operating above the critical speed (no grinding!) and the energy is being lost to friction.

### 3.3.3 Part (c): Rittinger's Constant

Using Rittinger's law with sizes in **metres**:

$$D_1 = \frac{2000 \times 10^{-6}}{1} = 0.002 \; m, \quad D_2 = 0.00025 \; m$$

$$E = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1} \right) = K_R \left( \frac{1}{0.00025} - \frac{1}{0.002} \right)$$

$$= K_R (4000 - 500) = K_R \cdot 3500$$

$$50 = K_R \cdot 3500 \implies K_R = 0.01429 \; kWh \cdot m/ton$$

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Ball Mill Components and Material Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Feed Hopper: Limestone or Coal Feed] --> B[Screw Feeder]
    B --> C[Ball Mill Shell: Horizontal Cylindrical Vessel]
    C --> D[Internal Liner: Rubber or Steel Liner]
    C --> E[Grinding Media: Steel Balls 25-50 mm]
    C --> F[Discharge End]
    F --> G[Product Classifier: Cyclone or Screen]
    G --> H[Oversize Recycle to Mill Inlet]
    G --> I[Final Product: Fine Powder]
    C --> J[Drive Motor: Variable Speed Coupling]
    J --> K[Gear Reducer]
    K --> L[Mill Shaft: Trunnion Bearings]
    L --> M[Tachometer: Monitors RPM]
    M --> N[Controller: Maintains percent of Nc]
```

## 4.2 Ball Motion Regimes within a Rotating Mill

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Regime1[Low Speed 30-50 percent Nc]
        A1[Ball Tumble Cascading] --> A2[Attrition Dominant: Shear Force]
        A2 --> A3[Used for Fine Wet Grinding]
    end
    subgraph Regime2[Optimum Speed 65-80 percent Nc]
        B1[Balls Rise Up the Wall] --> B2[Break Free at Apex]
        B2 --> B3[Impact the Lower Charge]
        B3 --> B4[Combined Impact plus Attrition: Maximum Grinding Efficiency]
    end
    subgraph Regime3[Super Critical above 100 percent Nc]
        C1[Centrifugal Force greater than Gravity] --> C2[Balls Pinned to Wall]
        C2 --> C3[NO Grinding Occurs]
    end
```

## 4.3 Experimental Procedure Block Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    S1[Step 1: Sieve Feed Material and Record Feed PSD] --> S2[Step 2: Weigh 500 g of Feed Sample]
    S2 --> S3[Step 3: Charge Mill with 40 percent Volume of Steel Balls]
    S3 --> S4[Step 4: Set Variable Speed Drive to Desired RPM]
    S4 --> S5[Step 5: Load Feed Material into Mill]
    S5 --> S6[Step 6: Operate Mill for Fixed Time t minutes]
    S6 --> S7[Step 7: Discharge Product and Sieve Product PSD]
    S7 --> S8[Step 8: Record Energy kWh from Watt-Hour Meter]
    S8 --> S9[Step 9: Repeat at Three Different Speeds e.g. 50 60 70 RPM]
    S9 --> S10[Step 10: Calculate F80 and P80 for Each Run]
    S10 --> S11[Step 11: Compute Wi and Compare with Literature]
    S11 --> S12[Step 12: Compute Nc Theory and Compare with Observations]
```

## 4.4 Forces Acting on a Ball at the Top of the Mill

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph FreeBody[Free Body Diagram of Outermost Ball at Top of Mill]
        F1[Centrifugal Force Fc: Radially Outward]
        F2[Weight Wg: Vertically Downward]
        F3[Normal Reaction from Wall: Zero at Critical Speed]
    end
    F1 --> Condition1
    F2 --> Condition1
    Condition1[Equilibrium at Nc: Fc equals Wg] --> Result
    Result[At N greater than Nc: Fc exceeds Wg, Ball Pinned to Wall]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A.1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define critical speed of a ball mill. Why should a ball mill never be operated above its critical speed?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The critical speed $N_c$ of a ball mill is the rotational speed at which the centrifugal force acting on the outermost ball equals the gravitational force acting on it. At this speed, the ball just clings to the mill wall without falling back, ceasing the cascading action. A ball mill must never be operated above its critical speed because at $N > N_c$, all the balls get pinned to the inner wall of the mill due to excessive centrifugal force, there is no relative motion between the balls, and therefore **no grinding action takes place**. Energy supplied by the motor is wasted as heat due to friction, leading to zero comminution efficiency and possible damage to the mill liners.

### Question A.2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"State Rittinger's law of crushing. Why is it most suitable for fine grinding operations?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Rittinger's law (1867) states that the energy consumed in crushing a given mass of solid material is directly proportional to the **newly created surface area**. Mathematically:
$$E = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1} \right)$$
where $K_R$ is Rittinger's constant and $D_1, D_2$ are the feed and product sizes respectively.
Rittinger's law is most suitable for **fine grinding** because in fine grinding, the surface area increases dramatically (proportional to $D^2$) as particle size decreases. A large amount of energy is consumed in creating the new surface — energy that is no longer proportional to the volume reduction (Kick's law) but to the surface area exposed. Hence, Rittinger's law accurately predicts energy consumption in ball mills and pulverised-fuel systems producing particles below 100 µm.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU Internal Choice Pattern

### Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024 – Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Derive the expression for the **critical speed of a ball mill** starting from the force balance on the outermost ball. Express the final formula in RPM form. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A ball mill of internal diameter **0.8 m** is charged with steel balls of diameter **0.04 m**. The mill is operated at **50 RPM** for 30 minutes. The energy consumed is **0.05 kWh** to grind **1 kg of limestone** with $F_{80} = 1500 \; \mu m$ to $P_{80} = 200 \; \mu m$. **(7 marks)**
- (i) Calculate the theoretical critical speed in RPM.
- (ii) State whether the mill is operating in cascading, cataracting, or centrifuging regime.
- (iii) Calculate the experimental Bond Work Index and compare with the literature value of 11.5 kWh/ton for limestone.

---

### SOLUTION — Question A

#### Part (a) [7 marks — full derivation]

**Step 1: Free-body setup** (2 marks)
Consider the outermost grinding ball of mass $m$ and radius $r$ rotating with the mill of radius $R$ at angular velocity $\omega$. The ball's centre traces a circle of radius $(R - r)$.

**Step 2: Identify forces at topmost position** (1 mark)
At the topmost point, the ball is on the verge of separation from the mill wall. Two principal forces act:
- Centrifugal force (radially outward): $F_c = m \, \omega_c^2 \, (R - r)$
- Weight (radially inward, downward): $W = m \, g$

**Step 3: Apply equilibrium condition** (1 mark)
At critical speed, the centrifugal force just equals the weight:

$$m \, \omega_c^2 \, (R - r) = m \, g \implies \omega_c^2 = \frac{g}{R - r}$$

**Step 4: Convert to rev/s and then RPM** (2 marks)

$$\omega_c = \sqrt{\frac{g}{R - r}} \; \text{rad/s} \implies N_c = \frac{1}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{R - r}} \; \text{rev/s}$$

$$N_c = \frac{60}{2\pi} \sqrt{\frac{g}{R - r}} = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{D - d}} \; \text{RPM}$$

where $D = 2R$ (mill diameter) and $d = 2r$ (ball diameter), both in metres.

**Step 5: Physical interpretation** (1 mark)
At this speed, balls are pinned to the wall — no grinding occurs. Operating speed is therefore kept at 65–80% of $N_c$.

#### Part (b) [7 marks — numerical solution]

**(i) Theoretical critical speed** (2 marks)

$$N_c = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{0.8 - 0.04}} = \frac{42.3}{\sqrt{0.76}} = \frac{42.3}{0.8718} = 48.52 \; RPM$$

**(ii) Operating regime** (2 marks)

$$\% N_c = \frac{50}{48.52} \times 100 = 103.05\%$$

Since this is **greater than 100%**, the mill is operating in the **centrifuging regime** — balls are pinned to the wall, and no grinding takes place. This is **operationally incorrect**.

**(iii) Experimental Bond Work Index** (3 marks)

Energy per ton:
$$E = 0.05 \; kWh / 1 \; kg = 50 \; kWh/ton$$

Bond's law:
$$E = 10 \, W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right)$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{200}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{1500}} = \frac{1}{14.142} - \frac{1}{38.730} = 0.07071 - 0.02582 = 0.04489$$

$$50 = 10 \cdot W_i \cdot 0.04489 = 0.4489 \cdot W_i$$

$$W_i = \frac{50}{0.4489} = 111.4 \; kWh/ton$$

The experimental $W_i$ (111.4) is far above the literature value of 11.5 kWh/ton, confirming the mill was operating incorrectly above the critical speed. The energy was being lost to friction without producing size reduction.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2 (Alternative Question)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 – Module Internal Choice Pattern]**

**(a)** Explain the **three laws of crushing** (Kick, Rittinger, Bond) with mathematical expressions. Discuss the range of particle sizes for which each law is most accurate. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** The following sieve analysis data were obtained in a ball mill grinding experiment on iron ore (feed = 2 kg, grinding time = 10 minutes, motor input = 0.075 kWh). **(7 marks)**

| Tyler Mesh | Size (µm) | Feed Weight (g) | Product Weight (g) |
|---|---|---|---|
| +4 | 4760 | 200 | 0 |
| −4 +8 | 2380 | 400 | 50 |
| −8 +16 | 1190 | 500 | 200 |
| −16 +30 | 595 | 500 | 400 |
| −30 +50 | 297 | 300 | 500 |
| −50 +100 | 149 | 100 | 400 |
| −100 +200 | 74 | 0 | 350 |
| −200 | <74 | 0 | 100 |

- (i) Determine $F_{80}$ and $P_{80}$ from the cumulative data.
- (ii) Calculate the Bond Work Index and identify if the ore is "hard" or "soft".
- (iii) Calculate the Rittinger's constant from the same data.

---

### SOLUTION — Question B

#### Part (a) [7 marks]

**Kick's Law (1885):** Energy required is proportional to the logarithm of the reduction ratio. Best for **coarse crushing** (>1 cm).
$$E = K_K \ln\left( \frac{D_1}{D_2} \right)$$

**Rittinger's Law (1867):** Energy required is proportional to the new surface area created. Best for **fine grinding** (<0.1 mm).
$$E = K_R \left( \frac{1}{D_2} - \frac{1}{D_1} \right)$$

**Bond's Law (1952):** Energy required is proportional to $D^{-0.5}$ — an intermediate empirical law. Best for **general industrial** use.
$$E = 10 \, W_i \left( \frac{1}{\sqrt{P_{80}}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{F_{80}}} \right)$$

(1 mark for each law's expression + 1 mark for range + 2 marks for comparative discussion)

#### Part (b) [7 marks]

**(i) Determine F80 and P80** (3 marks)

**Feed cumulative data** (cumulative % finer than each size):

| Size (µm) | Feed Wt (g) | Cum Retained (g) | Cum Retained (%) | Cum Finer (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 4760 | 200 | 200 | 10.0 | 90.0 |
| 2380 | 400 | 600 | 30.0 | 70.0 |
| 1190 | 500 | 1100 | 55.0 | 45.0 |
| 595 | 500 | 1600 | 80.0 | 20.0 |
| 297 | 300 | 1900 | 95.0 | 5.0 |
| 149 | 100 | 2000 | 100.0 | 0.0 |

**F80 (size at 80% passing in feed):** Looking at the table, 80% passing corresponds to a size between 595 µm (20% passing) and 1190 µm (45% passing). By interpolation on a log-log scale (or linear interpolation in the 20–45% range):

$$F_{80} = 595 + \frac{80 - 20}{45 - 20} \times (1190 - 595) = 595 + \frac{60}{25} \times 595 = 595 + 1428 = 2023 \; \mu m$$

A simpler engineering interpolation: $F_{80} \approx 2024 \; \mu m$.

**Product cumulative data:**

| Size (µm) | Product Wt (g) | Cum Retained (g) | Cum Retained (%) | Cum Finer (%) |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 2380 | 50 | 50 | 2.5 | 97.5 |
| 1190 | 200 | 250 | 12.5 | 87.5 |
| 595 | 400 | 650 | 32.5 | 67.5 |
| 297 | 500 | 1150 | 57.5 | 42.5 |
| 149 | 400 | 1550 | 77.5 | 22.5 |
| 74 | 350 | 1900 | 95.0 | 5.0 |
| <74 | 100 | 2000 | 100.0 | 0.0 |

**P80 (size at 80% passing in product):** 80% passing lies between 297 µm (42.5% passing) and 595 µm (67.5% passing). Interpolation:

Wait — the 80% passing is between 87.5% and 67.5%. So we need the inverse — the size at which 80% is **finer**, which lies between 297 µm (42.5% passing) and 595 µm (67.5% passing). To reach 80% passing we need to go beyond 595 µm.

Looking again: at 595 µm, passing is 67.5%. At 1190 µm, passing is 87.5%. So 80% lies between 595 and 1190 µm:

$$P_{80} = 595 + \frac{80 - 67.5}{87.5 - 67.5} \times (1190 - 595) = 595 + \frac{12.5}{20} \times 595 = 595 + 371.9 = 967 \; \mu m$$

**(ii) Bond Work Index** (2 marks)

Energy per ton:
$$E = \frac{0.075 \; kWh}{2 \; kg} \times 1000 = 37.5 \; kWh/ton$$

$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{967}} - \frac{1}{\sqrt{2024}} = \frac{1}{31.10} - \frac{1}{44.99} = 0.03216 - 0.02223 = 0.00993$$

$$37.5 = 10 \cdot W_i \cdot 0.00993 = 0.0993 \cdot W_i$$

$$W_i = \frac{37.5}{0.0993} = 377.6 \; kWh/ton$$

Since $W_i = 377.6$ kWh/ton is far above typical iron ore (15 kWh/ton), this ore is classified as **extremely hard** — or, more likely, the experiment has errors. In lab practice, this value is compared to literature to validate the experiment.

**(iii) Rittinger's constant** (2 marks)

Using sizes in metres: $D_1 = 2024 \times 10^{-6} = 0.002024 \; m$, $D_2 = 967 \times 10^{-6} = 0.000967 \; m$.

$$E = K_R \left( \frac{1}{0.000967} - \frac{1}{0.002024} \right) = K_R (1034.1 - 494.1) = K_R \cdot 540.0$$

$$K_R = \frac{37.5}{540.0} = 0.0694 \; kWh \cdot m/ton$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Common Errors in this Lab Exam (Loss of 1–3 Marks per Error):**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in Bond's law:** Forgetting to use **microns** (not mm or m) inside the square-root terms. If you substitute mm, you must divide $W_i$ by 1000. Mixing up causes massive numerical errors.
> 2. **F80 and P80 from cumulative data:** Using the arithmetic mean of top and bottom sieve sizes instead of **interpolating** at exactly 80% passing. Always read off the cumulative-undersize curve.
> 3. **Confusing $D$ and $d$ in critical speed formula:** $D$ is the mill **diameter**, $d$ is the ball **diameter**. Students frequently subtract in the wrong order or add them.
> 4. **Operating regime misidentification:** Speed above $N_c$ = centrifuging, not "ideal". Students often confuse "high RPM" with "more efficient grinding".
> 5. **Mixing Rittinger and Bond in one equation:** Either use one OR the other with consistent units. Never substitute $W_i$ into a Rittinger expression.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **High-Yield Rapid Revision — Master These Before the Exam:**

- [ ] **Critical speed formula (most important):** $N_c = \dfrac{42.3}{\sqrt{D - d}}$ RPM, where $D$ = mill diameter, $d$ = ball diameter (both in m).
- [ ] **Optimum operating speed:** 65–80% of $N_c$ for dry grinding; 50–80% for wet.
- [ ] **Above $N_c$:** Centrifuging — no grinding — energy wasted as heat.
- [ ] **Kick's law:** $E = K_K \ln(D_1/D_2)$ — best for **coarse** crushing.
- [ ] **Rittinger's law:** $E = K_R (1/D_2 - 1/D_1)$ — best for **fine** grinding; $D$ in metres.
- [ ] **Bond's law:** $E = 10 W_i (1/\sqrt{P_{80}} - 1/\sqrt{F_{80}})$ — general industrial; sizes in **microns**.
- [ ] **Work Index (Wi) definition:** Energy in kWh/ton to reduce material from infinite size to 80% passing 100 µm.
- [ ] **Literature Wi values to remember:** Limestone ≈ 11.5, Coal ≈ 13, Iron ore ≈ 15, Quartz ≈ 14, Cement clinker ≈ 13.0 (all kWh/ton).
- [ ] **F80 / P80:** The 80% passing sizes of the feed and product, obtained by interpolating the cumulative-undersize curve.
- [ ] **Energy calculation:** $E = (\text{kWh on meter} / \text{kg of feed}) \times 1000 \; (kWh/ton)$.
- [ ] **Three modes of ball motion:** Cascading (low speed), Cataracting (optimum), Centrifuging (above Nc).
- [ ] **Reduction ratio:** $RR = D_1/D_2 = F_{80}/P_{80}$.
- [ ] **Verification:** The law that gives a $W_i$ matching literature values is the verified law. Lab results often confirm **Bond's law** as the most reliable.
- [ ] **Grinding media:** Balls are typically steel, 25–50 mm diameter; ball charge = 30–50% of mill volume.
- [ ] **Total mill power** scales as: $P \propto D^{2.5}$ (classic Bond mill-power scaling).
- [ ] **Wet vs Dry grinding:** Wet grinding requires ~25% less energy than dry grinding (no dust, better lubrication, less noise, more efficient).

> [!NOTE]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "KRB = Kelvin, Room Temp, Boltz":**
> **K**ick (log), **R**ittinger (1/D), **B**ond (1/√D) — the exponents on the size term are 1, 1.5 (approx), and 2. Remember: coarser particles = lower exponent. Bond's compromise value of 1.5 is the workhorse for industry.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Study of the working of plate and frame filter press
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Study of the working of plate and frame filter press

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Plate and Frame Filter Press (PFFP)** is a *batch-type*, *intermittently operated*, *positive-pressure* filtration device used in chemical engineering for the separation of solids from liquids (slurries) on an industrial scale. It consists of a series of alternating **recessed plates** and **hollow frames** mounted on a horizontal frame, with **filter cloth (filter media)** clamped between each plate-frame pair. The slurry is pumped under high pressure (typically **2–15 bar**), the liquid (filtrate) passes through the cloth and exits via channels in the plates, while the solid particles accumulate as a **filter cake** within the frame chambers.

The equipment conforms to the **Indian Standard IS 9580** and is widely used for the dewatering of mineral concentrates, pigments, dyestuffs, pharmaceutical intermediates, sludge treatment, and effluent treatment in process industries.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The laboratory study focuses on the determination of **(i) filter medium resistance, Rm**, **(ii) specific cake resistance, α**, and **(iii) cake moisture content** from constant-pressure filtration data.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Think of a PFFP like a TOASTED SANDWICH MAKER (or a waffle iron):**
> - The **frames** are the "hollow space" inside the sandwich maker where you put the filling.
> - The **plates** are the hot metal surfaces that squeeze the sandwich.
> - The **filter cloth** is the "bread slices" that hold the solid filling (cheese/butter) but let the melted oil/butter drip out.
> - The **slurry** is the unfiltered filling (e.g., vegetable with water).
> - The **filtrate** is the clear liquid that drips out.
> - The **filter cake** is the solid residue that remains stuck to the cloth.

In simple words, when you squeeze a wet cloth, water comes out and the solid mass remains in the cloth — the PFFP performs the same job, but with **mechanical hydraulic pressure** and on a much larger scale.

## 1.3 Key Physical Metrics

| Metric | Typical Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Operating Pressure** | **2–15 bar (gauge)** | Driving force for filtration |
| **Filtration Area (A)** | **0.5 – 200 m²** | Total surface of filter cloth |
| **Filter Medium** | Cotton, Polypropylene, Nylon | Selects particle retention |
| **Cake Thickness** | **20 – 50 mm** | Determines cycle time |
| **Cake Moisture** | **15 – 80 %** | Depends on slurry & pressure |
| **Filtrate Viscosity (μ)** | **0.001 – 0.005 Pa·s** | For water-based slurries |

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Engineering Units used in KTU lab manual:** Filtrate volume **V** in **m³**, time **t** in **seconds**, area **A** in **m²**, pressure **ΔP** in **Pa (N/m²)**, specific cake resistance **α** in **m/kg**.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Constructional Details (Component Breakdown)

A typical PFFP consists of the following components, each with a specific engineering role:

1. **Filter Plates (Recessed Plates):** Grooved or ribbed surfaces on both sides. Channels guide filtrate to the outlet. Made of **polypropylene, cast iron, or stainless steel**.
2. **Filter Frames:** Hollow rectangular frames that hold the slurry and form the cake chamber. Available thicknesses: **25 mm, 40 mm, 50 mm**.
3. **Filter Cloth / Media:** Draped over each plate. Acts as the primary barrier for solids. Washable and replaceable.
4. **Inlet Slurry Port:** A central channel feeds slurry into each frame simultaneously.
5. **Filtrate Outlets:** Drained through cocks at the bottom of each plate.
6. **Hydraulic / Manual Closing Mechanism:** Keeps the plate pack compressed.
7. **End Plates (Fixed + Movable):** Provide structural rigidity to the entire assembly.
8. **Cake Discharge Chute:** Located beneath the press to collect the wet cake when plates are opened.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why alternating arrangement?** Each frame is bounded on both sides by a plate — meaning *one frame gives TWO filtration surfaces*. For **n frames**, the effective filtration area is **A = 2·n·A₁**, where A₁ is the area of one side of a frame.

## 2.2 Working Cycle (Step-by-Step Logic)

The PFFP operates in **three distinct phases**, each engineered for a specific goal:

| Step | Phase | Action | Engineering Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | **Filtration** | Slurry is pumped into frames under pressure | Solid builds cake; filtrate drains out |
| **2** | **Washing (optional)** | Wash water is forced through the cake | Removes soluble impurities |
| **3** | **Cake Discharge** | Plates are separated; cake falls out | Prepares equipment for next batch |

### 2.2.1 Why Constant Pressure?

> [!TIP]
> Because the PFFP uses a **positive displacement pump** (or compressed air / hydraulic pressure), the upstream pressure remains essentially **constant**, while the flow rate *decreases with time* as the cake thickness grows. This is the classical **constant-pressure filtration** problem — a favourite KTU theory question!

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind the Physics

The flow of filtrate through the cake + filter medium is governed by **Darcy's Law**:

$$
q = \frac{1}{A}\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}
$$

Where:
- $q$ = superficial velocity of filtrate (m/s)
- $V$ = cumulative filtrate volume (m³)
- $A$ = total filtration area (m²)
- $\Delta P$ = pressure drop across cake + medium (Pa)
- $\mu$ = filtrate viscosity (Pa·s)
- $R_{total}$ = total resistance = **R_cake + R_medium**

The cake resistance is *not constant* — it grows linearly with the mass of cake deposited:

$$
R_{cake} = \alpha \cdot \nu \cdot \frac{V}{A} = \frac{\alpha \cdot C \cdot V}{A}
$$

Where:
- $\alpha$ = **specific cake resistance** (m/kg) — measure of cake's "flow hindrance"
- $\nu$ = volume of cake formed per unit volume of filtrate (m³ cake / m³ filtrate)
- $C$ = mass of solids per unit volume of filtrate (kg/m³)

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table is a **complete reference** for solving any PFFP problem in the KTU lab exam.

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\dfrac{dV}{dt} = \dfrac{A^{2} \cdot \Delta P}{\mu \left(\alpha \nu V + R_{m} A\right)}$ | Instantaneous filtration rate | m³/s |
| 2 | $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 \Delta P \cdot A^{2}} V + \dfrac{\mu R_{m}}{\Delta P \cdot A}$ | **Linear form (constant ΔP)** — Slope-Intercept | s/m³ |
| 3 | $\text{Slope} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 \Delta P \cdot A^{2}}$ | Used to compute **α** | s/m⁶ |
| 4 | $\text{Intercept} = \dfrac{\mu R_{m}}{\Delta P \cdot A}$ | Used to compute **R_m** | s/m³ |
| 5 | $\nu = \dfrac{\text{Vol of cake}}{\text{Vol of filtrate}} = \dfrac{C}{\rho_{s}(1-\varepsilon)}$ | Volume ratio | dimensionless |
| 6 | $C = \dfrac{\text{mass of solids}}{\text{Volume of filtrate}}$ | Solid loading | kg/m³ |
| 7 | $\alpha = \dfrac{2 \Delta P \cdot A^{2} \cdot m}{\mu \cdot \nu}$ | Specific cake resistance | m/kg |
| 8 | $R_{m} = \dfrac{\Delta P \cdot A \cdot C_i}{\mu \cdot m}$ | Filter medium resistance | m⁻¹ |
| 9 | $K_{p} = \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\mu \rho_{s} \nu}$ | Constant-pressure filtration constant | m²/s |
| 10 | $V^{2} + 2 V V_{f} = K_{p} A^{2} t$ | **Carman Equation (constant ΔP)** | m⁶ |

> Where in equations 2-4: $m$ = slope of $t/V$ vs $V$ plot, and $C_i$ = intercept of $t/V$ vs $V$ plot.

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL: Equation (10) is a KTU favourite for 14-mark derivations.** Note that $V_{f}$ is the *equivalent volume of filtrate* that would produce the same resistance as the filter medium — it is **NOT** a measured volume. It can be calculated from $V_{f} = \dfrac{R_m A}{\alpha \nu}$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Application |
|---|---|
| **Mineral Processing** | Dewatering of copper, zinc, iron concentrates |
| **Pharmaceuticals** | Recovery of antibiotics, fermentation broths |
| **Effluent Treatment** | Sludge dewatering in CETPs / STPs |
| **Dye & Pigment** | Separation of organic pigments from mother liquor |
| **Sugar Industry** | Clarification and mud filtration |
| **Cement Industry** | Kiln feed slurry dewatering |
| **Edible Oil** | Bleaching earth removal from refined oil |

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** PFFPs achieve **dryer cakes** (lower moisture content) than vacuum filters because of the higher pressure drop (15 bar vs 0.7 bar), but they have **lower continuous throughput** — a classic *yield vs cost* trade-off.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation of Constant-Pressure Filtration Equation (The "Carman Equation")

This derivation is a **guaranteed 14-mark question** in KTU 2024 scheme. Each step is shown explicitly.

### Step 1 — Start with Darcy's Law for flow through porous media

> Darcy's Law states that the superficial velocity of a fluid through a porous bed is proportional to the pressure gradient and inversely proportional to the fluid viscosity.

$$
\frac{dV}{A \cdot dt} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \cdot R_{total}}
$$

### Step 2 — Express the total resistance

The total resistance is the sum of cake resistance and filter medium resistance.

$$
R_{total} = R_{cake} + R_{m}
$$

### Step 3 — Relate cake resistance to cumulative filtrate volume

The cake grows as filtration proceeds. The volume of cake formed is proportional to the volume of filtrate produced (through the volumetric ratio $\nu$). Cake resistance increases linearly with the mass of solids deposited.

$$
R_{cake} = \alpha \cdot \nu \cdot \frac{V}{A}
$$

### Step 4 — Substitute and rearrange

$$
\frac{dV}{A \cdot dt} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu \left( \alpha \nu \dfrac{V}{A} + R_{m} \right)}
$$

Cross-multiplying:

$$
\left( \alpha \nu \frac{V}{A} + R_{m} \right) dV = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} dt
$$

### Step 5 — Separate variables and integrate

$$
\int_0^{V} \left( \frac{\alpha \nu}{A} V + R_{m} \right) dV = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} \int_0^{t} dt
$$

Performing the integration on the LHS:

$$
\frac{\alpha \nu}{2 A} V^{2} + R_{m} V = \frac{A \cdot \Delta P}{\mu} t
$$

### Step 6 — Rearrange into the "Linear Form" used in KTU lab plots

Dividing both sides by $V$:

$$
\frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 \Delta P A^{2}} V + \frac{\mu R_{m}}{\Delta P A}
$$

This is the **equation of a straight line**: $y = m x + C$, where:

- $y = t/V$, $x = V$
- $m$ (slope) $= \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 \Delta P A^{2}}$
- $C$ (intercept) $= \dfrac{\mu R_{m}}{\Delta P A}$

### Step 7 — Solve for the experimental unknowns

From the slope:

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 \Delta P A^{2} m}{\mu \nu}
$$

From the intercept:

$$
R_{m} = \frac{\Delta P A \cdot C}{\mu}
$$

### Step 8 — Alternative "Square Law" form (Carman Equation)

Multiplying the result from Step 5 by 2:

$$
V^{2} + \frac{2 R_{m} A}{\alpha \nu} V = \frac{2 A^{2} \Delta P}{\mu \alpha \nu} t
$$

Defining $V_{f} = \dfrac{R_{m} A}{\alpha \nu}$ and $K_{p} = \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\mu \rho_{s} \nu}$:

$$
V^{2} + 2 V V_{f} = K_{p} A^{2} t
$$

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (Typical KTU Lab Calculation)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem Statement:** A plate and frame filter press with **total filtration area A = 0.5 m²** is used to filter a **CaCO₃ slurry** (C = 25 kg/m³ filtrate, ν = 0.04 m³ cake/m³ filtrate, μ = 0.001 Pa·s) at a constant pressure of **ΔP = 3.5 × 10⁵ Pa**. Lab data is given below. Find α and R_m.

| Time t (s) | Volume V (L) | V in m³ | t/V (s/m³) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 10 | 0.40 | 0.0004 | 25,000 |
| 30 | 0.75 | 0.00075 | 40,000 |
| 60 | 1.05 | 0.00105 | 57,143 |
| 120 | 1.45 | 0.00145 | 82,759 |
| 240 | 2.05 | 0.00205 | 117,073 |

### Solution Walkthrough

**Step A — Linear regression of (V, t/V):**

Using least-squares fit on the data, we obtain:

- Slope $m \approx 5.357 \times 10^{7}$ s/m⁶
- Intercept $C \approx 5,000$ s/m³

**Step B — Calculate specific cake resistance α:**

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 \Delta P A^{2} m}{\mu \nu} = \frac{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^{5} \times (0.5)^{2} \times 5.357 \times 10^{7}}{0.001 \times 0.04}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 \times 3.5 \times 10^{5} \times 0.25 \times 5.357 \times 10^{7}}{4 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{9.375 \times 10^{12}}{4 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.34 \times 10^{17} \text{ m/kg}
$$

> ⚠️ The large magnitude is due to SI units — KTU examiners accept values in the range of $10^{11}$–$10^{14}$ m/kg for inorganic slurries when CGS or different units are used. Always check the unit system before submitting.

**Step C — Calculate filter medium resistance R_m:**

$$
R_{m} = \frac{\Delta P A C}{\mu} = \frac{3.5 \times 10^{5} \times 0.5 \times 5{,}000}{0.001}
$$

$$
R_{m} = \frac{8.75 \times 10^{8}}{10^{-3}} = 8.75 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix (PFFP vs Other Filters)

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks comparison questions. Memorize the right column.

| Feature | PFFP (Pressure) | Rotary Vacuum Filter | Sand Filter |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Operating Pressure** | High (2–15 bar) | Vacuum (<1 bar) | Gravity / low |
| **Cake Moisture** | **Low (15–35 %)** | Higher (25–60 %) | High (60–80 %) |
| **Mode** | Batch | Continuous | Batch / Continuous |
| **Cake Wash** | Excellent (2-sided) | Limited | Poor |
| **Capital Cost** | Medium | High | Low |
| **Throughput** | Medium | High | Low |
| **Slurry Solids Limit** | Up to 50 % | Up to 30 % | < 5 % |
| **Filtration Equation** | $V^{2} + 2VV_{f} = K_{p} A^{2} t$ | $V^{2} + 2VV_{f} = K_{p} A^{2} t$ | Empirical |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Constructional Diagram of Plate and Frame Filter Press

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph A["HYDRAULIC CLOSING SYSTEM (TOP)"]
        A1["Pressure Gauge (0-20 bar)"]
        A2["Closing Cylinder"]
    end

    subgraph B["FILTER PACK ASSEMBLY (CENTER)"]
        direction LR
        B1["Fixed End Plate"] --> B2["Filter Plate 1"]
        B2 --> B3["Filter Frame 1"]
        B3 --> B4["Filter Plate 2"]
        B4 --> B5["Filter Frame 2"]
        B5 --> B6["Filter Plate 3"]
        B6 --> B7["Filter Frame 3"]
        B7 --> B8["Movable End Plate"]
    end

    subgraph C["SLURRY INLET LINE (LEFT)"]
        C1["Slurry Feed Pump"] --> C2["Slurry Header Pipe"]
    end

    subgraph D["FILTRATE COLLECTION (BOTTOM)"]
        D1["Filtrate Header"] --> D2["Filtrate Storage Tank"]
    end

    subgraph E["CAKE DISCHARGE (BOTTOM)"]
        E1["Cake Collection Chute"] --> E2["Cake Conveyor / Disposal"]
    end

    C2 -.feeds slurry.-> B3
    C2 -.feeds slurry.-> B5
    C2 -.feeds slurry.-> B7
    B2 -.filtrate drains.-> D1
    B4 -.filtrate drains.-> D1
    B6 -.filtrate drains.-> D1
    B3 -.wet cake drops.-> E1
    B5 -.wet cake drops.-> E1
    B7 -.wet cake drops.-> E1
    A2 --> B8
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The slurry enters each frame through the central channel. As pressure builds, the liquid portion is forced through the filter cloth on either side of the frame, flows down the grooved plate surfaces, and exits through the filtrate header. The solids are trapped inside the frame, forming the cake.

## 4.2 Functional Flow Block Diagram of PFFP Operation

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start(["START: Slurry in Feed Tank"]) --> prep["Step 1: Prime the Slurry Pump"]
    prep --> close["Step 2: Close the Filter Pack (Hydraulic)"]
    close --> press["Step 3: Build Pressure to Setpoint (e.g. 5 bar)"]
    press --> filt["Step 4: Slurry Pumped into Frames"]
    filt --> filt2["Step 5: Cake Builds on Cloth; Filtrate Drains"]
    filt2 --> check{"Filtrate Clarity OK?"}
    check -- No --> stop1["Stop & Investigate Cloth Damage"]
    check -- Yes --> cont["Step 6: Continue to End-Point Pressure"]
    cont --> wash{"Washing Required?"}
    wash -- Yes --> wsh["Step 7: Pump Wash Water through Cake"]
    wash -- No --> disch
    wsh --> disch["Step 8: Release Pressure & Open Plates"]
    disch --> drop["Step 9: Cake Discharges into Chute"]
    drop --> clean["Step 10: Wash Cloth (Back-flush)"]
    clean --> ready{"Next Batch?"}
    ready -- Yes --> prep
    ready -- No --> stop(["END: Equipment Secured"])
```

## 4.3 Schematic Cross-Section of One Frame Assembly

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph frame1["ONE FRAME ASSEMBLY (CROSS-SECTION)"]
        direction LR
        P1["PLATE A (Left)"] --- C1["Filter Cloth L"]
        C1 --- F1["FRAME CHAMBER (Slurry + Cake)"]
        F1 --- C2["Filter Cloth R"]
        C2 --- P2["PLATE B (Right)"]
    end

    Slurry(["Slurry Inlet (top center)"]) --.feeds.-> F1
    FiltL(["Filtrate Outlet L"]) --.collects from.-> P1
    FiltR(["Filtrate Outlet R"]) --.collects from.-> P2
    Cake(["Filter Cake (solid)")] --.retained in.-> F1
```

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering pedagogy:** Notice that a *single frame* is bounded by *two plates*, each fitted with a filter cloth. This is why **A = 2 × n × A_frame_side** — the factor of 2 is a frequently missed point in KTU exams.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper 1, Q1(a)]**
> **Q1.** What is a *plate and frame filter press*? List any two of its industrial applications.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A plate and frame filter press is a **batch-type pressure filtration equipment** used for solid–liquid separation, consisting of alternating **filter plates and hollow frames** mounted on a horizontal structure. A filter cloth is placed over each plate; the slurry is fed under pressure, the liquid (filtrate) passes through the cloth and drains out, while the solids accumulate as a **filter cake** inside the frame. *(2 marks)*

Two industrial applications: *(1 mark)*
1. Dewatering of mineral concentrates (e.g., Cu, Zn, Fe) in metallurgical industries.
2. Sludge dewatering in effluent / wastewater treatment plants.

---

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Q1(b)]**
> **Q2.** Define **(a) specific cake resistance (α)** and **(b) filter medium resistance (R_m)**. State their SI units.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**(a) Specific cake resistance (α):** It is defined as the resistance offered by a unit mass of filter cake to the flow of filtrate. It is a function of cake porosity, particle size, and shape. **SI Unit: m/kg**. *(1.5 marks)*

**(b) Filter medium resistance (R_m):** It is the resistance offered by the filter cloth (or any filter medium) to the flow of filtrate at the beginning of filtration. **SI Unit: m⁻¹**. *(1.5 marks)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Choice-Based)

> **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module Experiment 2, Q9]**

### ⭐ OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)

**A plate and frame filter press with a total filtration area of 0.45 m² is used to filter a slurry at a constant pressure of 4.0 × 10⁵ Pa. The viscosity of the filtrate is 1.0 × 10⁻³ Pa·s. The following data are obtained:**

| t (s) | V (L) | t (s) | V (L) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 12 | 0.42 | 110 | 1.30 |
| 28 | 0.65 | 200 | 1.72 |
| 60 | 0.92 | 340 | 2.15 |
| 85 | 1.10 | 500 | 2.55 |

**Assume ν = 0.05 m³ cake / m³ filtrate.**

**(a)** Plot t/V vs V and determine the slope and intercept. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the specific cake resistance (α) and the filter medium resistance (R_m). **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Plot t/V vs V (7 marks):**

Convert volumes to m³ and calculate t/V:

| t (s) | V (m³) | t/V (s/m³) |
|---|---|---|
| 12 | 0.00042 | 28,571 |
| 28 | 0.00065 | 43,077 |
| 60 | 0.00092 | 65,217 |
| 85 | 0.00110 | 77,273 |
| 110 | 0.00130 | 84,615 |
| 200 | 0.00172 | 116,279 |
