| 340 | 0.00215 | 158,140 |
| 500 | 0.00255 | 196,078 |

Plotting t/V (y-axis) against V (x-axis) yields a straight line. By least-squares linear regression:

$$
m = \frac{N \sum x y - \sum x \sum y}{N \sum x^{2} - (\sum x)^{2}} \approx 6.85 \times 10^{7} \; \text{s/m}^{6}
$$

$$
C = \frac{\sum y - m \sum x}{N} \approx 2{,}500 \; \text{s/m}^{3}
$$

*(Award 7 marks for correct plotting procedure, identification of slope and intercept with units, and tabular work.)*

**Part (b) — Calculation of α and R_m (7 marks):**

**Specific cake resistance (α):**

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 \Delta P A^{2} m}{\mu \nu}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{5} \times (0.45)^{2} \times 6.85 \times 10^{7}}{1.0 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.05}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{2 \times 4.0 \times 10^{5} \times 0.2025 \times 6.85 \times 10^{7}}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}}
$$

$$
\alpha = \frac{1.1097 \times 10^{13}}{5.0 \times 10^{-5}} = 2.22 \times 10^{17} \text{ m/kg}
$$

**Filter medium resistance (R_m):**

$$
R_{m} = \frac{\Delta P A C}{\mu} = \frac{4.0 \times 10^{5} \times 0.45 \times 2{,}500}{1.0 \times 10^{-3}}
$$

$$
R_{m} = \frac{4.5 \times 10^{8}}{10^{-3}} = 4.5 \times 10^{11} \text{ m}^{-1}
$$

*(Award 7 marks: 3 for correct α formula substitution, 2 for R_m formula and substitution, 2 for final answers with units.)*

---

### ⭐ OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)

**(a)** With the aid of a neat sketch, describe the **construction and working** of a plate and frame filter press. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Derive the **constant-pressure filtration equation** in the linear form $t/V = mV + C$, clearly defining all terms. **(7 marks)**

#### MODEL SOLUTION

**Part (a) — Construction & Working (7 marks):**

**Construction (3.5 marks):** The filter press consists of a **head plate** (fixed) and a **terminal plate** (movable) supported on two strong horizontal bars. Between them are arranged **alternating filter plates and frames**. The plates have **recessed/grooved surfaces** on both sides and an outlet for the filtrate; the frames are **hollow rectangular sections** that form the chamber where the cake builds up. A **filter cloth** is draped over each plate. The whole pack is **tightened by a hydraulic or screw mechanism**. Slurry enters through a **central channel** that runs through all frames, and filtrate exits through individual cocks at the base of each plate.

**Working (3.5 marks):** The plates and frames are tightly clamped. Slurry is pumped under pressure (2–15 bar) by a positive-displacement pump. It enters the frames through the central channel; the **liquid portion (filtrate)** is forced through the filter cloth, flows down the grooved plate faces, and drains out through the cocks. The **solid particles** are retained, building up a cake inside the frame. When the frames are full, the inlet is closed, the pack is opened, and the **wet cake falls out** by gravity. The cloth may then be washed and the cycle repeated.

*(Award marks: 1 for labelled sketch, 1.5 each for construction & working, 1 for neatness.)*

**Part (b) — Derivation of Linear Form (7 marks):**

The complete derivation is presented below step-by-step. Each step is graded:

1. **Start from Darcy's Law (1 mark):**
   $$ \frac{1}{A}\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{\Delta P}{\mu(R_c + R_m)} $$

2. **Express cake resistance (1 mark):**
   $$ R_c = \frac{\alpha \nu V}{A} $$

3. **Substitute and rearrange (1 mark):**
   $$ \left( \frac{\alpha \nu V}{A} + R_m \right) dV = \frac{A \Delta P}{\mu} dt $$

4. **Integrate from 0 → V and 0 → t (2 marks):**
   $$ \frac{\alpha \nu}{2A} V^2 + R_m V = \frac{A \Delta P}{\mu} t $$

5. **Divide both sides by V to obtain linear form (1 mark):**
   $$ \frac{t}{V} = \frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 \Delta P A^{2}} V + \frac{\mu R_m}{\Delta P A} $$

6. **Identify slope and intercept with units (1 mark):**
   $$ m = \frac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 \Delta P A^{2}} \quad ; \quad C = \frac{\mu R_m}{\Delta P A} $$

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse R_m (m⁻¹) with R_c (m⁻¹) and α (m/kg).** These are the three most-confused symbols in the entire syllabus. A unit-mismatch in the final answer costs you 1 full mark.
> 2. **Do NOT forget the factor of 2 in the area** — for n frames, A = 2·n·A_side, not n·A_side. Examiners specifically test this.
> 3. **In the linear equation $t/V = mV + C$, the slope is $m$ and intercept is $C$** — do not interchange them when writing the formula for α.
> 4. **Units of α in SI are extremely large ($10^{14}$–$10^{17}$ m/kg)** because the cake is very thin per kg of solids. Do not "sanity check" by comparison with textbook values in CGS units.
> 5. **Do not omit the constant-pressure assumption** in the derivation — without stating $\Delta P = $ constant, the integration in Step 4 is invalid and you lose 2 marks.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist — Plate and Frame Filter Press**

- [ ] **Type:** Batch, pressure-operated, intermittent solid-liquid separation device.
- [ ] **Key components:** Plates (recessed/grooved), frames (hollow), filter cloth, hydraulic closing, slurry inlet header, filtrate outlet cocks.
- [ ] **For n frames, total filtration area A = 2·n·A_side** (most-missed fact).
- [ ] **Pressure:** 2–15 bar (much higher than vacuum filters → drier cake).
- [ ] **Filtration mode:** Constant pressure, since positive-displacement pump is used.
- [ ] **Darcy's Law:** $q = \dfrac{\Delta P}{\mu R_{total}}$ — starting point of all filtration theory.
- [ ] **Cake resistance grows with V:** $R_c = \dfrac{\alpha \nu V}{A}$.
- [ ] **Master linear form:** $\dfrac{t}{V} = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 \Delta P A^{2}} V + \dfrac{\mu R_m}{\Delta P A}$ (slope–intercept form).
- [ ] **Slope $m = \dfrac{\mu \alpha \nu}{2 \Delta P A^{2}}$** → gives α.
- [ ] **Intercept $C = \dfrac{\mu R_m}{\Delta P A}$** → gives R_m.
- [ ] **Carman Equation (Square Law):** $V^{2} + 2VV_f = K_p A^{2} t$, with $V_f = \dfrac{R_m A}{\alpha \nu}$ and $K_p = \dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\mu \rho_s \nu}$.
- [ ] **Specific cake resistance α units: m/kg** (SI) — values typically 10¹¹ – 10¹⁷ m/kg.
- [ ] **Filter medium resistance R_m units: m⁻¹** (SI) — values typically 10⁹ – 10¹² m⁻¹.
- [ ] **Volumetric ratio ν** = volume of cake per unit volume of filtrate = C / [ρ_s(1−ε)].
- [ ] **Three operating phases:** Filtration → (Optional) Washing → Cake Discharge.
- [ ] **Advantages:** High pressure, low cake moisture, excellent washing, simple construction, scalable.
- [ ] **Disadvantages:** Batch operation (low throughput), high labour for cake removal, cloth wear.
- [ ] **Industrial applications:** Minerals, pharma, effluent treatment, sugar, dye, cement, edible oil.
- [ ] **PFFP vs RVF comparison:** PFFP gives drier cake but is batch; RVF is continuous but cake is wetter.
- [ ] **ΔP is constant** — never integrate as a variable in this experiment.
- [ ] **Always convert V to m³ and t to seconds** before plotting — KTU valuation is unit-strict.
- [ ] **Plot t/V vs V** is ALWAYS a straight line for constant-pressure filtration — this is the visual confirmation that the experiment is valid.
- [ ] **Lab viva favourites:** (i) Why grooves on plates? (to channel filtrate out), (ii) Why two cloths per frame? (each plate has a cloth on its cake-facing side), (iii) What is V_f? (equivalent filtrate volume of the medium resistance), (iv) What is the role of slurry concentration on α?
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Free settling: To find out the drag coefficient of a falling sphere in a fluid and verification of Stoke's law
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB > - **Module:** Module Experiment 2 > - **Topic:** Free settling: To find out the drag coefficient of a falling sphere in a fluid and verification of Stoke's law # 🧪 Module Experiment 2 — Free Settling of a Sphere in a Fluid ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Free Settling** is the gravity-induced descent of a solid particle through a stationary fluid medium (gas or liquid) without any interference from walls, other particles, or external fields. In the context of this KTU laboratory experiment, a spherical ball is allowed to fall freely through a transparent liquid column held in a graduated glass tube, and its motion is analyzed to determine the **drag coefficient (C_D)** and to verify the validity of **Stokes' Law**. > [!NOTE] > **Stokes' Law (1851)** states that the viscous drag force (F_d) experienced by a small sphere moving slowly through a viscous fluid is directly proportional to the dynamic viscosity (μ) of the fluid, the radius (r) of the sphere, and the relative velocity (v) between the sphere and the fluid. $$\boxed{F_d = 6\pi \mu r v}$$ The **Drag Coefficient (C_D)** is a dimensionless number that quantifies the resistance of a fluid environment to the motion of an object. For a sphere, it is defined as: $$C_D = \frac{F_d}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_f v^2 A}$$ where **ρ_f** is the fluid density and **A = πr²** is the projected (cross-sectional) area of the sphere. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment lies at the intersection of *fluid mechanics* and *mechanical separations* (sedimentation, classification, cyclone design). The drag curve (C_D vs. Re) is the foundation for designing settling chambers, hydrocyclones, and electrostatic precipitators used in chemical process industries. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine a **skydiver jumping out of an airplane**: - Initially, the diver accelerates downward because gravity dominates. - As speed increases, air resistance (drag) builds up. - Eventually, drag force equals the net gravitational pull (weight minus buoyancy), and the diver reaches a constant speed — the **terminal velocity**. A steel ball dropped in oil behaves *exactly* like the skydiver, but inside a much denser, more viscous fluid. The ball does not "feel" a parachute, but the oil itself behaves like one — creating viscous friction that balances gravity. > [!NOTE] > A **rain droplet** is the perfect natural example: tiny droplets (~0.1 mm) fall slowly and obey Stokes' Law; large raindrops (~5 mm) fall faster and follow Newton's Law regime. ### 1.3 Standard Physical Constants & Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Acceleration due to gravity | **g** | **9.81 m/s²** | | Dynamic viscosity of water (20 °C) | μ | **1.002 × 10⁻³ Pa·s** | | Kinematic viscosity of air (20 °C) | ν | **1.51 × 10⁻⁵ m²/s** | | Density of water (20 °C) | ρ | **998.2 kg/m³** | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Force Balance on a Falling Sphere When a sphere falls through a fluid, **three forces** act on it along the vertical axis: 1. **Gravitational (Weight) Force — F_g = m·g = (4/3)πr³ρ_p·g** Acts downward. Depends on sphere density (ρ_p). 2. **Buoyancy Force (Upthrust) — F_b = (4/3)πr³ρ_f·g** Acts upward, as per Archimedes' principle. 3. **Drag Force — F_d = 6πμrv (Stokes' regime)** Acts upward, opposing motion. At **terminal velocity (v_t)**, the net force becomes zero, meaning acceleration is zero. Therefore: $$F_g = F_b + F_d$$ ## 2.2 Why and How of Each Step - **Why terminal velocity?** Because a sphere cannot accelerate indefinitely — fluid friction grows with speed until equilibrium is established. - **How is it measured in the lab?** The sphere is dropped through a tall column, and a "timing zone" is marked between two reference lines (A and B). Once the sphere enters the steady-state (constant velocity) region below the entrance zone, the time `t` to traverse a known distance `L` is recorded. The terminal velocity is then: $$v_t = \frac{L}{t}$$ - **Why is there a marked "entry" and "exit" zone?** The sphere requires a small distance to shed its initial impulsive velocity and reach true terminal velocity. Measurements are only valid in the steady region. ## 2.3 Drag Coefficient and Reynolds Number The **Reynolds number** characterizes the flow regime around the sphere: $$Re = \frac{\rho_f \, v_t \, D}{\mu}$$ where **D = 2r** is the sphere diameter. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Stokes' Law is valid ONLY when Re < 0.2 (laminar flow regime).** This is the KTU 2024 experimental condition you must satisfy. With higher Re, the flow becomes turbulent and Stokes' law under-predicts drag. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $F_d = 6\pi \mu r v$ | Stokes' drag force | N | | 2 | $v_t = \frac{L}{t}$ | Terminal velocity (experimental) | m/s | | 3 | $Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t D}{\mu}$ | Reynolds number | dimensionless | | 4 | $C_D = \frac{2 F_d}{\rho_f v_t^2 A}$ | Drag coefficient | dimensionless | | 5 | $C_D = \frac{24}{Re}$ | Stokes' law prediction | dimensionless | | 6 | $F_g = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_p g$ | Weight of sphere | N | | 7 | $F_b = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g$ | Buoyancy force | N | | 8 | $v_t = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}$ | Theoretical terminal velocity | m/s | | 9 | $v_t = \frac{D^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$ | Alternate form using D | m/s | | 10 | $A = \pi r^2$ | Projected area of sphere | m² | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The drag coefficient curve is essential in designing **cyclone separators** (petrochemical dust removal), **sedimentation tanks** (water treatment plants), **fluidized bed reactors**, and **elutriators** (mineral processing). KTU frequently tests this application context. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1: Terminal Velocity from Stokes' Law **Step 1 — Start with the force balance at terminal velocity:** $$F_g - F_b - F_d = 0$$ **Step 2 — Substitute the known force expressions:** $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_p g - \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 \rho_f g - 6\pi \mu r v_t = 0$$ **Step 3 — Group the gravity and buoyancy terms:** $$\frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 g (\rho_p - \rho_f) = 6\pi \mu r v_t$$ **Step 4 — Simplify the left side by factoring out common terms (r, π, 3):** $$\frac{4 r^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3} = \frac{6 \mu v_t}{1}$$ **Step 5 — Solve for v_t:** $$v_t = \frac{4 r^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \times 6 \mu} = \frac{4 r^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$$ $$\boxed{v_t = \frac{2 r^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9 \mu}}$$ > **Logic:** This is the **theoretical terminal velocity** predicted by Stokes' law. The experimental value should match this if Stokes' law is valid. ## 3.2 Derivation 2: Experimental Drag Coefficient **Step 1 — Measure the experimental terminal velocity v_t = L / t.** **Step 2 — Calculate the measured drag force using the force balance at terminal velocity:** $$F_{d,\text{exp}} = F_g - F_b = \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g$$ **Step 3 — Compute the projected area of the sphere:** $$A = \pi r^2$$ **Step 4 — Substitute into the drag coefficient definition:** $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{2 F_{d,\text{exp}}}{\rho_f v_t^2 A} = \frac{2 \times \frac{4}{3}\pi r^3 (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{\rho_f v_t^2 \times \pi r^2}$$ **Step 5 — Simplify the expression:** $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{8 r (\rho_p - \rho_f) g}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$$ $$\boxed{C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{8 r g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}}$$ > **Logic:** This is the **experimentally determined drag coefficient** — it has no viscosity in the formula because we computed drag from a force balance, not from Stokes' law itself. ## 3.3 Derivation 3: Theoretical Drag Coefficient (Stokes) **Step 1 — Begin with Stokes' drag force:** $$F_d = 6\pi \mu r v_t$$ **Step 2 — Insert into the drag coefficient formula:** $$C_{D,\text{theory}} = \frac{2 \times 6\pi \mu r v_t}{\rho_f v_t^2 \times \pi r^2}$$ **Step 3 — Cancel common terms:** $$C_{D,\text{theory}} = \frac{12 \mu}{\rho_f v_t r}$$ **Step 4 — Multiply numerator and denominator by 2 and use the Reynolds number identity:** $$Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t D}{\mu} = \frac{2 \rho_f v_t r}{\mu}$$ Rearranging: $\mu / (\rho_f v_t r) = 2 / Re$ $$C_{D,\text{theory}} = \frac{12 \times 2}{Re} = \frac{24}{Re}$$ $$\boxed{C_{D,\text{theory}} = \frac{24}{Re}}$$ > **Logic:** The famous result C_D = 24/Re is the canonical Stokes' law prediction. Plotting log(C_D) vs log(Re) should give a straight line of slope -1, intercept 24. ## 3.4 Worked-Out Numerical Example **Given data for a typical KTU lab problem:** - Sphere material: Steel, ρ_p = **7800 kg/m³** - Sphere diameter: D = **3 mm = 0.003 m**, r = **0.0015 m** - Fluid: Glycerine-water mix, ρ_f = **1200 kg/m³**, μ = **0.5 Pa·s** - Drop distance: L = **0.5 m** - Time recorded: t = **4.2 s** **Step 1 — Compute experimental terminal velocity:** $$v_t = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.5}{4.2} = 0.1190 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Compute Reynolds number:** $$Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t D}{\mu} = \frac{1200 \times 0.1190 \times 0.003}{0.5} = 0.857$$ > [!WARNING] > Re = 0.857 > 0.2 — this means **Stokes' law is at the boundary of validity**. KTU expects students to *comment* on this in the viva. **Step 3 — Experimental drag coefficient:** $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{8 r g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$$ $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{8 \times 0.0015 \times 9.81 \times (7800 - 1200)}{3 \times 1200 \times (0.1190)^2}$$ $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{8 \times 0.0015 \times 9.81 \times 6600}{3 \times 1200 \times 0.01416}$$ $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{776.95}{50.98} = 15.24$$ **Step 4 — Theoretical drag coefficient (Stokes' prediction):** $$C_{D,\text{theory}} = \frac{24}{Re} = \frac{24}{0.857} = 28.00$$ **Step 5 — Percentage deviation (verification of Stokes' law):** $$\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{\vert C_{D,\text{theory}} - C_{D,\text{exp}} \vert}{C_{D,\text{theory}}} \times 100$$ $$\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{\vert 28.00 - 15.24 \vert}{28.00} \times 100 = 45.6\%$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Interpretation:** A 45.6% deviation confirms that Stokes' law **does not hold** in this regime because Re = 0.857 > 0.2. To verify Stokes' law experimentally, the lab uses very small spheres in very viscous fluids to keep Re < 0.2. ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Verification of Stokes' Law | Sphere # | D (mm) | ρ_p (kg/m³) | t (s) | v_t (m/s) | Re | C_D,exp | C_D,theory | % Deviation | |---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | 1.0 | 7800 | 8.0 | 0.0625 | 0.075 | 320 | 320.0 | ~0% ✅ | | 2 | 2.0 | 7800 | 3.0 | 0.167 | 0.40 | 72 | 60.0 | 20% ⚠️ | | 3 | 3.0 | 7800 | 1.5 | 0.333 | 1.20 | 22 | 20.0 | 10% ⚠️ | | 4 | 5.0 | 7800 | 0.8 | 0.625 | 3.75 | 6.4 | 6.4 | ~0% (transition) | | 5 | 8.0 | 7800 | 0.5 | 1.000 | 9.60 | 2.5 | 2.5 | ~0% (Newton) | > **Logic:** This table is what a KTU-graded lab report should contain. The plot of C_D vs. Re (log-log) is the **mandatory graph** required for this experiment. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Experimental Setup — Functional Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Glass Settling Column
Length 1.0 m, ID 30 mm] --> B[Fill with Test Fluid
Glycerine-Water Mixture] B --> C[Mark Reference Lines
Line A - top, Line B - bottom] C --> D[Hold Sphere with Tweezers
at Top of Column] D --> E[Release Sphere
Start Stopwatch at Line A] E --> F[Stop Stopwatch at Line B] F --> G[Record Time t
for Distance L] G --> H[Repeat 3-5 Times
for Accuracy] H --> I[Compute Terminal
Velocity v_t = L/t] I --> J[Calculate Re and C_D] J --> K[Plot C_D vs Re
log-log Graph] K --> L[Compare with
Stokes Law C_D = 24/Re] L --> M[Verify Stokes Law
and Report Conclusion] ``` ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Forces Acting on a Falling Sphere ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph FORCES[FORCES ACTING ON SPHERE] FG[Weight F_g = 4/3 x pi x r^3 x rho_p x g
Acts DOWNWARD] FB[Buoyancy F_b = 4/3 x pi x r^3 x rho_f x g
Acts UPWARD] FD[Drag Force F_d = 6 pi mu r v
Acts UPWARD] end subgraph STATE[STEADY STATE] TS[Terminal Velocity v_t
Net Force = 0] end FG --> TS FB --> TS FD --> TS TS --> CALC[CALCULATE
C_D,exp AND Re] CALC --> PLOT[PLOT AND VERIFY
STOKES LAW] ``` ## 4.3 Apparatus Inventory ```mermaid flowchart TD A[APPARATUS REQUIRED] --> B[Glass Settling Tube
Length 1m, ID 30mm] A --> C[Spheres of Different Sizes
Steel, Glass, Lead] A --> D[Stopwatch
Least Count 0.01 s] A --> E[Measuring Cylinder
for Fluid Volume] A --> F[Thermometer
for Fluid Temperature] A --> G[Vernier Caliper
for Sphere Diameter] A --> H[Weighing Balance
for Sphere Mass] A --> I[Viscometer
for Fluid Viscosity] A --> J[Hydrometer
for Fluid Density] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define Stokes' Law. State the conditions under which it is applicable. **Model Answer (3 marks):** Stokes' Law (1851) gives the viscous drag force on a small sphere moving slowly through a viscous fluid: $$F_d = 6\pi \mu r v$$ **Conditions of Applicability (any 2 for full marks):** 1. The flow around the sphere must be **laminar**, i.e., Reynolds number **Re < 0.2**. 2. The fluid must be **incompressible** and **Newtonian**. 3. The sphere must be **smooth, rigid, and spherical**. 4. There must be **no slip** at the sphere surface. 5. The fluid must extend to **infinity** (i.e., no wall effects — sphere diameter << tube diameter). 6. The motion must be at **low velocities** (creeping flow regime). > **[1 mark — definition; 1 mark — formula; 1 mark — any two conditions.]** --- ### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] What is a drag coefficient? Why is it expressed as a dimensionless number? **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **drag coefficient (C_D)** is defined as the ratio of the drag force to the product of the dynamic pressure and the projected area of the body: $$C_D = \frac{F_d}{\frac{1}{2}\rho_f v^2 A}$$ **Why dimensionless (2 marks):** 1. The numerator has units of N (force). 2. The denominator has units of $\frac{1}{2} \times \frac{kg}{m^3} \times \frac{m^2}{s^2} \times m^2 = N$. 3. Numerator and denominator have identical units, hence C_D is **dimensionless**. 4. This allows comparison of drag behavior across fluids of widely different densities, viscosities, and flow speeds — a sphere falling in water behaves similarly (in terms of C_D) to one falling in air at the same Re. > **[1 mark — definition; 1 mark — formula; 1 mark — dimensionless explanation with reason.]** --- ## 5.2 Part B — Full-Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each) > **INTERNAL CHOICE:** Answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. ### ❓ Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Jul 2024]** A steel sphere of diameter 2 mm and density 7800 kg/m³ is dropped in a column of oil of density 900 kg/m³ and viscosity 0.8 Pa·s. The sphere traverses 0.4 m in 3.6 seconds at terminal velocity. **a)** Calculate the experimental terminal velocity, Reynolds number, and drag coefficient. **(7 marks)** **b)** Verify Stokes' law and comment on the validity of your result. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Solution to Q.A(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Terminal velocity (2 marks):** $$v_t = \frac{L}{t} = \frac{0.4}{3.6} = 0.1111 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2 — Reynolds number (2 marks):** $$Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t D}{\mu} = \frac{900 \times 0.1111 \times 0.002}{0.8} = 0.250$$ **Step 3 — Experimental drag coefficient (3 marks):** $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{8 r g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$$ $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{8 \times 0.001 \times 9.81 \times (7800 - 900)}{3 \times 900 \times (0.1111)^2}$$ $$C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{541.51}{33.33} = 16.25$$ --- #### ✅ Solution to Q.A(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Theoretical drag coefficient (3 marks):** $$C_{D,\text{theory}} = \frac{24}{Re} = \frac{24}{0.250} = 96.00$$ **Step 2 — Percentage deviation (2 marks):** $$\%\text{ Deviation} = \frac{\vert 96.00 - 16.25 \vert}{96.00} \times 100 = 83.07\%$$ **Step 3 — Comment on validity (2 marks):** The deviation is **83%**, which is very large. This is because **Re = 0.25 > 0.2**, the upper limit of Stokes' law. Hence, **Stokes' law is not valid** in this experiment. To verify Stokes' law, the sphere diameter, fluid density, and viscosity must be chosen such that Re < 0.2. --- ### ❓ Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** **a)** With a neat diagram, describe the experimental procedure to determine the drag coefficient of a falling sphere and verify Stokes' law. List the apparatus used. **(7 marks)** **b)** A glass sphere (ρ_p = 2500 kg/m³) of diameter 4 mm falls through castor oil (ρ_f = 960 kg/m³, μ = 0.985 Pa·s) at 25 °C. Calculate the theoretical terminal velocity and theoretical drag coefficient. Comment on whether Stokes' law will hold. **(7 marks)** --- #### ✅ Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks **Apparatus (2 marks):** - Vertical glass tube (length ~1 m, ID 30 mm) with two reference marks A and B - Spheres of different diameters (steel/glass) of known density - Glycerine-water mixture or castor oil (test fluid) - Stopwatch (accuracy 0.01 s) - Vernier caliper, weighing balance - Viscometer, hydrometer, thermometer **Procedure (5 marks):** 1. Clean the glass column and fill with test fluid. Allow to stand to remove air bubbles. 2. Mark two reference lines A and B, separated by a known distance L (e.g., 0.5 m), with adequate entry length above A and deceleration length below B. 3. Measure sphere diameter D using vernier caliper and mass using balance to compute density ρ_p. 4. Measure fluid density using hydrometer and viscosity using viscometer at room temperature. 5. Hold the sphere gently with tweezers at the top of the column. Release without imparting initial velocity. 6. Start the stopwatch when the sphere crosses line A; stop when it crosses line B. Record time `t`. 7. Repeat the experiment 3-5 times for the same sphere and take the average time. 8. Repeat for spheres of different diameters. 9. Calculate terminal velocity v_t = L/t, Reynolds number Re, and drag coefficient C_D. 10. Plot log(C_D) vs log(Re) and compare with the Stokes' line C_D = 24/Re. --- #### ✅ Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 — Theoretical terminal velocity (3 marks):** $$v_t = \frac{D^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18 \mu}$$ $$v_t = \frac{(0.004)^2 \times 9.81 \times (2500 - 960)}{18 \times 0.985}$$ $$v_t = \frac{1.5696 \times 10^{-4} \times 1540}{17.73} = \frac{0.2417}{17.73}$$ $$v_t = 0.01363 \text{ m/s} = 1.363 \text{ cm/s}$$ **Step 2 — Reynolds number (2 marks):** $$Re = \frac{\rho_f v_t D}{\mu} = \frac{960 \times 0.01363 \times 0.004}{0.985} = 0.0531$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical drag coefficient and conclusion (2 marks):** $$C_{D,\text{theory}} = \frac{24}{Re} = \frac{24}{0.0531} = 451.8$$ **Conclusion:** Since **Re = 0.0531 < 0.2**, Stokes' law **WILL hold** in this experiment. The drag coefficient is very high (451.8), typical of the creeping flow regime. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > > 1. **Forgetting to check Re < 0.2:** KTU examiners *always* deduct 2-3 marks if you compute C_D but do not verify the Stokes regime. Always state the Re value and comment on whether it falls in the laminar regime. > 2. **Unit conversion errors:** Diameter must be in **meters** (not mm) for SI units. Density in **kg/m³** (not g/cc). Forgetting this gives wrong answers and full mark deduction. > 3. **Sign errors in (ρ_p - ρ_f):** If ρ_p < ρ_f, the sphere *floats* — the experiment is invalid. Always check this first. > 4. **Wall effects:** KTU expects a comment that the tube diameter must be at least **10× the sphere diameter** to avoid wall effects. Missing this loses 1 mark. > 5. **Averaging time incorrectly:** Take the *mean* of 3-5 readings, not the median. The standard deviation should be reported for the viva. > 6. **Graph plotting:** The log-log plot of C_D vs Re is **mandatory**. Without it, full marks cannot be awarded for the "verification" component. --- ## 5.3 📌 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Stokes' Law:** $F_d = 6\pi\mu r v$ — valid **ONLY** for Re < 0.2. - **Terminal velocity formula (theoretical):** $v_t = \frac{2 r^2 g(\rho_p - \rho_f)}{9\mu} = \frac{D^2 g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{18\mu}$. - **Experimental terminal velocity:** $v_t = L/t$ — measured between two reference marks. - **Reynolds number:** $Re = \rho_f v_t D / \mu$ — must be **< 0.2** for Stokes regime. - **Drag coefficient definition:** $C_D = F_d / (½ \rho_f v^2 A)$ — dimensionless. - **Stokes' law prediction:** $C_D = 24/Re$ — gives a **straight line of slope -1** on a log-log plot. - **Three forces on the falling sphere:** Weight (↓), Buoyancy (↑), Drag (↑). At terminal velocity, **net force = 0**. - **Force balance equation:** $F_g = F_b + F_d$. - **Experimental drag coefficient formula:** $C_{D,\text{exp}} = \frac{8 r g (\rho_p - \rho_f)}{3 \rho_f v_t^2}$. - **Wall effect rule:** Tube diameter ≥ 10× sphere diameter (KTU lab standard). - **Apparatus mnemonic (G-S-S-V-W-H-T):** Glass tube, Spheres, Stopwatch, Scale (vernier), Weighing balance, Hydrometer/Viscometer, Thermometer. - **Validity check protocol:** Compute Re → If Re < 0.2 ✓ Stokes' law valid → Plot C_D vs Re → Verify slope. - **High-density spheres in viscous fluids = small Re** → Stokes' law holds. - **Air bubbles in the fluid** are a major experimental error source — always allow the column to settle. - **Three repetitions minimum** — for statistical reliability. - **Glycerine-water mixtures** are commonly used because their viscosity can be tuned by mixing ratio.

topics.title: Sedimentation: To study batch sedimentation of slurry and to determine the area of the continuous thickener
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Sedimentation: To study batch sedimentation of slurry and to determine the area of the continuous thickener

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Sedimentation** is a solid–liquid separation unit operation in which a dilute slurry (or suspension) is allowed to stand quiescently, and the suspended solid particles settle out of the fluid under the influence of **gravity (g = 9.81 m/s²)**. The concentration of the slurry is high enough that the particles cannot settle independently (free settling); instead, they experience **hindered settling**, where neighbouring particles hinder the motion of one another.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** Sedimentation is the gravity-driven settling of particles from a liquid medium. In chemical engineering practice, sedimentation is used to thicken slurries, clarify liquids, and classify particles by size. When a slurry is allowed to settle in a **batch column** (graduated cylinder), it forms four distinct zones: **clear liquid zone, constant-concentration (hindered settling) zone, transition zone, and compression zone**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine pouring a glass of **mango-flavoured milk shake** with crushed ice and leaving it undisturbed for an hour. The lighter, watery fraction rises to the top, while the denser pulp and crushed-ice pieces slowly sink to the bottom. A sharp, visible **interface** moves downward as the solid-rich region compacts below. That moving interface is exactly what we measure in a batch sedimentation test.

- The **clear liquid** on top behaves like the watery top layer of your milkshake.
- The **slurry** in the middle is the pulp that is just starting to settle collectively.
- The **sludge** at the bottom is the thick mass that has fully settled.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why do we need to study this?** Every chemical plant that handles slurries — mineral processing, wastewater treatment, paper & pulp, sugar refining, effluent treatment — must design a **continuous thickener**. The lab experiment teaches you to take a small batch sample, mimic it in a 1-litre cylinder, measure the settling rate, and then **scale up** that single test into the **cross-sectional area** of a full-scale industrial thickener. This is the engineering principle of **dimensional analysis and similarity** applied to solid–liquid separation.

## 1.3 Distinction Between Three Modes of Settling

| Mode | Condition | Governing Law | Industrial Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Free settling** | Dilute slurry (φ < 0.001) | Stokes' Law / Newton's Law | Cyclones, classifiers |
| **Hindered settling** | Concentrated slurry (φ ~ 0.05 – 0.4) | **Kynch's theory** | **Thickener design** (our experiment) |
| **Compression settling** | Very thick sludge (φ > 0.4) | Empirical (rheological) | Filter cake, sludge beds |

> [!NOTE]
> Our batch sedimentation test is fundamentally a **hindered settling** experiment, governed by **Kynch's theory of sedimentation (1952)**, which we will use to determine the critical settling velocity and hence the thickener area.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Zones Formed During Batch Sedimentation

When a slurry of uniform initial concentration $C_0$ is placed in a graduated cylinder and allowed to settle, four distinct zones form:

1. **Zone A — Clear Liquid (Supernatant):** At the very top, completely solids-free liquid.
2. **Zone B — Initial Settling Zone (Constant Concentration):** Right below Zone A, the suspension has *uniform* initial concentration $C_0$. All particles in this zone settle at the **same hindered-settling velocity** (a clear interface moves down at this velocity).
3. **Zone C — Transition Zone:** Concentration increases from $C_0$ to some larger value. Settling velocity decreases.
4. **Zone D — Compression Zone:** A dense sludge at the bottom where particles actually touch and form a network. Further compaction occurs slowly by **creep** and **liquid expulsion**.

> [!NOTE]
> The **interface between Zone A and Zone B** is what we visually track and record as a function of time. Plotting the height of this interface (H) versus time (t) gives the **Kynch sedimentation curve**.

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Sedimentation (The "Why" and "How")

- **Step 1 — Gravity acts on dispersed particles:** Each particle experiences weight $\rho_s V g$ minus buoyancy $\rho_L V g$, giving a net gravitational force per unit volume of $(\rho_s - \rho_L) g (1 - \phi)$.
- **Step 2 — Drag force resists motion:** In concentrated slurries, the upward drag on each particle comes not just from the fluid, but also from displaced neighbouring particles — this is the **hindered** effect.
- **Step 3 — At low concentrations, drag ≈ Stokes drag:** $F_D = 3\pi \mu d v$.
- **Step 4 — Kynch's assumption (1952):** The settling velocity $u$ of a uniform suspension depends *only* on its local solids concentration $C$ — not on its history. So $u = u(C)$.
- **Step 5 — Mass conservation in the column:** A mass balance on the dispersed phase yields the famous Kynch continuity equation that describes the propagation of constant-concentration "shock waves" upward through the slurry.
- **Step 6 — The interface height H vs t curve** shows a steep initial linear portion, a curved transition, and a near-horizontal final portion. The slope of the *linear* portion is the **hindered-settling velocity** of the initial slurry.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Remarks |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass of dry solids in slurry | $M_s = C_0 \cdot H_0 \cdot A_c$ | kg | $A_c$ = cylinder cross-section area |
| 2 | Initial height of slurry | $H_0$ | m | Read at $t = 0$ |
| 3 | Hindered settling velocity | $u_0 = -dH/dt$ (linear portion) | m/s | Negative sign → H decreases |
| 4 | Critical settling velocity (Talmage–Fitch) | $u_c$ from graphical construction | m/s | See Section 3 |
| 5 | Initial solids concentration | $C_0 = M_s / (A_c \cdot H_0)$ | kg/m³ | Same as $C_D$ in thickener feed |
| 6 | Underflow concentration | $C_B$ | kg/m³ | From plant data / mass balance |
| 7 | Continuous thickener area (Talmage–Fitch) | $A = \dfrac{M_s / t_c}{u_c \cdot \rho_s \cdot (1 - X_{avg}/X_F)}$ | m² | **Most important KTU formula** |
| 8 | Alt. form: Area (mass-flux form) | $A = \dfrac{F \cdot C_F}{u_c \cdot \rho_s \cdot \Delta \rho \cdot \phi}$ | m² | Using feed solids mass flux |
| 9 | Limiting solids flux | $G_s = u_c \cdot C_0$ | kg/m²·s | Mass flux at limiting point |
| 10 | Liquid rise velocity in thickener | $v_L = \dfrac{L_f}{A \cdot \rho_L}$ | m/s | For clear overflow |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** In the 14-mark ESE question on this experiment, you will be asked to: (a) plot the **H vs t curve**, (b) draw the **tangent from the origin** to the Kynch curve, (c) read off the **critical time $t_c$** and the **critical height $H_c$**, and (d) calculate the **thickener area** using the formula above. Memorize the **Talmage–Fitch graphical construction** — it is asked almost every year.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility in Engineering

- **Mineral processing plants:** Designing thickeners for copper, iron ore, and alumina tailings.
- **Wastewater treatment:** Primary and secondary clarifiers in sewage plants (a circular thickener is essentially a clarifier).
- **Sugar industry:** Mud thickeners in cane sugar clarification.
- **Pulp & paper:** Recovery of fibre from white water.
- **Cement industry:** Thickening of kiln-feed slurries.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Kynch Continuity Equation

Consider a vertical column of uniform cross-section $A_c$ filled with slurry of local solids concentration $C(z,t)$ at height $z$ and time $t$. The mass flux of solids in the upward direction at any height is:

$$N_s = C \cdot u(C)$$

where $u(C)$ is the local (downward) settling velocity — taken as **positive downward** for convenience in many texts, but let's take it as positive **upward flux** of mass relative to the liquid. A differential mass balance over a slice of thickness $dz$ gives:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} = -\frac{\partial N_s}{\partial z} = -\frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left[C \cdot u(C)\right]$$

Since $u$ is a function of $C$ alone, by the chain rule:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + u(C) \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} + C \cdot \frac{du}{dC} \cdot \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0$$

Combining the two middle terms:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \left[u + C \frac{du}{dC}\right] \frac{\partial C}{\partial z} = 0$$

This is a **first-order quasi-linear PDE** whose characteristic speed (the velocity at which a given concentration $C$ travels up the column) is:

$$u_c(C) = u + C \frac{du}{dC} = \frac{d(C \cdot u)}{dC}$$

**Physical interpretation:** A layer of concentration $C$ propagates upward through the slurry at a characteristic speed $u_c(C)$ which is the *slope of the chord* drawn from the origin to the point $(C, C \cdot u)$ on the **flux-concentration curve** $G_s = C \cdot u(C)$.

## 3.2 Derivation of the Continuous-Thickener Area (Talmage–Fitch Construction)

In a continuous thickener operating at steady state, the feed slurry enters at the top, clear liquid overflows at the top, and thickened sludge (underflow) leaves at the bottom. The condition for **successful operation** is that the **downward solids flux due to settling** plus the **downward bulk transport** equals the feed flux everywhere in the column.

**Step 1:** Define the total downward solids mass flux in the thickener at concentration $C$:

$$G_s(C) = C \cdot u(C) + C \cdot v$$

where $v$ is the bulk downward velocity of the suspension (positive downward). The first term is settling, the second is bulk transport.

**Step 2:** The thickener operates without choking if $G_s(C) \geq G_s(\text{feed})$ for all $C$ between the feed and underflow concentrations. This is the **Coe–Clever–Clevenger condition** (1916).

**Step 3:** Therefore the **limiting (minimum) thickener area** corresponds to the maximum of the flux function $G_s(C)$. Differentiating and setting to zero:

$$\frac{dG_s}{dC} = u(C) + C \frac{du}{dC} + v = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad v = -u_c(C)$$

At this **critical concentration $C_c$**, the bulk downward velocity exactly cancels the upward propagation of the layer (shock wave stays stationary).

**Step 4:** Hence the **minimum area per unit volumetric feed** is:

$$\frac{1}{A} = \frac{\max G_s(C)}{M_s / t}$$

For a batch column of total solids mass $M_s$ settling in time $t_c$ (the time at which the H vs t curve flattens out):

$$A = \frac{M_s / t_c}{u_c \cdot \rho_s (1 - X_{avg}/X_F)}$$

where:
- $M_s$ = total dry solids in the batch cylinder (kg)
- $t_c$ = critical time read from the Kynch plot (s)
- $u_c$ = critical settling velocity (m/s)
- $X_F$ = mass fraction of solids in feed
- $X_{avg}$ = mass fraction of solids in the average slurry

## 3.3 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-Mark Question)

**Given data:**
- Cylinder cross-sectional area: $A_c = 1.0 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}^2$ (i.e. 100 cm²)
- Initial slurry height: $H_0 = 0.30 \, \text{m}$ (30 cm)
- Mass of dry solids: $M_s = 0.060 \, \text{kg}$ (60 g)
- Feed concentration $C_F = 200 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- Underflow concentration $C_B = 800 \, \text{kg/m}^3$
- From Kynch plot: critical height $H_c = 0.18 \, \text{m}$, critical time $t_c = 1200 \, \text{s}$

**Step 1 — Verify initial concentration:**

$$C_0 = \frac{M_s}{A_c H_0} = \frac{0.060}{(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(0.30)} = \frac{0.060}{3.0 \times 10^{-4}} = 200 \, \text{kg/m}^3 \checkmark$$

**Step 2 — Determine critical settling velocity:**

$$u_c = \frac{H_0 - H_c}{t_c} = \frac{0.30 - 0.18}{1200} = \frac{0.12}{1200} = 1.0 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of solids fed per unit time (assume feed rate $F = 0.005 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s}$):**

$$\dot{M}_s = F \cdot C_F = (0.005)(200) = 1.0 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4 — Mass fraction calculation:**

$$X_F = \frac{C_F}{\rho_s} = \frac{200}{2500} = 0.080 \quad (\text{assuming } \rho_s = 2500 \, \text{kg/m}^3)$$

$$X_{avg} = X_F \cdot \frac{H_0}{H_c} = 0.080 \times \frac{0.30}{0.18} = 0.133$$

**Step 5 — Apply thickener area formula:**

$$A = \frac{\dot{M}_s}{u_c \cdot \rho_s \left(1 - \dfrac{X_{avg}}{X_F}\right)} = \frac{1.0}{(1.0 \times 10^{-4})(2500)\left(1 - \dfrac{0.133}{0.080}\right)}$$

$$A = \frac{1.0}{(0.25)\left(1 - 1.667\right)} = \frac{1.0}{(0.25)(-0.667)}$$

**Sign convention check:** The negative sign indicates the underflow is *more concentrated* than the average — physically correct. The magnitude gives:

$$\vert A \vert = \frac{1.0}{0.167} = 6.0 \, \text{m}^2$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always take the absolute value** of the area. A negative value simply means $C_B > C_0$ (thickening occurs), which is the desired operation of a thickener.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Key Variables at a Glance

| Variable | Symbol | Typical Value | Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cylinder cross-section | $A_c$ | $1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m² | Measured |
| Initial slurry height | $H_0$ | 0.20 – 0.40 m | Measured |
| Mass of dry solids | $M_s$ | 0.03 – 0.10 kg | Weighed |
| Critical height | $H_c$ | 0.15 – 0.25 m | From Kynch plot |
| Critical time | $t_c$ | 600 – 1800 s | From Kynch plot |
| Hindered settling velocity | $u_0$ | $1 \times 10^{-4}$ – $5 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s | Slope of linear portion |
| Critical settling velocity | $u_c$ | $0.5 \times 10^{-4}$ – $3 \times 10^{-4}$ m/s | Talmage–Fitch |
| Particle density | $\rho_s$ | 2400 – 2700 kg/m³ | Standard for CaCO₃ |
| Liquid density | $\rho_L$ | 1000 kg/m³ | Water |
| Computed thickener area | $A$ | 1 – 50 m² | Final answer |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Batch Sedimentation Test

The following Mermaid diagram maps the entire workflow of the experiment from slurry preparation to thickener area calculation, including the four zones that form inside the cylinder.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Step1: Prepare slurry of known concentration] --> B[Step2: Pour into graduated cylinder of known cross-section Ac]
    B --> C[Step3: Note initial slurry height H0 at time t = 0]
    C --> D[Step4: Start stopwatch and record mudline height H at fixed time intervals]
    D --> E[Step5: Continue recording until H becomes nearly constant]
    E --> F[Step6: Plot H versus t to obtain Kynch sedimentation curve]
    F --> G[Step7: Draw linear fit to initial straight portion to get u0]
    F --> H[Step8: Draw tangent from origin to curved portion to get critical point Hc, tc]
    G --> I[Step9: Compute hindered settling velocity u0 = delta H by delta t]
    H --> J[Step10: Compute critical settling velocity uc = H0 minus Hc divided by tc]
    I --> K[Step11: Compute total dry solids Ms = C0 x Ac x H0]
    J --> K
    K --> L[Step12: Apply Talmage Fitch formula for thickener area A]
    L --> M[Final Output: Cross sectional area A of continuous thickener in square meters]
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram of the Four Zones Formed Inside the Cylinder

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CYL[Graduated Sedimentation Cylinder]
        direction TB
        Z1[Zone A: Clear Liquid Supernatant]
        Z2[Zone B: Hindered Settling Zone at initial concentration C0]
        Z3[Zone C: Transition Zone where concentration increases]
        Z4[Zone D: Compression Zone dense sludge at bottom]
    end
    Z1 --- Z2 --- Z3 --- Z4
    INT1[Interface 1 between A and B: moves down linearly tracked mudline] -.-> Z1
    INT2[Interface 2 between B and C: rises upward as settled layer grows] -.-> Z3
    INT3[Interface 3 between C and D: marks start of compression] -.-> Z4
```

## 4.3 Schematic of the Talmage–Fitch Graphical Construction on the H–t Plot

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLOT[H versus t Kynch Plot]
        direction LR
        AXISY[Vertical axis: H in metres] --- ORIGIN[Origin 0,0]
        ORIGIN --- AXISX[Horizontal axis: t in seconds]
        CURVE[Kynch sedimentation curve: starts at H0 then drops linearly then curves and flattens] -.-> PLOT
        TANGENT[Tangent line drawn from origin to the curved portion of the plot] --- ORIGIN
        POINT[Point of tangency gives critical coordinates Hc and tc] -.-> TANGENT
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The tangent from the origin touches the curved part of the H–t plot at exactly the **critical point**. The coordinates of this tangency point $(t_c, H_c)$ are then used in the thickener area formula. This is the heart of the **Talmage–Fitch method (1955)**.

## 4.4 Functional Block Diagram — Scale-Up from Lab Cylinder to Industrial Thickener

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    LAB[Lab Batch Cylinder Volume 1 L] --> HtPlot[H versus t Kynch curve]
    HtPlot --> TF[Talmage Fitch graphical construction]
    TF --> Uc[Critical settling velocity uc]
    HtPlot --> Ms[Total dry solids Ms from initial data]
    Ms --> A[Thickener area formula]
    Uc --> A
    A --> IND[Industrial continuous thickener cross sectional area in square metres]
    IND --> DT[Diameter D = 2 x sqrt A by pi of continuous thickener]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 (Conceptual) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q:** Define **hindered settling**. How does it differ from **free settling**? State **Kynch's hypothesis** for hindered settling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Hindered settling is the gravity-driven settling of solid particles in a concentrated suspension where the motion of one particle is impeded by the presence of neighbouring particles. The settling velocity becomes a function of the local solids concentration rather than just the particle size.
- **Difference from free settling (1 mark):** In **free settling** (dilute, $\phi < 0.1\%$), particles settle independently and the velocity follows **Stokes' Law** $u = \frac{d^2(\rho_s - \rho_L)g}{18\mu}$. In **hindered settling** (concentrated, $\phi \approx 5\%-40\%$), $u$ depends on $C$ because each particle experiences additional upward drag from displaced particles.
- **Kynch's hypothesis (1 mark):** *"The settling velocity $u$ of a uniform suspension at any point depends only on the local solids concentration $C$ at that point, and not on the past history of the suspension."*

### Question 2 (Short derivation/definition) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q:** State the **four zones** that form during batch sedimentation of a slurry. What is the **mudline**?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Zone A — Clear liquid zone (supernatant):** Top region, completely solids-free. (0.75 marks)
- **Zone B — Initial settling zone (constant concentration $C_0$):** Slurry of uniform original concentration, all particles settle at the same hindered velocity. (0.75 marks)
- **Zone C — Transition zone:** Concentration rises from $C_0$ toward a higher value. (0.75 marks)
- **Zone D — Compression zone:** Dense sludge at bottom where particles are in contact and the bed compacts by slow liquid expulsion. (0.75 marks)
- **Mudline (bonus, 0 marks extra — included in above):** The visible **interface between Zone A and Zone B** is called the *mudline*; it is the line whose height vs time is recorded in the experiment.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Statement:** In a batch sedimentation experiment, a slurry of concentration **$C_0 = 180$ kg/m³** is placed in a graduated cylinder of **internal cross-section $A_c = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m²** to an initial height **$H_0 = 0.35$ m**. The mudline height $H$ is recorded as a function of time:

| $t$ (s) | 0 | 60 | 120 | 240 | 420 | 600 | 900 | 1500 | 2400 | 3600 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $H$ (m) | 0.350 | 0.330 | 0.310 | 0.270 | 0.220 | 0.180 | 0.140 | 0.110 | 0.090 | 0.082 |

The underflow concentration expected from the thickener is **$C_B = 720$ kg/m³** and the feed volumetric flow rate is **$F = 4.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s**. The particle density is $\rho_s = 2500$ kg/m³. Determine the **cross-sectional area** of the continuous thickener.

**(a) Plot the H vs t curve and determine the critical time $t_c$ and critical height $H_c$ using the Talmage–Fitch construction.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Calculate the cross-sectional area of the thickener.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Kynch plot and Talmage–Fitch construction (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Tabulate and plot the data:**

| $t$ (s) | $H$ (m) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 0.350 |
| 60 | 0.330 |
| 120 | 0.310 |
| 240 | 0.270 |
| 420 | 0.220 |
| 600 | 0.180 |
| 900 | 0.140 |
| 1500 | 0.110 |
| 2400 | 0.090 |
| 3600 | 0.082 |

The plot shows a steep linear drop from 0–240 s, then a curved transition, then near-flattening beyond ~2400 s.

**Step 2 (1 mark) — Initial linear portion (constant-concentration settling):**

The initial linear portion is from $t = 0$ to $t = 240$ s. The hindered settling velocity:

$$u_0 = \frac{H_0 - H(240)}{240 - 0} = \frac{0.350 - 0.270}{240} = \frac{0.080}{240} = 3.33 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 (2 marks) — Talmage–Fitch construction:**

Draw a straight line from the origin $(0, 0)$ tangent to the curved portion of the H–t plot. The point of tangency is at approximately $t_c \approx 600$ s, $H_c \approx 0.180$ m.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Critical settling velocity:**

$$u_c = \frac{H_0 - H_c}{t_c} = \frac{0.350 - 0.180}{600} = \frac{0.170}{600} = 2.83 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Total dry solids in cylinder:**

$$M_s = C_0 \cdot A_c \cdot H_0 = (180)(1.0 \times 10^{-3})(0.350) = 6.30 \times 10^{-2} \, \text{kg}$$

---

**Part (b) — Thickener area calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Mass fraction of solids in feed:**

$$X_F = \frac{C_F}{\rho_s} = \frac{180}{2500} = 0.0720$$

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Mass fraction of solids at the average point (under Talmage–Fitch):**

By mass balance across the thickener, the average mass fraction in the settling region:

$$X_{avg} = X_F \cdot \frac{H_0}{H_c} = 0.0720 \cdot \frac{0.350}{0.180} = 0.0720 \times 1.944 = 0.140$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Mass feed rate of solids:**

$$\dot{M}_s = F \cdot C_F = (4.0 \times 10^{-3})(180) = 0.720 \, \text{kg/s}$$

**Step 4 (2.5 marks) — Apply thickener area formula:**

$$A = \frac{\dot{M}_s}{u_c \cdot \rho_s \left(1 - \dfrac{X_{avg}}{X_F}\right)}$$

$$A = \frac{0.720}{(2.83 \times 10^{-4})(2500)\left(1 - \dfrac{0.140}{0.0720}\right)}$$

$$A = \frac{0.720}{(0.7075)\left(1 - 1.944\right)} = \frac{0.720}{(0.7075)(-0.944)}$$

$$\vert A \vert = \frac{0.720}{0.668} = 1.078 \, \text{m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> The negative sign in the denominator is expected because $X_{avg} > X_F$ (the underflow is *thicker* than the feed). Always report the area as a **positive magnitude**.

**Diameter of the circular thickener:**

$$D = \sqrt{\frac{4A}{\pi}} = \sqrt{\frac{4 \times 1.078}{\pi}} = \sqrt{1.373} = 1.172 \, \text{m}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Statement:** A sedimentation column test is conducted for a calcium carbonate slurry. The following data are obtained:

| $t$ (min) | 0 | 2 | 5 | 10 | 20 | 40 | 80 | 160 | 320 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $H$ (cm) | 50.0 | 47.0 | 43.0 | 37.0 | 28.0 | 18.0 | 11.0 | 8.0 | 7.0 |

The initial slurry concentration is $C_0 = 200$ kg/m³, the underflow concentration desired is $C_B = 1000$ kg/m³, the feed rate is $F = 5.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, and the particle density is $\rho_s = 2700$ kg/m³. The cross-sectional area of the lab cylinder is $A_c = 1.0 \times 10^{-3}$ m².

**(a) Explain Kynch's theory of sedimentation and describe the four zones formed in the column.** **(7 marks)**

**(b) Using the Talmage–Fitch method, determine the cross-sectional area required for the continuous thickener.** **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Kynch's theory and zone description (7 marks):**

**Kynch's theory (3.5 marks):**

- Kynch (1952) proposed that in a hindered-settling regime, the local settling velocity of a uniform suspension depends only on its **local concentration $C$** and not on its past history. Mathematically: $u = u(C)$.
- A mass balance on the solid phase over a thin horizontal slice of the column yields the **Kynch continuity equation**:

$$\frac{\partial C}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial}{\partial z}\left[C \cdot u(C)\right] = 0$$

- Solutions of this equation show that layers of constant concentration propagate **upward** through the column at a characteristic speed equal to the slope of the chord from the origin to the point on the flux-concentration curve.
- This theory forms the basis of the **Talmage–Fitch method** for designing continuous thickeners.

**Four zones (3.5 marks):**

- **Zone A (Clear liquid / supernatant):** Top region with no solids.
- **Zone B (Initial settling zone):** Constant concentration $C_0$, all particles fall at the same hindered velocity. The mudline moves down linearly with time.
- **Zone C (Transition zone):** Concentration increases from $C_0$ to a higher value $C_c$.
- **Zone D (Compression zone):** Sludge at the bottom where particles are in physical contact; further compaction is slow.

**Part (b) — Thickener area (7 marks):**

**Step 1 (1 mark) — Convert data and identify linear portion:**

Plot $H$ (in m) vs $t$ (in s). The data show a near-linear drop from $t = 0$ to $t = 20$ min.

**Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Hindered settling velocity:**

$$u_0 = \frac{0.50 - 0.28}{(20)(60)} = \frac{0.22}{1200} = 1.833 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 3 (1.5 marks) — Talmage–Fitch tangent construction:**

Drawing a tangent from the origin to the curved portion, the point of tangency is approximately at $t_c = 40$ min $= 2400$ s, $H_c = 0.18$ m.

**Step 4 (1.5 marks) — Critical settling velocity:**

$$u_c = \frac{H_0 - H_c}{t_c} = \frac{0.50 - 0.18}{2400} = \frac{0.32}{2400} = 1.333 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 (1.5 marks) — Area calculation:**

$$X_F = \frac{C_F}{\rho_s} = \frac{200}{2700} = 0.0741$$

$$X_{avg} = X_F \cdot \frac{H_0}{H_c} = 0.0741 \cdot \frac{0.50}{0.18} = 0.0741 \times 2.778 = 0.2058$$

$$\dot{M}_s = F \cdot C_F = (5.0 \times 10^{-3})(200) = 1.0 \, \text{kg/s}$$

$$A = \frac{1.0}{(1.333 \times 10^{-4})(2700)\left(1 - \dfrac{0.2058}{0.0741}\right)} = \frac{1.0}{(0.360)(-1.778)} = \frac{1.0}{0.640} = 1.563 \, \text{m}^2$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Take the magnitude.** $A = 1.563$ m². Diameter: $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi} = 1.41$ m.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common mistakes KTU students make (each = loss of 2–3 marks):**
> 1. **Drawing the tangent wrongly:** Many students draw the tangent to the *linear* part of the curve (which gives the *initial* velocity, not the *critical* one). The correct tangent goes from the **origin** to the **curved transition portion** of the H–t plot.
> 2. **Forgetting the negative sign in the area formula:** The denominator contains $(1 - X_{avg}/X_F)$ which is *negative* since $X_{avg} > X_F$ for a thickener. The area must be reported as a **positive magnitude** with units m².
> 3. **Mixing up $C_0$ and $C_F$:** $C_0$ is the *initial* batch concentration; $C_F$ is the *feed* concentration to the continuous thickener (often the same in lab scale-up). Do not confuse with $C_B$ (underflow).
> 4. **No unit consistency:** Always convert $H$ to metres and $t$ to **seconds** before computing $u_c$ (m/s). Marks deducted for mixed units (cm/s, m/min, etc.).
> 5. **Skipping the H vs t plot:** A sketch of the plot with axes labelled and the tangent drawn is worth **at least 3 marks** in the ESE. Never skip it.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (must memorize before exam):**
> - ☐ **Sedimentation** = gravity-driven solid–liquid separation. Four zones form: clear liquid, initial settling (constant $C_0$), transition, compression.
> - ☐ **Mudline** = the visible interface between the clear liquid and the slurry; this is the height we measure.
> - ☐ **Kynch's hypothesis:** $u = u(C)$ — settling velocity depends only on local concentration.
> - ☐ **Kynch continuity equation:** $\partial C/\partial t + \partial [C \cdot u(C)]/\partial z = 0$.
> - ☐ **Hindered settling velocity $u_0$:** slope of the *linear* portion of the H–t plot.
> - ☐ **Talmage–Fitch construction:** draw a **tangent from the origin** to the **curved** portion of the H–t plot; the point of tangency gives $(t_c, H_c)$.
> - ☐ **Critical settling velocity:** $u_c = (H_0 - H_c)/t_c$.
> - ☐ **Total dry solids in batch:** $M_s = C_0 \cdot A_c \cdot H_0$.
> - ☐ **Thickener area formula:** $A = \dfrac{\dot{M}_s}{u_c \cdot \rho_s \left(1 - X_{avg}/X_F\right)}$; report $\vert A \vert$ in m².
> - ☐ **Diameter of thickener:** $D = \sqrt{4A/\pi}$ in metres.
> - ☐ **Industrial applications:** mineral processing, wastewater, sugar, paper, cement.
> - ☐ **Always use SI units** (m, s, kg, m³) to avoid penalty.
> - ☐ **Plot the H–t graph** — at least 3 marks reserved for the sketch with labelled axes and tangent.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Heat transfer from steam to air: Determination of overall heat transfer coefficient
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Heat transfer from steam to air: Determination of overall heat transfer coefficient

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is defined as the proportionality constant that relates the **steady-state rate of heat transfer (Q)** between two fluids separated by a solid wall, to the **driving force (ΔT_lm)** and the **effective heat transfer area (A)**. It is the inverse of the total thermal resistance offered by the convective boundary layers on both sides of the wall and the conductive resistance of the wall itself.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus-Highlight Definition (KTU 2024):**
> The Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient is the empirical parameter that lumps together the individual resistances (convective + conductive) encountered by heat as it moves from the hot fluid to the cold fluid. In the **steam-to-air** experiment, heat flows from condensing steam (shell side) → through the metal tube wall → to the flowing air (tube side).

**Mathematical Statement:**

$$Q \;=\; U \, A \, \Delta T_{lm}$$

Where:
- **Q** = Rate of heat transfer [W]
- **U** = Overall heat transfer coefficient [W·m⁻²·K⁻¹]
- **A** = Effective heat transfer area [m²]
- **ΔT_lm** = Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference [K or °C]

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Plain-English Analogy: The "Bus Conductor" of Heat**
> Imagine a crowded bus (heat) trying to move from the front door (steam) to the back door (air). Three things slow it down:
> 1. **The mob pushing at the front door** (steam-side condensation resistance, $1/h_o$)
> 2. **The narrow aisle inside the bus** (metal wall conduction resistance, $x/k$)
> 3. **The crowd squeezing out the back door** (air-side convection resistance, $1/h_i$)
> The **Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)** is like the **overall "ease of movement"** of passengers through the entire bus. A low U means a very crowded, slow transfer; a high U means a smooth, fast transfer.

**Physical constants and standard metrics (bolded):**
- Latent heat of condensation of steam at **100 °C**: **λ_steam ≈ 2257 kJ·kg⁻¹**
- Specific heat capacity of air: **C_p,air ≈ 1.005 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- Density of air at NTP: **ρ_air ≈ 1.2 kg·m⁻³**
- Thermal conductivity of copper tube: **k_Cu ≈ 384 W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- Typical experimental value of U (steam → air, finned/unsteady system): **20 – 200 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Physical Mechanism of Heat Flow

In the steam-to-air heat exchanger, three distinct thermal resistances act **in series**:

| Step | Mechanism | Location | Governing Law |
|------|-----------|----------|---------------|
| 1 | **Convective heat transfer from condensing steam** | Outside of tube | Newton's law of cooling: $q = h_o \Delta T$ |
| 2 | **Conductive heat transfer through tube wall** | Metal wall | Fourier's law: $q = (k/x) \Delta T$ |
| 3 | **Convective heat transfer to flowing air** | Inside of tube | Newton's law of cooling: $q = h_i \Delta T$ |

> [!TIP]
> **Why the resistances add up (not the coefficients):**
> Because heat flows **sequentially** through each layer, the temperature drops are additive. Therefore the **reciprocals of coefficients** (i.e., resistances) are additive — analogous to electrical resistors in series.

## 2.2 The Resistance-in-Series Concept

The overall heat transfer coefficient for a **clean, thin-walled tube** is given by:

$$\frac{1}{U} \;=\; \frac{1}{h_i} \;+\; \frac{x_w}{k_w} \;+\; \frac{1}{h_o}$$

Where:
- $h_i$ = inside (air-side) convective heat transfer coefficient [W·m⁻²·K⁻¹]
- $h_o$ = outside (steam-side) condensation heat transfer coefficient [W·m⁻²·K⁻¹]
- $x_w$ = thickness of the tube wall [m]
- $k_w$ = thermal conductivity of wall material [W·m⁻¹·K⁻¹]

## 2.3 LMTD Concept

Because the temperature of **air rises continuously** from inlet ($T_1$) to outlet ($T_2$), while **steam remains at saturation temperature** ($T_s$) throughout (since condensation is isothermal at constant pressure), the temperature difference across the heat exchanger is **not constant**. An arithmetic mean would be inaccurate. Hence, the **Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)** is used.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following table must be memorized verbatim for KTU exams. The 14-mark numerical problems are solved using these exact equations.**

| # | Equation | Symbol Description | Typical Units |
|---|----------|--------------------|----------------|
| 1 | $Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$ | Fundamental heat transfer equation | W |
| 2 | $Q_{air} = m_a C_{p,a} (T_2 - T_1)$ | Heat gained by air | W |
| 3 | $m_a = \rho_a \cdot V_a$ | Mass flow rate of air | kg·s⁻¹ |
| 4 | $Q_{steam} = m_s \lambda_s$ | Heat released by condensing steam | W |
| 5 | $m_s = \dfrac{W}{t}$ | Steam condensation rate | kg·s⁻¹ |
| 6 | $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$ | Log-mean temperature difference | K |
| 7 | $\Delta T_1 = T_s - T_1$ | Temperature diff. at air inlet end | K |
| 8 | $\Delta T_2 = T_s - T_2$ | Temperature diff. at air outlet end | K |
| 9 | $A = \pi d L$ | Heat transfer area (cylindrical tube) | m² |
| 10 | $V_a = C_d \, a \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \, \Delta h}{\rho_a}}$ | Orifice-meter air flow rate | m³·s⁻¹ |
| 11 | $\dfrac{1}{U} = \dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}$ | Resistance-in-series model | m²·K·W⁻¹ |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

> [!NOTE]
> **Where this experiment matters in industry:**
> - **Design of air pre-heaters** in boilers and HVAC systems
> - **Fin-fan coolers** in refineries and chemical plants
> - **Process air heating** in drying operations (spray dryers, fluidized bed dryers)
> - **Economizers** in power plants (flue gas → feed water)
> - **Validation of commercial heat exchanger design** software (HTRI, ASPEN)

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)

Consider a **counter-flow** heat exchanger where:
- Hot fluid enters at $T_{h,in}$ and leaves at $T_{h,out}$
- Cold fluid enters at $T_{c,in}$ and leaves at $T_{c,out}$

**Step 1:** Consider a differential area $\mathrm{d}A$ across which the temperature difference is $\Delta T$.

$$\mathrm{d}Q = U \, \mathrm{d}A \, \Delta T \quad \cdots (i)$$

**Step 2:** Apply energy balance on the hot and cold fluids for that differential area.

$$\mathrm{d}Q = -m_h C_{p,h} \, \mathrm{d}T_h \quad \cdots (ii)$$

$$\mathrm{d}Q = m_c C_{p,c} \, \mathrm{d}T_c \quad \cdots (iii)$$

**Step 3:** The differential change in the temperature difference (for counter-flow) is:

$$\mathrm{d}(\Delta T) = \mathrm{d}T_c - \mathrm{d}T_h \quad \cdots (iv)$$

**Step 4:** Substitute (ii) and (iii) into (iv):

$$\mathrm{d}(\Delta T) = \frac{\mathrm{d}Q}{m_c C_{p,c}} - \left(-\frac{\mathrm{d}Q}{m_h C_{p,h}}\right)$$

$$\mathrm{d}(\Delta T) = \mathrm{d}Q \left[\frac{1}{m_c C_{p,c}} + \frac{1}{m_h C_{p,h}}\right] \quad \cdots (v)$$

**Step 5:** From (i), $\mathrm{d}Q = U \Delta T \, \mathrm{d}A$, so $\mathrm{d}A = \dfrac{\mathrm{d}Q}{U \Delta T}$.

Substituting into (v) and rearranging:

$$\frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = U \left[\frac{1}{m_c C_{p,c}} + \frac{1}{m_h C_{p,h}}\right] \mathrm{d}A$$

**Step 6:** Integrate from $0$ to $A$, with $\Delta T$ going from $\Delta T_1$ (inlet) to $\Delta T_2$ (outlet):

$$\int_{\Delta T_1}^{\Delta T_2} \frac{\mathrm{d}(\Delta T)}{\Delta T} = U \left[\frac{1}{m_c C_{p,c}} + \frac{1}{m_h C_{p,h}}\right] \int_{0}^{A} \mathrm{d}A$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = U A \left[\frac{1}{m_c C_{p,c}} + \frac{1}{m_h C_{p,h}}\right]$$

**Step 7:** From energy balance, $Q = m_c C_{p,c} (T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = m_h C_{p,h} (T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$. Therefore:

$$\frac{1}{m_c C_{p,c}} + \frac{1}{m_h C_{p,h}} = \frac{T_{c,out} - T_{c,in} + T_{h,in} - T_{h,out}}{Q} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{Q}$$

**Step 8:** Substitute this back:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{\Delta T_2}{\Delta T_1}\right) = \frac{U A (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{Q}$$

$$\therefore \quad Q = \frac{U A (\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2)}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)} \;=\; U A \Delta T_{lm}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta T_{lm} \;=\; \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}\;}$$

## 3.2 Derivation of the Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (Resistance Model)

Consider a composite wall separating steam (hot) and air (cold), with three layers:
1. Steam-side convective film (resistance $R_o$)
2. Tube wall conduction (resistance $R_w$)
3. Air-side convective film (resistance $R_i$)

**Step 1:** Apply Newton's law of cooling on each face:

$$q = h_i (T_{w,i} - T_{c}) = h_o (T_{h} - T_{w,o}) = \frac{k_w}{x_w}(T_{w,o} - T_{w,i})$$

**Step 2:** By the principle of conservation of energy in steady state, the heat flux $q$ is the same through each layer. Hence:

$$T_h - T_c = q \left[\frac{1}{h_o} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_i}\right]$$

**Step 3:** By definition, the overall heat transfer coefficient is $q = U (T_h - T_c)$. Therefore:

$$U = \frac{1}{\dfrac{1}{h_i} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_o}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\frac{1}{U} = \frac{1}{h_i} + \frac{x_w}{k_w} + \frac{1}{h_o}\;}$$

## 3.3 Fully Solved Numerical Problem (Worked Example)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard KTU numerical problem format** — this exact pattern appears in almost every 14-mark question on this experiment.

### Given Data

| Parameter | Symbol | Value |
|-----------|--------|-------|
| Air inlet temperature | $T_1$ | 30 °C |
| Air outlet temperature | $T_2$ | 65 °C |
| Steam (saturation) temperature | $T_s$ | 100 °C |
| Air volumetric flow rate | $V_a$ | 60 m³·h⁻¹ |
| Density of air | $\rho_a$ | 1.2 kg·m⁻³ |
| Specific heat of air | $C_{p,a}$ | 1.005 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| Condensate collected in 4 min | $W$ | 0.4 kg |
| Latent heat of steam | $\lambda_s$ | 2257 kJ·kg⁻¹ |
| Tube inner diameter | $d$ | 0.025 m |
| Tube length | $L$ | 1.5 m |

### Solution — Step by Step

**Step A: Mass flow rate of air**

$$m_a = \rho_a \cdot V_a = 1.2 \times \frac{60}{3600} = 1.2 \times 0.01667 = 0.0200 \;\text{kg·s}^{-1}$$

**Step B: Heat gained by air (Q_air)**

$$Q_{air} = m_a C_{p,a}(T_2 - T_1) = 0.0200 \times 1005 \times (65 - 30)$$

$$Q_{air} = 0.0200 \times 1005 \times 35 = 703.5 \;\text{W} \quad \text{(or } 0.7035 \;\text{kW)}$$

**Step C: Heat released by steam (Q_steam) — for energy balance check**

$$m_s = \frac{W}{t} = \frac{0.4}{4 \times 60} = \frac{0.4}{240} = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg·s}^{-1}$$

$$Q_{steam} = m_s \cdot \lambda_s = 1.667 \times 10^{-3} \times 2257 \times 10^{3} = 3762 \;\text{W}$$

> Heat loss to surroundings = $Q_{steam} - Q_{air} = 3762 - 703.5 = 3058.5$ W
> **% Heat loss** $= (3058.5 / 3762) \times 100 \approx 81.3\%$ *(typical for lab-scale rigs)*

**Step D: Log Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)**

$$\Delta T_1 = T_s - T_1 = 100 - 30 = 70 \;\text{°C}$$

$$\Delta T_2 = T_s - T_2 = 100 - 65 = 35 \;\text{°C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{70 - 35}{\ln(70 / 35)} = \frac{35}{\ln 2} = \frac{35}{0.6931} = 50.50 \;\text{°C}$$

**Step E: Heat transfer area**

$$A = \pi \, d \, L = \pi \times 0.025 \times 1.5 = 0.1178 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step F: Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (using Q_air — air-side basis)**

$$U = \frac{Q_{air}}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}} = \frac{703.5}{0.1178 \times 50.50}$$

$$\boxed{\;U = \frac{703.5}{5.949} = 118.3 \;\text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-1}\;}$$

**Step G: Result interpretation**

The obtained $U \approx 118 \;\text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-1}$ is **physically realistic** for a shell-and-tube exchanger heating air with steam. The low value (compared to liquid-liquid systems) is due to the **poor convective heat transfer coefficient of air** ($h_i \approx 20$–$50$ W·m⁻²·K⁻¹).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Flow Diagram of the Experimental Setup

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The following Mermaid diagram is a faithful representation of the typical KTU laboratory rig. The IDs are deliberately chosen to be descriptive and non-reserved.**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SteamLoop[STEAM SIDE CIRCUIT]
        boiler[Steam Boiler with Pressure Gauge] --> valve1[Steam Control Valve]
        valve1 --> inletT[Steam Inlet Temperature Sensor Ts]
        inletT --> shellTop[Shell Side Inlet of Heat Exchanger]
        shellTop --> shellMid[Shell Chamber Condensing Zone]
        shellMid --> shellBot[Condensate Outlet]
        shellBot --> drainValve[Drain Valve]
        drainValve --> tank[Condensate Collection Measuring Cylinder]
    end

    subgraph AirLoop[AIR SIDE CIRCUIT]
        atm[Atmospheric Air Inlet] --> blower[Centrifugal Air Blower]
        blower --> orifice[Orifice Plate with U-tube Manometer]
        orifice --> tubeIn[Tube Inlet Temperature T1]
        tubeIn --> tubeMid[Tube Chamber Heating Zone]
        tubeMid --> tubeOut[Tube Outlet Temperature T2]
        tubeOut --> exhaust[Discharge to Atmosphere]
    end

    shellMid -- Heat Transfer through Tube Wall --> tubeMid
    tubeMid -- Hot Air Discharge --> exhaust
```

## 4.2 Component-Level Block Diagram (Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[Boiler] --> A2[Steam Line]
    A2 --> A3[Pressure Indicator]
    A2 --> A4[HE Shell Side]
    A4 --> A5[Condensate Line]
    A5 --> A6[Tank with Stopwatch]
    B1[Blower] --> B2[Orifice Assembly]
    B2 --> B3[Manometer dh]
    B2 --> B4[HE Tube Side]
    B4 --> B5[Inlet Thermocouple T1]
    B4 --> B6[Outlet Thermocouple T2]
    A4 -- Latent Heat Release --> B4
    B4 -- Sensible Heat Gain --> B7[Exhaust]
    C1[Data Acquisition] --- A3
    C1 --- A6
    C1 --- B3
    C1 --- B5
    C1 --- B6
```

## 4.3 Thermal Resistance Network (Electrical Analogy)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Tsteam[Steam Temp Ts] --> R1[Resistance 1/h_o]
    R1 --> Rw[Wall Resistance x_w / k_w]
    Rw --> R2[Resistance 1/h_i]
    R2 --> Tair[Air Temp T]
    Tsteam --- Tsteam
    Tair --- Tair
    Qflux[Heat Flux q flows from Ts to T] -.-> Tsteam
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- Total resistance $R_{total} = \dfrac{1}{h_o} + \dfrac{x_w}{k_w} + \dfrac{1}{h_i}$
- Heat flux $q = \dfrac{T_s - T_{air}}{R_{total}} = U (T_s - T_{air})$
- The **largest resistance dominates** the overall $U$ (in air systems, $1/h_i$ is largest).

```mermaid
    end

        TT2[Air Inlet T1] --- TT3[Air Outlet T2]
    end

```
