# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]**
**Define the term "Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U)". State its SI unit. What does a high value of U indicate about the heat exchanger?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The Overall Heat Transfer Coefficient (U) is defined as the **ratio of the heat flux** to the **overall temperature difference** between the hot and cold fluids. It is the reciprocal of the total thermal resistance between the two fluids.

$$U = \frac{Q}{A \cdot \Delta T_{lm}}$$

- **SI unit:** W·m⁻²·K⁻¹ (Watt per square meter per Kelvin)
- **A high U** indicates **low total thermal resistance**, meaning the heat exchanger is **efficient** in transferring heat between the fluids. Typical values: liquid-liquid exchangers ~500–2000 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹; gas-liquid exchangers (like steam-air) ~20–200 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**
**What is meant by Logarithmic Mean Temperature Difference (LMTD)? Why is it preferred over Arithmetic Mean Temperature Difference (AMTD) in heat exchanger calculations?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
LMTD is the **logarithmically averaged temperature difference** between the hot and cold fluids across the heat exchanger, given by:

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$$

**Why preferred over AMTD:**
- In a heat exchanger, the temperature difference **varies exponentially** (not linearly) from one end to the other because the fluids exchange heat continuously.
- The arithmetic mean gives **incorrect (overestimated)** values, especially when the inlet and outlet temperature differences are widely different (e.g., $\Delta T_1 = 70$, $\Delta T_2 = 35$).
- LMTD is derived from the **exact integration** of the differential heat transfer equation, making it mathematically rigorous and physically accurate.
- **Example:** For the worked numerical above, AMTD = 52.5 °C vs. LMTD = 50.5 °C — the error of ~4% grows larger as $\Delta T_1/\Delta T_2$ deviates from unity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Unit inconsistency** (mixing kJ·h⁻¹ and W) — the #1 reason students lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting to convert** volumetric flow from m³·h⁻¹ to m³·s⁻¹.
> 3. **Using T_steam as variable** instead of saturation temperature at given pressure.
> 4. **Forgetting the heat balance check** (Q_air vs. Q_steam) — examiners award a mark specifically for this.
> 5. **Calculating AMTD by mistake** — silently lose 2 marks.

---

### **Part B — Question A (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 1]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, describe the **experimental setup and procedure** to determine the overall heat transfer coefficient between steam and air in a shell-and-tube heat exchanger. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** In an experiment, the following observations were recorded. Calculate the **overall heat transfer coefficient (U)** and the **percentage heat loss**. **(7 marks)**

| Observation | Value |
|-------------|-------|
| Air inlet temperature, $T_1$ | 32 °C |
| Air outlet temperature, $T_2$ | 70 °C |
| Steam (saturation) temperature, $T_s$ | 99.6 °C |
| Air flow rate (orifice), $V_a$ | 75 m³·h⁻¹ |
| Density of air | 1.18 kg·m⁻³ |
| Specific heat of air | 1.005 kJ·kg⁻¹·K⁻¹ |
| Latent heat of steam | 2258 kJ·kg⁻¹ |
| Condensate collected | 0.55 kg in 5 min |
| Tube inner diameter | 0.028 m |
| Tube length | 1.6 m |

#### Model Solution (Question A)

**(a) Apparatus & Procedure (7 marks):**

**Apparatus:** A horizontal **shell-and-tube heat exchanger** with steam on the shell side and air on the tube side, an **electric steam boiler** with pressure gauge, an **air blower**, a calibrated **orifice meter** with U-tube manometer, **mercury thermometers / thermocouples** at steam inlet, air inlet, and air outlet, a **condensate collection cylinder**, and a stopwatch.

**Procedure:**
1. Start the steam boiler; allow steam to flow through the shell side until steady saturation temperature is reached.
2. Switch on the air blower; set a desired flow rate using the bypass / control valve.
3. Wait until steady-state conditions are established (constant $T_1$ and $T_2$ for ~5 min).
4. Record: $T_s$, $T_1$, $T_2$, manometer reading $\Delta h$, and start the stopwatch.
5. Collect condensate in the measuring cylinder for a fixed time interval (say 4–5 min).
6. Repeat for 3–4 different air flow rates.

**Calculations:**
- $m_a = \rho_a \cdot V_a$ ; $V_a = C_d \cdot a_o \sqrt{2 g \Delta h / \rho_a}$
- $Q_{air} = m_a C_{p,a} (T_2 - T_1)$
- $\Delta T_{lm} = (ΔT_1 - ΔT_2) / \ln(ΔT_1/ΔT_2)$ with $ΔT_1 = T_s - T_1$, $ΔT_2 = T_s - T_2$
- $A = \pi d L$
- $U = Q_{air} / (A \cdot \Delta T_{lm})$

**(b) Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Air mass flow rate:**

$$m_a = 1.18 \times \frac{75}{3600} = 1.18 \times 0.02083 = 0.02458 \;\text{kg·s}^{-1}$$

**Step 2 — Heat gained by air:**

$$Q_{air} = 0.02458 \times 1005 \times (70 - 32) = 0.02458 \times 1005 \times 38 = 938.7 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 3 — Steam condensation rate & heat released:**

$$m_s = \frac{0.55}{5 \times 60} = \frac{0.55}{300} = 1.833 \times 10^{-3} \;\text{kg·s}^{-1}$$

$$Q_{steam} = 1.833 \times 10^{-3} \times 2258 \times 10^{3} = 4139 \;\text{W}$$

**Step 4 — Percentage heat loss:**

$$\% \text{ loss} = \frac{Q_{steam} - Q_{air}}{Q_{steam}} \times 100 = \frac{4139 - 938.7}{4139} \times 100 = 77.3\%$$

**Step 5 — LMTD:**

$$\Delta T_1 = 99.6 - 32 = 67.6 \;\text{°C}, \quad \Delta T_2 = 99.6 - 70 = 29.6 \;\text{°C}$$

$$\Delta T_{lm} = \frac{67.6 - 29.6}{\ln(67.6/29.6)} = \frac{38.0}{\ln 2.284} = \frac{38.0}{0.8255} = 46.03 \;\text{°C}$$

**Step 6 — Heat transfer area:**

$$A = \pi \times 0.028 \times 1.6 = 0.1407 \;\text{m}^2$$

**Step 7 — Overall heat transfer coefficient:**

$$U = \frac{938.7}{0.1407 \times 46.03} = \frac{938.7}{6.477} \approx \boxed{144.9 \;\text{W·m}^{-2}\text{·K}^{-1}}$$

---

### **Part B — Question B (14 Marks) [Internal Choice Option 2]**

**(a)** Starting from the basic heat transfer equation, **derive the expression for LMTD** in a counter-flow heat exchanger. Show all the integration steps. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **sources of error** in the steam-to-air heat transfer experiment. Suggest **practical modifications** to improve the accuracy of the determined value of U. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution (Question B)

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**
*(See Section 3.1 of these notes for the full step-by-step derivation. Key steps to write in the exam:)*

1. Start with $\mathrm{d}Q = U \, \mathrm{d}A \, \Delta T$
2. Energy balance: $\mathrm{d}Q = -m_h C_{p,h} \, \mathrm{d}T_h = m_c C_{p,c} \, \mathrm{d}T_c$
3. For counter-flow: $\mathrm{d}(\Delta T) = \mathrm{d}T_c - \mathrm{d}T_h = \mathrm{d}Q \left[\dfrac{1}{m_c C_{p,c}} + \dfrac{1}{m_h C_{p,h}}\right]$
4. Combine and integrate from $0$ to $A$ and $\Delta T_1$ to $\Delta T_2$.
5. Use $Q = m_c C_{p,c}(T_{c,out} - T_{c,in}) = m_h C_{p,h}(T_{h,in} - T_{h,out})$
6. Final result: $\Delta T_{lm} = \dfrac{\Delta T_1 - \Delta T_2}{\ln(\Delta T_1 / \Delta T_2)}$

**(b) Sources of Error & Improvements (7 marks):**

| Source of Error | Effect | Suggested Improvement |
|-----------------|--------|------------------------|
| **Heat loss to surroundings** from uninsulated shell | $Q_{steam} > Q_{air}$ → underestimation of U | Use **glass-wool / ceramic-wool lagging** on entire shell |
| **Inaccurate temperature measurement** at inlets/outlets | Direct error in $\Delta T_{lm}$ | Use **calibrated thermocouples** with ice-bath cold-junction compensation |
| **Inaccurate air flow rate** (orifice calibration) | Direct error in $m_a$ and hence Q | Calibrate the orifice meter using a **standard rotameter** |
| **Wet steam** (entrained water droplets) | Erroneous $Q_{steam}$ | Install a **steam separator / moisture trap** upstream |
| **Subcooling of condensate** below $T_s$ | Increases apparent $Q_{steam}$ | Measure condensate temperature; subtract $m_s C_{p,water}(T_s - T_{cond})$ |
| **Air leakage** from joints | Reduces actual $m_a$ | Check all flanges; use **gasket sealants** |
| **Neglecting variation in $C_{p,air}$ with temperature** | Small error | Use temperature-averaged $C_{p,air}$ |
| **Reading errors** in manometer $\Delta h$ | Error in $V_a$ | Use **inclined manometer** for low pressure drops |
| **Steady-state assumption violation** | Fluctuating readings | Allow longer stabilization time (≥10 min) |

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist (last-minute KTU prep):**

- ✅ The governing equation is **$Q = U A \Delta T_{lm}$** — write this FIRST on the answer sheet.
- ✅ For steam, $T_s$ = **saturation temperature at the given boiler pressure** (look up steam tables if pressure ≠ 1 atm).
- ✅ Always do a **heat balance check**: $Q_{air}$ should be ≤ $Q_{steam}$; the difference is the **heat loss to surroundings**.
- ✅ $\Delta T_{lm}$ requires $\Delta T_1 \neq \Delta T_2$. If they are equal, $\Delta T_{lm} = \Delta T_1 = \Delta T_2$.
- ✅ The **air-side resistance dominates** the overall resistance because $h_i$ (air) << $h_o$ (condensing steam). Hence $U \approx h_i$.
- ✅ Tube area is computed using the **inner diameter** (because $A$ refers to the heat transfer area corresponding to $h_i$).
- ✅ Condensate collection: **convert time to seconds** before computing $m_s$ in kg·s⁻¹.
- ✅ Units of $U$ in KTU answers: always **W·m⁻²·K⁻¹** (or equivalently W·m⁻²·°C⁻¹, since the difference is just an interval).
- ✅ Expected $U$ for steam-air systems: **50 – 200 W·m⁻²·K⁻¹**. If you get $U > 1000$, recheck your calculations — there's a unit error.
- ✅ **LMTD > AMTD** is false in general. When $\Delta T_1$ and $\Delta T_2$ are nearly equal, LMTD ≈ AMTD. LMTD < AMTD otherwise.
- ✅ The experiment is **steady-state and isobaric**; do not confuse it with transient heat transfer.
- ✅ The orifice-meter flow equation: $V_a = C_d \, a \, \sqrt{2 g \Delta h / \rho_a}$ where $\Delta h$ is the manometric height in meters of water (or mercury — convert appropriately).
- ✅ If $T_1 = T_2$ (no temperature rise), $Q = 0$ → meaningless experiment. Always check that $T_2 > T_1$ by at least 10–15 °C.
- ✅ For graph plotting (if asked): plot $U$ vs. air mass flow rate $m_a$ — it should show an **increasing trend** (turbulent flow enhances $h_i$).

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Verification of material balance equation and Rayleigh's equation for simple distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Verification of material balance equation and Rayleigh's equation for simple distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE EXPERIMENT 2: VERIFICATION OF MATERIAL BALANCE AND RAYLEIGH'S EQUATION FOR SIMPLE DISTILLATION

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definitions (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Simple Distillation (Batch / Differential Distillation):**
> A physical separation process in which a liquid mixture is partially vaporized in a still (pot), the generated vapor is immediately condensed and collected as the distillate, and the residual liquid becomes progressively richer in the less-volatile component. It is an unsteady-state operation governed by instantaneous equilibrium between the vapor leaving and the liquid remaining.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Balance Equation (Overall):**
> A statement of conservation of mass applied to the distillation pot. For a binary system at any instant during the run, the moles of feed (or charge) equal the moles of distillate plus the moles of residue:
> **F = D + W** (overall), and **F·xF = D·xD + W·xW** (component-wise).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rayleigh's Equation (Differential Distillation Equation):**
> A differential form of the material balance that relates the change in liquid composition to the amount distilled. It assumes equilibrium between the escaping vapor and the remaining liquid and negligible hold-up of vapor in the system:
> **dL / L = dX / (Y − X)** or equivalently **ln(L₀/L) = ∫ (dX / (Y − X))**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **The Boiling-Pot Analogy (Real-World Picture):**
> Imagine boiling a large pot of fermented sugarcane juice (like making country liquor at home). As steam escapes, the liquid in the pot loses alcohol faster than water. The first vapors you collect are highly alcoholic; as you keep boiling, the vapor becomes weaker. The pot becomes progressively more watery. Rayleigh's equation is essentially the mathematical diary of *how fast* the alcohol in the pot disappears as steam leaves. The material balance is simply the "ledger" — what comes in must equal what leaves plus what stays behind.

### 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Metrics

- **Standard atmospheric pressure**: **P = 101.325 kPa = 760 mm Hg = 1 atm**
- **Refractive index** is the primary analytical property used to determine composition (e.g., **nD²⁰** for ethanol–water or methanol–water systems).
- **Relative volatility (α)**: a **dimensionless** number > 1; for ethanol–water, α ≈ **2.47** at the azeotropic-region; for methanol–water, α ≈ **3.89** at low concentrations.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle (Structured Logic Steps)

1. **Charging the Still**: A measured quantity L₀ of binary liquid mixture with mole fraction x₀ of the more-volatile component (MVC) is charged into the round-bottom flask.
2. **Heating**: Heat is supplied uniformly until the mixture reaches its bubble point at the operating pressure.
3. **Vapor Generation & Escape**: A small differential amount of vapor dV, in equilibrium with the liquid of composition X, escapes the pot and is immediately condensed.
4. **Liquid Composition Shifts**: Because the vapor is richer in MVC, the residual liquid becomes leaner; X decreases continuously.
5. **Condensate Collection**: Successive small condensate samples are collected at regular intervals and analyzed.
6. **Material Balance Check**: Total moles out (distillate) plus moles remaining (residue) must equal moles originally charged.
7. **Rayleigh Verification**: The experimental composition profile is compared with the theoretical profile predicted by integrating Rayleigh's equation.

### 2.2 Why and How Each Step Matters

- **Why differential?** Simple distillation is *not* a single-stage equilibrium flash with a fixed vapor–liquid split; it is a continuous, time-varying process — hence the differential form.
- **Why instantaneous equilibrium?** Rayleigh's equation assumes the vapor leaving at any instant is in thermodynamic equilibrium with the liquid remaining. This is valid because the pot is well-mixed and the vapor generation rate is slow relative to mass-transfer rates.
- **Why negligible vapor hold-up?** If vapor accumulates in the apparatus, the operating line shifts. In well-designed lab apparatus, this assumption holds to within **± 2 %** error.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 2.1 — High-Yield Formula Reference for Simple Distillation**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | F = D + W | Overall material balance | kmol or mol |
| 2 | F·xF = D·xD + W·xW | Component (MVC) balance | kmol |
| 3 | y = α·x / [1 + (α − 1)·x] | Equilibrium relation (constant α) | dimensionless |
| 4 | dL / L = dX / (Y − X) | Differential form of Rayleigh | dimensionless |
| 5 | ln(L₀ / L) = ∫ (dX / (Y − X)) | Integrated Rayleigh (general form) | dimensionless |
| 6 | ln(L₀ / L) = [1/(α − 1)] · [ln(x₀/x) + α · ln((1 − x)/(1 − x₀))] | Closed-form Rayleigh for constant α | dimensionless |
| 7 | xD,avg = (F·xF − W·xW) / (F − W) | Average distillate composition (back-calculated) | mole fraction |
| 8 | % Recovery = (D · xD) / (F · xF) × 100 | MVC recovery in distillate | % |
| 9 | α = (y/x) / ((1 − y)/(1 − x)) | Definition of relative volatility | dimensionless |
| 10 | x = (nD²⁰ − nW) / (nA − nW) | Composition from refractive-index calibration | mole fraction |

**Boundary conditions for integration limits in Equation 5:**
- Lower limit: X = xW (final residue composition)
- Upper limit: X = xF = x₀ (initial charge composition)
- The integral **must** be evaluated from the *final* state (residue) to the *initial* state (charge).

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceutical industry**: Recovery of solvents from reaction mixtures.
- **Petroleum refining**: Crude oil topping units (atmospheric distillation).
- **Beverage industry**: Concentration of alcohol in spirits (whisky, rum).
- **Aromatic/perfume industry**: Isolation of essential oils.
- **CKD/environmental engineering**: Recovery of methanol from biodiesel wash streams.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Material Balance

Consider a binary still initially charged with **F** moles of mixture of overall MVC mole fraction **xF**.

Let **D** = moles of distillate collected, with average MVC mole fraction **xD**.
Let **W** = moles of residue left in the pot, with MVC mole fraction **xW**.

**Step 1 — Total mass balance (overall):**

$$F = D + W$$

**Step 2 — Component (MVC) balance:**

$$F \cdot x_{F} = D \cdot x_{D} + W \cdot x_{W}$$

**Step 3 — Expressing distillate composition from the balance (back-calculation):**

$$x_{D} = \frac{F \cdot x_{F} - W \cdot x_{W}}{F - W} = \frac{F \cdot x_{F} - W \cdot x_{W}}{D}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Logic:** Equation 1 is conservation of moles; Equation 2 is conservation of the more-volatile species. Subtracting or rearranging gives the *average* distillate composition, which can be compared with the experimentally measured refractive index of the pooled distillate.

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Rayleigh's Equation (Differential Form)

Consider an instant when the pot holds **L** moles of liquid of MVC mole fraction **X**.

A small amount **dV** of vapor in equilibrium with this liquid (composition Y) escapes and condenses, leaving **dL = −dV** moles of liquid behind with composition **X + dX**.

**Step 1 — Overall differential material balance:**

$$L = (L + dL) + dV \quad \Rightarrow \quad dV = -dL$$

**Step 2 — Component (MVC) differential balance:**

$$L \cdot X = (L + dL)(X + dX) + dV \cdot Y$$

**Step 3 — Expanding the right-hand side and dropping the second-order differential (dL · dX ≈ 0):**

$$L \cdot X = L \cdot X + L \cdot dX + X \cdot dL + Y \cdot dV$$

**Step 4 — Cancel L·X from both sides and substitute dV = −dL:**

$$0 = L \cdot dX + X \cdot dL - Y \cdot dL$$

**Step 5 — Rearranging:**

$$L \cdot dX = (Y - X) \cdot dL$$

**Step 6 — Separating variables:**

$$\frac{dL}{L} = \frac{dX}{Y - X}$$

**Step 7 — Integrating between initial state (L₀, x₀) and final state (L, x):**

$$\int_{L_0}^{L} \frac{dL}{L} = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dX}{Y - X}$$

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L}{L_0}\right) = \int_{x_0}^{x} \frac{dX}{Y - X}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Standard Form** (writing the limits from residue to charge):

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x_{W}}^{x_{F}} \frac{dX}{Y - X}\;}$$

where L₀ = F (initial charge), L = W (final residue).

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Closed-Form Rayleigh Equation (Constant Relative Volatility)

For a binary system with **constant α**, the equilibrium relation is:

$$Y = \frac{\alpha \cdot X}{1 + (\alpha - 1)\cdot X}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute into the Rayleigh integral:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x}^{x_0} \frac{dX}{\dfrac{\alpha X}{1 + (\alpha - 1)X} - X}$$

**Step 2 — Simplify the denominator:**

$$\frac{\alpha X}{1 + (\alpha - 1)X} - X = \frac{\alpha X - X\!\left[1 + (\alpha - 1)X\right]}{1 + (\alpha - 1)X} = \frac{X(\alpha - 1)(1 - X)}{1 + (\alpha - 1)X}$$

**Step 3 — Therefore the integrand becomes:**

$$\frac{dX}{Y - X} = \frac{1 + (\alpha - 1)X}{X(\alpha - 1)(1 - X)} \, dX$$

**Step 4 — Partial-fraction decomposition:**

$$\frac{1 + (\alpha - 1)X}{X(\alpha - 1)(1 - X)} = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\frac{1}{X} + \frac{\alpha}{1 - X}\right]$$

**Step 5 — Integrate term-by-term:**

$$\int \frac{dX}{X} = \ln X \qquad ; \qquad \int \frac{dX}{1 - X} = -\ln(1 - X)$$

**Step 6 — Apply limits from x (residue) to x₀ (initial):**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right) + \alpha \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right)\right]$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Closed-Form Result (Most Frequently Tested in KTU):**

$$\boxed{\;\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right) + \alpha \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right)\right]\;}$$

### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Methanol–Water System)

**Given data:**
- Initial charge: L₀ = **1000 mol** of methanol–water mixture
- Initial methanol mole fraction: x₀ = **0.40**
- Final residue: L = **600 mol**, residue composition x = **0.20**
- Relative volatility (assumed constant): α = **3.89**

**Step 1 — Compute L₀ / L:**

$$\frac{L_0}{L} = \frac{1000}{600} = 1.6667 \quad \Rightarrow \quad \ln(1.6667) = 0.5108$$

**Step 2 — Compute ln(x₀ / x):**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{0.40}{0.20}\right) = \ln(2.0) = 0.6931$$

**Step 3 — Compute α·ln[(1 − x)/(1 − x₀)]:**

$$3.89 \cdot \ln\!\left(\frac{0.80}{0.60}\right) = 3.89 \cdot \ln(1.3333) = 3.89 \cdot 0.2877 = 1.1192$$

**Step 4 — Add and divide by (α − 1) = 2.89:**

$$\text{RHS} = \frac{0.6931 + 1.1192}{2.89} = \frac{1.8123}{2.89} = 0.6271$$

**Step 5 — Compare LHS vs RHS:**

$$\text{LHS} = 0.5108 \quad ; \quad \text{RHS} = 0.6271$$

**Step 6 — Percentage deviation:**

$$\% \text{ Deviation} = \frac{|0.6271 - 0.5108|}{0.6271} \times 100 = 18.5\%$$

This indicates either an experimental error (loss of vapor, leakage) or the constant-α assumption is violated (α varies with composition for methanol–water).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Simple Distillation Apparatus

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[HEATING MANTLE] --> B[ROUND BOTTOM FLASK STILL POT]
    B --> C[VAPOR LINE TO CONDENSER]
    C --> D[CONDENSER WATER IN OUT]
    D --> E[CONDENSATE COLLECTION VIALS]
    B --> F[THERMOMETER POCKET]
    F --> G[VAPOR TEMPERATURE READOUT]
    E --> H[REFRACTOMETER ANALYSIS]
    H --> I[COMPOSITION TABLE X VS TIME]
    I --> J[RAYLEIGH VERIFICATION PLOT]
```

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Material Balance Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P1[INITIAL CHARGE F MOLES X ZERO] --> M1[HEATING AND VAPOR GENERATION]
    M1 --> M2[EQUILIBRIUM VAPOR ESCAPES Y]
    M2 --> M3[CONDENSATION AND COLLECTION]
    M3 --> P2[D MOLES OF DISTILLATE XD AVG]
    M1 --> P3[RESIDUE W MOLES XW]
    P1 --> P4[OVERALL BALANCE F = D + W]
    P1 --> P5[COMPONENT BALANCE F X F = D X D + W X W]
    P4 --> P6[VERIFICATION TABLE]
    P5 --> P6
```

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Rayleigh Equation Verification Logic

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S1[MEASURE L ZERO X ZERO FROM CALIBRATION] --> S2[START DISTILLATION AND COLLECT CUTS]
    S2 --> S3[RECORD CUMULATIVE D AND INSTANTANEOUS X]
    S3 --> S4[COMPUTE EXPERIMENTAL LN L ZERO OVER L]
    S4 --> S5[COMPUTE INTEGRAL OF DX OVER Y MINUS X USING ALPHA]
    S5 --> S6[PLOT LHS VS RHS]
    S6 --> S7{DEVIATION LESS THAN FIVE PERCENT}
    S7 -->|YES| S8[RAYLEIGH VERIFIED]
    S7 -->|NO| S9[CHECK FOR VAPOR HOLDUP OR ALPHA VARIATION]
```

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Experimental Procedure Timeline

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    STAGE1[STAGE 1 CHARGE FLASK WITH KNOWN MIXTURE] --> STAGE2[STAGE 2 RECORD INITIAL REFRACTIVE INDEX]
    STAGE2 --> STAGE3[STAGE 3 APPLY HEAT AND WAIT FOR BOILING]
    STAGE3 --> STAGE4[STAGE 4 COLLECT CONDENSATE AT 5 ML INTERVALS]
    STAGE4 --> STAGE5[STAGE 5 ANALYZE EACH CUT USING REFRACTOMETER]
    STAGE5 --> STAGE6[STAGE 6 RECORD RESIDUE COMPOSITION]
    STAGE6 --> STAGE7[STAGE 7 PLOT LN L ZERO OVER L VS INTEGRAL]
    STAGE7 --> STAGE8[STAGE 8 VERIFY BOTH EQUATIONS]
```

### 4.5 Functional Component Table

> [!NOTE]
> **Table 4.1 — Apparatus Components and Their Roles**

| Component | Function | Material |
|---|---|---|
| Round-bottom flask (2 L) | Liquid reservoir / still pot | Borosilicate glass |
| Heating mantle | Uniform heat supply | Stainless-steel-cladded ceramic |
| Distillation head | Vapor transport + thermometer port | Borosilicate glass |
| Condenser (Liebig type) | Vapor condensation | Borosilicate glass |
| Receiving adapter | Directs condensate to vials | Borosilicate glass |
| Thermometer (0–110 °C) | Measures vapor temperature | Mercury / alcohol filled |
| Refractometer (Abbe) | Determines composition | Optical glass + calibrated scale |
| Graduated vials | Measure cumulative distillate volume | Borosilicate glass |

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Module 2]**
*State and explain Rayleigh's equation for simple distillation. Mention the assumptions involved.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
Rayleigh's equation for simple distillation relates the change in residual liquid composition to the amount of liquid distilled. The integrated form is:

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \int_{x}^{x_0} \frac{dX}{Y - X}$$

**Assumptions (1.5 marks):**
1. **Equilibrium** between the escaping vapor and the residual liquid at every instant.
2. **Negligible vapor hold-up** in the apparatus.
3. **Perfect mixing** in the still pot (uniform composition).
4. **Constant operating pressure** (usually 1 atm).
5. **No chemical reaction** between components.

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Module 2]**
*Differentiate between simple distillation and equilibrium (flash) distillation in three points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Simple Distillation | Flash Distillation |
|---|---|---|
| Operation mode | Batch / unsteady state | Continuous / steady state |
| Vapor–liquid contact | Differential; vapor escapes as soon as formed | Single equilibrium stage; vapor and liquid separated after equilibrium |
| Governing equation | Rayleigh (differential) | Material + equilibrium (algebraic) |

### 5.2 Part B — ESE Module Question (14 Marks) — CHOICE REQUIRED

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. Students frequently **forget to flip the limits** of integration — the Rayleigh integral is taken from residue (lower) to charge (upper), not the other way around. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
> 2. The **average distillate composition must be back-calculated from the overall balance**, not directly taken as the arithmetic mean of the cuts. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 3. Always **convert volume measurements to moles** using molecular weight and density before applying Rayleigh's equation. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 4. The **closed-form Rayleigh equation is valid ONLY when α is constant**. If α varies with composition, numerical (Simpson's rule / trapezoidal) integration must be used. **Loss: 2 marks.**
> 5. Units of L and L₀ must be **consistent (both in moles)** — never mix mass with moles.

---

#### **Question A — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Modified]**

**(a) [7 marks]** A charge of **1500 moles** of a binary mixture (methanol–water) containing **35 mol % methanol** is subjected to simple distillation at 1 atm until **65 mol %** of the charge has been distilled. The relative volatility is **constant at α = 3.89**.
(i) Calculate the composition of the residue using Rayleigh's equation.
(ii) Calculate the moles of distillate obtained.

**(b) [7 marks]** The experimental residue composition was found to be **0.197** (instead of the theoretical value you calculated in (a)).
(i) Determine the percentage deviation and state two probable causes.
(ii) For the same system, derive the differential form of Rayleigh's equation, clearly stating the assumptions.

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Given:**
- L₀ = 1500 mol, x₀ = 0.35
- Fraction distilled = 0.65 ⇒ L = (1 − 0.65) × 1500 = **525 mol**
- α = 3.89

**Step 1 — Apply closed-form Rayleigh equation:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{L_0}{L}\right) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{x_0}{x}\right) + \alpha \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x}{1 - x_0}\right)\right]$$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values:**

$$\ln\!\left(\frac{1500}{525}\right) = \frac{1}{2.89}\left[\ln\!\left(\frac{0.35}{x}\right) + 3.89 \ln\!\left(\frac{1 - x}{0.65}\right)\right]$$

$$1.0498 = \frac{1}{2.89}\left[\ln(0.35) - \ln(x) + 3.89\ln(1 - x) - 3.89\ln(0.65)\right]$$

$$3.034 = -1.0498 - \ln(x) + 3.89\ln(1 - x) + 4.4949$$

$$3.034 + 1.0498 - 4.4949 = -\ln(x) + 3.89\ln(1 - x)$$

$$-0.4111 = -\ln(x) + 3.89\ln(1 - x)$$

**Step 3 — Solve iteratively (trial-and-error or Newton's method):**

Let f(x) = −ln(x) + 3.89 ln(1 − x) + 0.4111

| x | f(x) |
|---|---|
| 0.18 | −(−1.715) + 3.89(−0.199) + 0.4111 = 1.715 − 0.774 + 0.411 = **1.352** |
| 0.10 | 2.303 − 1.732 + 0.411 = **0.982** |
| 0.05 | 2.996 − 2.515 + 0.411 = **0.892** |
| 0.02 | 3.912 − 3.745 + 0.411 = **0.578** |
| 0.005 | 5.298 − 4.464 + 0.411 = **1.245** |

Linear interpolation between x = 0.02 (f = 0.578) and x = 0.005 (f = 1.245) gives:

$$x \approx 0.02 - (0.578 / 0.667) \times 0.015 \approx 0.013$$

**Answer (a-i):** xW ≈ **0.013 mol fraction methanol in residue** (i.e., 1.3 mol %).
**Answer (a-ii):** Distillate moles D = 1500 − 525 = **975 mol**.

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Theoretical xW from (a) = 0.013; Experimental xW = 0.197.**

$$\% \text{Deviation} = \frac{|0.197 - 0.013|}{0.197} \times 100 \approx 93.4\%$$

This is a very large deviation; the experiment is **NOT verified**.

**Step 2 — Two probable causes (2 marks):**
1. **Vapor hold-up** is not negligible — vapor accumulates in the dead volume of the distillation head, raising the actual residue composition.
2. **Relative volatility is NOT constant** for methanol–water; α varies sharply with composition (from ~3.9 at low x to ~1.4 near azeotrope), so the closed-form equation is invalid.

**Step 3 — Derivation of differential form (already shown in Section 3.2, 4 marks).**

---

#### **Question B — 14 Marks [KTU University Exam – June 2023, Modified] — ALTERNATIVE**

**(a) [7 marks]** Derive the **closed-form Rayleigh equation** for a binary mixture with constant relative volatility α. State clearly the assumptions made.

**(b) [7 marks]** In a simple-distillation experiment, **800 g** of an ethanol–water mixture containing **30 wt % ethanol** is distilled until **400 g** of residue remains. The relative volatility is **α = 2.47**. Estimate the ethanol composition of the residue and the cumulative distillate.

**Model Solution for (a):** (Refer to Section 3.3 derivation — full 7 marks.) Key steps:
- Start with general Rayleigh: ln(L₀/L) = ∫dX/(Y − X).
- Substitute Y = αX / [1 + (α − 1)X].
- Simplify denominator: Y − X = X(α − 1)(1 − X) / [1 + (α − 1)X].
- Partial-fraction decomposition.
- Integrate term-by-term.
- Final closed-form result (as boxed in Section 3.3).

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1 — Convert mass to moles:**

Molecular weight of ethanol = 46 g/mol; water = 18 g/mol.

Initial moles of ethanol: 0.30 × 800 / 46 = **5.217 mol**
Initial moles of water: 0.70 × 800 / 18 = **31.111 mol**
Total L₀ = **36.328 mol**
Initial mole fraction x₀ = 5.217 / 36.328 = **0.1436**

**Step 2 — Convert residue mass to moles (assume residue composition unknown, so approximate):**

For a first estimate, assume residue is mostly water. Residual moles ≈ 400 / 18 ≈ 22.2 mol.

**Step 3 — Apply closed-form Rayleigh and iterate:**

Trial 1: assume xW = 0.05, L = 22.2 mol
LHS = ln(36.328/22.2) = 0.4928
RHS = (1/1.47)·[ln(0.1436/0.05) + 2.47·ln(0.95/0.8564)] = (1/1.47)·[1.055 + 2.47·0.1038] = (1/1.47)·[1.055 + 0.2564] = (1/1.47)·1.3114 = 0.8921

LHS < RHS ⇒ need more distillate (smaller L). Iterating:

Trial 2: xW = 0.04, L = 21.5 mol → LHS = 0.5248, RHS = 0.9482
Trial 3: xW = 0.03, L = 20.5 mol → LHS = 0.5722, RHS = 1.0243
Trial 4: xW = 0.02, L = 19.5 mol → LHS = 0.6221, RHS = 1.1117

Convergence: xW ≈ **0.025**

**Step 4 — Back-calculate cumulative distillate composition:**

D = L₀ − L = 36.328 − 19.5 ≈ **16.83 mol**
Average distillate composition:

$$x_{D,avg} = \frac{L_0 x_0 - L x_W}{D} = \frac{36.328 \times 0.1436 - 19.5 \times 0.025}{16.83} = \frac{5.217 - 0.4875}{16.83} = 0.2812$$

**Final Answers (b):** xW ≈ **0.025** (mol fraction), xD,avg ≈ **0.281** (mol fraction).

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist**

- **Material balance** is purely algebraic: **F = D + W** and **F·xF = D·xD + W·xW**.
- **Rayleigh equation** is *differential*: **dL/L = dX/(Y − X)**.
- **Integrated form**: **ln(L₀/L) = ∫ dX/(Y − X)** with limits **xW → xF** (residue to charge).
- **Closed form (constant α)**: **ln(L₀/L) = (1/(α − 1)) · [ln(x₀/x) + α·ln((1 − x)/(1 − x₀))]**.
- **Equilibrium relation (constant α)**: **y = α·x / [1 + (α − 1)·x]**.
- **Units**: All L values in **moles**; convert grams using molecular weight.
- **Verification** is done by **plotting ln(L₀/L) vs. ∫dX/(Y − X)** — a straight line through the origin with slope ≈ 1 confirms Rayleigh.
- **Five key assumptions**: equilibrium, negligible vapor hold-up, perfect mixing, constant pressure, no reaction.
- **Common experimental systems**: methanol–water, ethanol–water, acetone–water.
- **Composition analysis**: **refractometer** (Abbe) using a pre-calibrated standard curve of **nD²⁰ vs. x**.
- **Refractive-index calibration equation**: **x = (nD²⁰ − nW)/(nA − nW)** where nW = pure-water index and nA = pure-MVC index.
- **Always report**: initial volume/moles, initial x, final volume/moles, final x, average distillate x, % recovery, % deviation between experimental and theoretical profiles.
- **Sources of error**: vapor hold-up, heat losses, condenser leakage, refractive-index measurement uncertainty (typically ± 0.0005), variation of α with composition, partial miscibility, atmospheric pressure fluctuations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Steam distillation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Steam distillation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Steam Distillation** is a specialized co-distillation process in which steam (or water vapor) is passed through a mixture of an immiscible, high-boiling organic compound and water. The total vapor pressure over the mixture equals the **sum of the individual vapor pressures** of the two immiscible phases (per Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures for immiscible systems). This causes the mixture to boil at a temperature **substantially lower** than the boiling point of either pure component, enabling the separation and recovery of heat-sensitive organic substances (e.g., essential oils, terpenes, turpentine, aniline) without thermal degradation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** The experiment is designed to verify Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures by experimentally determining the boiling point of an immiscible liquid pair (typically water + an organic liquid like *toluene, nitrobenzene, or turpentine oil*) and comparing it with the theoretical value calculated using the **Antoine Equation** or **Clausius-Clapeyron Equation**.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition (Plain English)

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy — "The Tandem Bicycle Effect" 🚲**
>
> Imagine two friends, **Water** (a heavy, lazy friend who only wants to start pedaling at **100 °C**) and **Turpentine Oil** (a strong friend who only agrees to move at **160 °C**). Individually, neither will start their journey until the sun is scorching hot.
>
> However, if they sit on a **tandem bicycle together**, they only need to reach a moderate temperature (around **95–98 °C**) for the bicycle to move. Why? Because *they share the load* — the total effort required to push the bike is just the **sum of each person's minimum effort**, not the maximum. As soon as their combined effort equals the road's resistance, they both start moving — at a much lower temperature than either would need alone.
>
> **Steam Distillation works on the same principle!** The water vapor and the organic oil "share the load" of the atmospheric pressure, allowing the system to boil at a temperature well below 100 °C and far below the oil's actual boiling point.

---

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** **P = 101.325 kPa** (or **760 mmHg**, **1 atm**)
- **Universal Gas Constant:** **R = 8.314 J·mol⁻¹·K⁻¹**
- **Density of Water at 25 °C:** **ρ_w = 997 kg/m³**
- **Latent Heat of Vaporization of Water:** **ΔH_vap ≈ 2257 kJ/kg** at 100 °C
- **Steam Quality Requirement:** **Dry, saturated steam** (quality ≥ 0.95)

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundation — Dalton's Law for Immiscible Systems

When two **mutually immiscible liquids** (e.g., water and an organic oil) are heated together in intimate contact, the following physical principles govern the system:

1. **Each liquid behaves as if the other is not present** in terms of vapor pressure contribution. This is because each liquid is "saturated" with the other but the molecules at the surface exert their own independent vapor pressure.
2. **The total vapor pressure over the mixture** is the **arithmetic sum** of the saturated vapor pressures of the individual pure components at the given temperature.
3. **Boiling begins** the moment the total vapor pressure equals the pressure of the surrounding atmosphere.

### The Core Governing Equation:

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{A}^{0}(T) + P_{B}^{0}(T) + P_{\text{other gases}}$$

For a closed boiling system at atmospheric pressure (no non-condensables):

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{A}^{0}(T) + P_{B}^{0}(T) = P_{\text{atm}}$$

where:
- $P_{A}^{0}(T)$ = saturated vapor pressure of component A (water) at temperature T
- $P_{B}^{0}(T)$ = saturated vapor pressure of component B (organic) at temperature T
- $P_{\text{atm}}$ = atmospheric pressure (typically 101.325 kPa)

---

## 2.2 The "Why" Behind Lower Boiling Points

For a pure liquid, the boiling point is the temperature at which its vapor pressure alone equals atmospheric pressure. For water, this is **100 °C** (at 1 atm). For turpentine, it is around **160 °C**.

When mixed, *both* contribute vapor pressure **simultaneously**, so the sum reaches 1 atm at a temperature where neither pure component has reached its own boiling point. The result: a **co-boiling temperature** significantly below 100 °C, protecting heat-sensitive compounds.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Equation | Description | Units |
|---|--------------------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $P_{\text{total}} = P_{A}^{0} + P_{B}^{0}$ | Dalton's law for immiscible system | kPa |
| 2 | $y_{A} = \dfrac{P_{A}^{0}}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | Mole fraction of A in vapor phase | dimensionless |
| 3 | $y_{B} = \dfrac{P_{B}^{0}}{P_{\text{total}}}$ | Mole fraction of B in vapor phase | dimensionless |
| 4 | $\dfrac{m_{A}}{m_{B}} = \dfrac{P_{A}^{0} \cdot M_{A}}{P_{B}^{0} \cdot M_{B}}$ | Mass ratio in distillate (A = water, B = organic) | kg/kg |
| 5 | $\ln P^{0} = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ | **Antoine Equation** for vapor pressure | P in kPa/mmHg, T in °C/K |
| 6 | $\ln \dfrac{P_{2}}{P_{1}} = -\dfrac{\Delta H_{\text{vap}}}{R}\left(\dfrac{1}{T_{2}} - \dfrac{1}{T_{1}}\right)$ | Clausius-Clapeyron equation | P in Pa, T in K |
| 7 | $\%\text{ Yield} = \dfrac{\text{Actual mass recovered}}{\text{Theoretical mass predicted}} \times 100$ | Experimental performance metric | % |
| 8 | $V_{\text{steam}} = \dfrac{m_{A}}{\rho_{\text{steam}}} = \dfrac{m_{A} \cdot R \cdot T}{P \cdot M_{A}}$ | Volumetric steam requirement (ideal gas law) | m³ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Exam Note:** In the mass ratio formula, $M_A$ and $M_B$ are **molar masses**. Using them in g/mol is conventional; the ratio itself is unitless, but the absolute mass predicted depends on how much water is condensed.

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Steam distillation is **not just a textbook exercise** — it is the industrial workhorse for:

- **Extraction of essential oils** (lavender, eucalyptus, peppermint, citronella) in the **flavor & fragrance industry**.
- **Recovery of turpentine** from pine resin in the **naval stores industry**.
- **Purification of aniline, nitrobenzene, and fatty acids** in the **bulk organic chemicals sector**.
- **Petroleum refining:** separation of cracked petroleum fractions and removal of light contaminants.
- **Pharmaceuticals:** isolation of heat-labile bioactive compounds (e.g., eugenol from cloves).

The underlying engineering principle — *cooperative boiling of immiscible phases* — is a fundamental **mass transfer + thermodynamic** operation, frequently tested in KTU's Mass Transfer and Chemical Engineering Thermodynamics theory papers as well.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Distillate Composition from First Principles

We derive the mass ratio of water to organic component in the condensed distillate, starting from the mole fractions in the vapor phase.

### Step 1: Start with Dalton's Law for immiscible system.

At the boiling point $T_b$ of the mixture:

$$P_{\text{atm}} = P_{A}^{0}(T_b) + P_{B}^{0}(T_b)$$

### Step 2: Express vapor-phase mole fractions.

From the ideal gas law applied to the vapor phase:

$$y_{A} = \frac{P_{A}^{0}}{P_{\text{atm}}} \qquad y_{B} = \frac{P_{B}^{0}}{P_{\text{atm}}}$$

And by definition, $y_A + y_B = 1$.

### Step 3: Convert mole fractions to molar flow rates.

For an ideal gas in the vapor phase, the mole ratio equals the partial pressure ratio:

$$\frac{n_{A}}{n_{B}} = \frac{y_{A}}{y_{B}} = \frac{P_{A}^{0}}{P_{B}^{0}}$$

### Step 4: Convert moles to masses using molar masses.

$$\frac{m_{A}}{M_{A}} = n_{A} \quad \text{and} \quad \frac{m_{B}}{M_{B}} = n_{B}$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{m_{A}/M_{A}}{m_{B}/M_{B}} = \frac{P_{A}^{0}}{P_{B}^{0}}$$

### Step 5: Rearrange to obtain the **distillate mass ratio**:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{m_{A}}{m_{B}} = \frac{P_{A}^{0}(T_b) \cdot M_{A}}{P_{B}^{0}(T_b) \cdot M_{B}}\;}$$

This is the **master working equation** for any KTU steam-distillation problem.

---

## 3.2 Worked-Out Numerical Example (KTU Exam Style)

**Problem Statement:**
A mixture of **water (A)** and **nitrobenzene (B)** is to be steam-distilled at an atmospheric pressure of **101.325 kPa**. The Antoine constants for nitrobenzene are: $A = 6.789$, $B = 1405.0$, $C = 200.0$ (P in kPa, T in °C). For water, the vapor pressure at 99 °C is 97.75 kPa.

**Find:**
(a) The boiling point of the mixture.
(b) The mass ratio of water to nitrobenzene in the distillate.
(c) The mass of nitrobenzene recovered per kg of steam consumed.

**Given Data:**
- Molar mass of water, $M_A = 18.015$ g/mol
- Molar mass of nitrobenzene, $M_B = 123.06$ g/mol
- Atmospheric pressure, $P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325$ kPa

### Part (a): Boiling Point of the Mixture

We need to find T such that:

$$P_{\text{atm}} = P_{A}^{0}(T) + P_{B}^{0}(T)$$

$$101.325 = P_{A}^{0}(T) + P_{B}^{0}(T)$$

Since we know $P_A^0(99\,°C) = 97.75$ kPa, we calculate $P_B^0$ required at 99 °C:

$$P_{B}^{0}(99\,°C) = 101.325 - 97.75 = 3.575 \text{ kPa}$$

Let's verify with the **Antoine equation** for nitrobenzene at T = 99 °C:

$$\log_{10} P_{B}^{0} = A - \frac{B}{T + C} = 6.789 - \frac{1405.0}{99 + 200}$$

$$\log_{10} P_{B}^{0} = 6.789 - \frac{1405.0}{299} = 6.789 - 4.699 = 2.090$$

$$P_{B}^{0}(99\,°C) = 10^{2.090} = 123.0 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{much higher than } 3.575 \text{ kPa})$$

This tells us the true boiling point is **higher than 99 °C**. The Antoine equation dramatically overestimates $P_B^0$ at 99 °C — meaning the temperature must be lower. Let us iterate.

**Iteration 1:** Try **T = 85 °C**.

$$P_{A}^{0}(85\,°C) = ? \text{ (from steam tables: } \approx 57.83 \text{ kPa)}$$

$$P_{B}^{0}(85\,°C) = 10^{\left(6.789 - \frac{1405.0}{285.0}\right)} = 10^{(6.789 - 4.930)} = 10^{1.859} = 72.27 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 57.83 + 72.27 = 130.10 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{too high})$$

**Iteration 2:** Try **T = 80 °C**.

$$P_{A}^{0}(80\,°C) \approx 47.39 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{B}^{0}(80\,°C) = 10^{\left(6.789 - \frac{1405.0}{280.0}\right)} = 10^{(6.789 - 5.018)} = 10^{1.771} = 58.99 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 47.39 + 58.99 = 106.38 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{still slightly high})$$

**Iteration 3:** Try **T = 78 °C**.

$$P_{A}^{0}(78\,°C) \approx 43.99 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{B}^{0}(78\,°C) = 10^{\left(6.789 - \frac{1405.0}{278.0}\right)} = 10^{(6.789 - 5.054)} = 10^{1.735} = 54.32 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 43.99 + 54.32 = 98.31 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{very close to } 101.325 \text{ kPa})$$

**Iteration 4:** Try **T = 79 °C**.

$$P_{A}^{0}(79\,°C) \approx 45.65 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{B}^{0}(79\,°C) = 10^{\left(6.789 - \frac{1405.0}{279.0}\right)} = 10^{(6.789 - 5.036)} = 10^{1.753} = 56.62 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 45.65 + 56.62 = 102.27 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{extremely close})$$

**Conclusion:** The mixture boiling point $T_b \approx \mathbf{79\,°C}$ — well below water's 100 °C and dramatically below nitrobenzene's actual boiling point of 211 °C. ✓

### Part (b): Mass Ratio in Distillate

$$\frac{m_{A}}{m_{B}} = \frac{P_{A}^{0}(T_b) \cdot M_{A}}{P_{B}^{0}(T_b) \cdot M_{B}} = \frac{45.65 \times 18.015}{56.62 \times 123.06}$$

$$\frac{m_{A}}{m_{B}} = \frac{822.39}{6966.46} = 0.1180 \quad \text{(kg water per kg nitrobenzene)}$$

### Part (c): Mass of Nitrobenzene per kg Steam

This is simply the inverse:

$$\frac{m_{B}}{m_{A}} = \frac{1}{0.1180} = 8.47 \text{ kg nitrobenzene per kg water (steam)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** About **8.47 kg of steam is needed to recover 1 kg of nitrobenzene**. This large steam requirement is a key *design parameter* in industrial steam distillation columns.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Numerical Patterns

| Scenario Type | Knowns | Unknown | Key Formula Used |
|---------------|--------|---------|------------------|
| **Type 1:** Find mixture boiling point | $P_{\text{atm}}$, Antoine constants of both | $T_b$ | $P_{\text{atm}} = P_A^0(T_b) + P_B^0(T_b)$ (iterative) |
| **Type 2:** Find distillate composition | $T_b$, $M_A$, $M_B$ | $m_A/m_B$ | $\dfrac{m_A}{m_B} = \dfrac{P_A^0 \cdot M_A}{P_B^0 \cdot M_B}$ |
| **Type 3:** Find steam requirement | $m_B$ desired, $T_b$ | $m_A$ (kg steam) | Inverse of above + steam tables |
| **Type 4:** Verify with Clausius-Clapeyron | $T_b$, $P_1$, $T_1$ | $\Delta H_{\text{vap}}$ | $\ln(P_2/P_1) = -\Delta H_{\text{vap}}/R \cdot (1/T_2 - 1/T_1)$ |
| **Type 5:** Calculate % yield | Theoretical $m_B$ (from mass balance), actual $m_B$ (measured) | % Yield | $\%\text{Yield} = (m_{B,\text{actual}} / m_{B,\text{theo}}) \times 100$ |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Define steam distillation. State the principle on which it is based. Mention two industrial applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Steam distillation is a separation technique in which steam is passed through an immiscible, high-boiling organic mixture to vaporize and recover the volatile component at a temperature **below 100 °C**, thus preventing thermal decomposition.

**Principle (1 mark):** It is based on **Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures** for immiscible liquids. The total vapor pressure over the mixture is the sum of the individual saturated vapor pressures of the components:

$$P_{\text{total}} = P_{A}^{0} + P_{B}^{0}$$

Boiling occurs when this sum equals atmospheric pressure, which happens at a temperature lower than the boiling point of either pure component.

**Applications (½ mark each, any two):**
1. Extraction of **essential oils** (e.g., eucalyptus oil, lemon grass oil) from plant materials.
2. Recovery of **turpentine** from pine oleoresin in the naval stores industry.
3. Purification of **heat-sensitive organic compounds** like aniline and nitrobenzene.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why is steam distillation preferred over simple distillation for separating high-boiling, heat-sensitive substances? Justify with a relevant equation.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

Steam distillation is preferred because it allows the recovery of high-boiling organic substances at temperatures **well below their actual boiling points**, thereby avoiding thermal decomposition (1 mark).

**Justification (2 marks):** For an immiscible binary mixture, the boiling point $T_b$ is the temperature at which:

$$P_{A}^{0}(T_b) + P_{B}^{0}(T_b) = P_{\text{atm}} = 101.325 \text{ kPa}$$

Since both components contribute partial pressure *simultaneously*, $T_b$ is much lower than the boiling point of the pure organic component. For example, **turpentine (b.p. 160 °C)** can be steam-distilled at around **95–98 °C**, preserving its chemical integrity.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing mass ratio direction:** Students often write $\dfrac{m_B}{m_A}$ when the question asks for $\dfrac{m_A}{m_B}$ — read the question twice. The formula is *symmetric*; your interpretation is not.
> 2. **Forgetting units in Antoine equation:** P may be in **mmHg or kPa**, and T may be in **°C or K**. Always confirm which system before substituting.
> 3. **Mixing Dalton's Law for miscible vs. immiscible:** For miscible liquids (Raoult's Law), we use mole-fraction-weighted sums. For immiscible systems (steam distillation), it is a **simple arithmetic sum** — this distinction costs 2–3 marks if missed.
> 4. **Ignoring barometric pressure:** If the day-of-exam atmospheric pressure is, say, 100.5 kPa, the calculated $T_b$ shifts. KTU expects you to **use the given pressure**, not blindly assume 101.325 kPa.
> 5. **Not reporting % error:** KTU's CO5 mandates error analysis. Always compute:
> $$\%\text{ Error} = \frac{\vert T_{b,\text{exp}} - T_{b,\text{theo}} \vert}{T_{b,\text{theo}}} \times 100$$

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** With a neat labeled diagram, describe the laboratory setup for steam distillation. Explain the role of each component.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mixture of water and **bromobenzene** is steam-distilled at 100 kPa. Antoine constants for bromobenzene: $A = 6.881$, $B = 1440.5$, $C = 200$ (P in kPa, T in °C). For water, use steam table data.
- (i) Determine the boiling point of the mixture.
- (ii) Calculate the mass of bromobenzene distilled per kg of water condensed.
- (iii) Comment on the % yield if 18 g of bromobenzene is recovered from 250 g of steam consumed.
- **Given:** $M_{\text{bromobenzene}} = 157.01$ g/mol, $M_{\text{water}} = 18.015$ g/mol.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Apparatus Components (3 marks):**
1. **Steam Generator (Boiler):** Produces dry saturated steam at slightly above atmospheric pressure.
2. **Distillation Flask (Round-bottom):** Contains the organic sample; steam is bubbled through it.
3. **Thermometer:** Placed at the vapor outlet of the flask to record the boiling point.
4. **Condenser (Liebig type):** Cools the mixed vapor using counter-current cooling water.
5. **Receiver / Separating Funnel:** Collects the distillate; allows the organic and aqueous layers to separate by density difference.
6. **Heating Mantle / Burner:** Provides controlled heat input.
7. **Barometer:** Records atmospheric pressure for accurate calculations.

**Working Principle (2 marks):** Steam from the boiler passes into the distillation flask containing the organic compound. Since the two are immiscible, their vapor pressures add up (Dalton's Law). The mixture boils at a temperature lower than 100 °C. The vapor mixture rises, gets condensed, and is collected. The two immiscible liquids are then separated in the funnel.

**Diagram (2 marks):** (Refer to Section 4.1 Mermaid block diagram for the schematic; in exam, draw a labeled line diagram with all 7 components above.)

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Boiling Point of the Mixture (3 marks):**

We need $P_A^0(T_b) + P_B^0(T_b) = 100$ kPa.

**Trial 1: T = 95 °C.**

For water: $P_A^0(95\,°C) = 84.55$ kPa (from steam tables).

For bromobenzene via Antoine:
$$\log_{10} P_B^0 = 6.881 - \frac{1440.5}{95 + 200} = 6.881 - \frac{1440.5}{295} = 6.881 - 4.883 = 1.998$$

$$P_B^0(95\,°C) = 10^{1.998} = 99.5 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 84.55 + 99.5 = 184.05 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{too high})$$

**Trial 2: T = 80 °C.**

$P_A^0(80\,°C) = 47.39$ kPa.

$$\log_{10} P_B^0 = 6.881 - \frac{1440.5}{280} = 6.881 - 5.144 = 1.737$$

$$P_B^0(80\,°C) = 10^{1.737} = 54.6 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 47.39 + 54.6 = 101.99 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{very close to } 100 \text{ kPa})$$

**Conclusion:** $T_b \approx \mathbf{80\,°C}$ (more precisely, ~79.8 °C by further iteration).

**(ii) Mass of Bromobenzene per kg water (2 marks):**

At $T_b = 80\,°C$:

$$\frac{m_B}{m_A} = \frac{P_B^0 \cdot M_B}{P_A^0 \cdot M_A} = \frac{54.6 \times 157.01}{47.39 \times 18.015}$$

$$\frac{m_B}{m_A} = \frac{8572.7}{853.7} = 10.04 \text{ kg bromobenzene per kg water}$$

Theorist: **10.04 kg of bromobenzene per kg of steam.**

**(iii) % Yield (2 marks):**

For 250 g (0.250 kg) of steam consumed:

$$m_{B,\text{theoretical}} = 10.04 \times 0.250 = 2.51 \text{ kg} = 2510 \text{ g}$$

Actual recovery = 18 g.

$$\%\text{ Yield} = \frac{18}{2510} \times 100 = 0.72\%$$

**Comment:** The yield is extremely low (less than 1%), indicating significant losses — likely due to incomplete condensation, leaks in the apparatus, or incomplete phase separation. In a real lab, a yield of 60–85% is typical; below 50% suggests procedural errors.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks, Alternative) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the expression for the **mass ratio of water to organic component in the distillate** during steam distillation, starting from Dalton's Law of Partial Pressures.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A mixture of water and **aniline** is to be steam distilled at 101.325 kPa. The vapor pressure of aniline is given by the Antoine equation: $\log_{10} P = 7.362 - \dfrac{1731.5}{T + 200}$ with P in kPa and T in °C. Using steam tables for water:
- (i) Find the boiling point of the mixture.
- (ii) Calculate the mass of aniline recovered per kg of water distilled.
- **Given:** $M_{\text{aniline}} = 93.13$ g/mol.

**Model Solution:**

#### Part (a) — 7 Marks

**Refer to Section 3.1 of these notes for the complete step-by-step derivation.** Key milestones for marks:

1. State Dalton's Law for immiscible system: $P_{\text{atm}} = P_A^0 + P_B^0$ — **1 mark**
2. Express mole fractions: $y_A = P_A^0/P_{\text{atm}}$, $y_B = P_B^0/P_{\text{atm}}$ — **1 mark**
3. State ideal gas law / mole ratio: $n_A/n_B = P_A^0/P_B^0$ — **1 mark**
4. Convert to mass using molar masses: $m_A = n_A M_A$, $m_B = n_B M_B$ — **2 marks**
5. Final boxed equation:
   $$\frac{m_A}{m_B} = \frac{P_A^0 \cdot M_A}{P_B^0 \cdot M_B} \quad \text{— 2 marks}$$

#### Part (b) — 7 Marks

**(i) Boiling Point (4 marks):**

We solve iteratively: $P_A^0(T_b) + P_B^0(T_b) = 101.325$ kPa.

**Trial 1: T = 95 °C.**

For water: $P_A^0 = 84.55$ kPa.

For aniline:
$$\log_{10} P_B^0 = 7.362 - \frac{1731.5}{95 + 200} = 7.362 - 5.867 = 1.495$$

$$P_B^0(95\,°C) = 10^{1.495} = 31.26 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 84.55 + 31.26 = 115.81 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{too high})$$

**Trial 2: T = 90 °C.**

$P_A^0(90\,°C) = 70.14$ kPa.

$$\log_{10} P_B^0 = 7.362 - \frac{1731.5}{290} = 7.362 - 5.971 = 1.391$$

$$P_B^0(90\,°C) = 10^{1.391} = 24.60 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 70.14 + 24.60 = 94.74 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{slightly low})$$

**Trial 3: T = 92 °C.**

$P_A^0(92\,°C) = 75.59$ kPa.

$$\log_{10} P_B^0 = 7.362 - \frac{1731.5}{292} = 7.362 - 5.930 = 1.432$$

$$P_B^0(92\,°C) = 10^{1.432} = 27.04 \text{ kPa}$$

$$P_{\text{total}} = 75.59 + 27.04 = 102.63 \text{ kPa} \quad (\text{very close})$$

**Conclusion:** $T_b \approx \mathbf{91.7\,°C}$ (linear interpolation between 90 and 92 °C).

**(ii) Mass of aniline per kg water (3 marks):**

At $T_b \approx 91.7\,°C$:

Using $T = 92\,°C$ values (close enough):

$$\frac{m_B}{m_A} = \frac{P_B^0 \cdot M_B}{P_A^0 \cdot M_A} = \frac{27.04 \times 93.13}{75.59 \times 18.015}$$

$$\frac{m_B}{m_A} = \frac{2518.3}{1361.7} = 1.849 \text{ kg aniline per kg water}$$

**Theoretical recovery:** For 1 kg (1000 g) of steam, approximately **1.85 kg of aniline** is recovered.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why this is significant industrially:** Pure aniline boils at **184 °C**; via steam distillation, it boils at **~92 °C** — a **92 °C reduction**! This prevents oxidation and thermal cracking of the aniline, making steam distillation a **green, energy-efficient** alternative to vacuum distillation for many aromatics.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **The "5-Point Cheat Sheet" for the last 5 minutes before the exam:**

- ✅ **Principle:** Dalton's Law for immiscible liquids → $P_{\text{total}} = P_A^0 + P_B^0 = P_{\text{atm}}$
- ✅ **Master Equation (Distillate Composition):** $\dfrac{m_A}{m_B} = \dfrac{P_A^0 \cdot M_A}{P_B^0 \cdot M_B}$
- ✅ **Antoine Equation (for vapor pressure data):** $\log_{10} P = A - \dfrac{B}{T + C}$ — check the units of P and T before substituting.
- ✅ **Always include three calculations:** (1) Boiling point (iterative), (2) Mass ratio, (3) % Yield with error analysis.
- ✅ **Common mistake to avoid:** The mixture boiling point is *below* 100 °C — never report it as 100 °C or higher. Use iteration (trial-and-error) method, bracketing between two temperatures.

> [!TIP]
> **Bonus Mnemonic — "DAMP PA"** for the key concepts:
> - **D** — Dalton's Law (immutable)
> - **A** — Antoine (vapor pressure)
> - **M** — Mass ratio formula
> - **P** — Pressure balance: $P_A + P_B = 1$ atm
> - **P** — Phase separation in receiver
> - **A** — Atmospheric pressure correction
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Leaching: Leaching a mixture of salt and sand
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Leaching: Leaching a mixture of salt and sand

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 🧪 CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **LEACHING (Solid–Liquid Extraction):** Leaching is a **mass transfer operation** in which a soluble constituent (the *solute*) is selectively extracted from a solid mixture (the *feed*) by dissolving it into a liquid solvent. The process involves two simultaneous steps: **(i)** dissolution of the solute into the solvent, and **(ii)** diffusion of the dissolved solute away from the solid–liquid interface into the bulk liquid phase.

In the context of the KTU 2024 Scheme experiment, **leaching of a salt–sand mixture** uses **water (H₂O, M = 18.02 g/mol)** as the solvent to extract **sodium chloride (NaCl, M = 58.44 g/mol)** from an inert sand matrix (SiO₂). The dissolved NaCl-rich liquid is termed the **extract** or **leach liquor**, and the residual sand (carrying some retained solution) is the **underflow** or **raffinate**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tea Bag" Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Picture (Tea Brewing Analogy):**
> Imagine placing tea leaves (insoluble solid) with sugar (soluble solid) inside a porous cloth bag and dipping it into a cup of hot water. The hot water gradually dissolves the sugar, and the sugar-laden water trickles out of the bag as a clear, sweet solution — leaving behind the tea leaves still inside the bag. The *sugar* behaves like **salt**, the *tea leaves* behave like **sand**, the *hot water* is our **solvent**, and the *sugar solution dripping out* is our **extract**. The bag itself acts like a **filter medium** separating the spent solid from the product liquor.

In a laboratory setup, instead of a tea bag we use a **Buchner funnel + filter paper** and a **stirred beaker** to achieve the same separation efficiently.

## 1.3 Standard Physical Constants and Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value / Unit |
|---|---|---|
| Solubility of NaCl in water (at 25 °C) | $S_{NaCl}$ | **36 g / 100 g H₂O** |
| Density of water (at 25 °C) | $\rho_{H_2O}$ | **0.997 g/cm³** |
| Molecular weight of NaCl | $M_{NaCl}$ | **58.44 g/mol** |
| Molecular weight of water | $M_{H_2O}$ | **18.02 g/mol** |
| Typical feed ratio (salt : sand) | — | **1 : 2 to 1 : 4 (mass)** |
| Operating temperature | $T$ | **25 – 60 °C** |
| Particle size of sand | $d_p$ | **0.2 – 1.0 mm** |
| Viscosity of water (at 25 °C) | $\mu_{H_2O}$ | **0.89 cP** |
| Stirring speed (batch) | $N$ | **300 – 600 rpm** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Terminology Checklist (KTU Board expects these exact words):**
> - **Feed (F):** The initial salt–sand mixture charged to the extractor.
> - **Solvent (Sₒ):** Pure water used to dissolve the salt.
> - **Extract / Overflow (V):** The clarified salt solution leaving the extractor (salt-lean solid-free stream).
> - **Underflow (L):** The wet solid leaving the extractor, retaining some solvent + dissolved salt.
> - **Inerts (A or N):** The mass of insoluble solid (sand) — passes through unchanged.
> - **Solute (B):** The mass of NaCl — what we want to recover.
> - **Recovery (%):** $\eta = \dfrac{\text{Mass of salt in extract}}{\text{Mass of salt in feed}} \times 100$
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 🔬 DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Leaching Mechanism — Three Sequential Resistances

Leaching is governed by **three resistances in series** through which the solute must travel:

1. **Solvent Penetration Resistance:** The solvent must first wet the solid and reach the solute particle surface (overcome surface tension).
2. **Dissolution Resistance:** The solid NaCl dissolves at the interface — rate depends on **temperature**, **particle size**, and **solubility limit**.
3. **Diffusion Resistance:** The dissolved NaCl ions diffuse through the boundary layer (Nernst film) into the bulk liquid — governed by **Fick's Law**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Favourite:** When asked "which step is rate-controlling?" the safe answer is — *"In well-agitated batch leaching, **diffusion through the boundary layer** is the slowest (rate-controlling) step, because dissolution of NaCl is fast and reversible."*

## 2.2 Modes of Leaching Operation

| Mode | Description | Use Case in Lab |
|---|---|---|
| **Batch (Single Stage)** | All feed + solvent mixed in one vessel, then filtered | **Standard KTU experiment** |
| **Co-current Multi-stage** | Solid & solvent move in same direction through stages | Not common for this lab |
| **Counter-current Multi-stage** | Fresh solvent meets partially leached solid; rich extract meets fresh feed | Industrial (Bollman, Hildebrandt extractors) |

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **All quantities below are on a *batch / single-stage* basis as per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for PCSFL508.**

| # | Formula (LaTeX) | Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\eta = \dfrac{m_{salt,\,extract}}{m_{salt,\,feed}} \times 100$ | **Recovery percentage** | % |
| 2 | $F = A + B$ | Feed = Inerts + Solute (by mass) | kg |
| 3 | $F + S_o = V + L$ | **Overall mass balance** | kg |
| 4 | $B = V \cdot y_V + L \cdot x_L$ | **Solute (salt) balance** | kg |
| 5 | $A_{feed} = A_{underflow}$ | **Inerts balance** (sand is conserved) | kg |
| 6 | $R = \dfrac{L_{soln}}{A}$ | **Underflow ratio** (mass solution per kg inerts) | kg/kg |
| 7 | $y^* = \dfrac{x}{x + R}$ | **Equilibrium tie-line** (for fully-dissolved solutes) | dimensionless |
| 8 | $V_{solvent} = \dfrac{m_{water}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Volume of water added | L or m³ |
| 9 | $N_{Sh} = \dfrac{k_c \cdot d_p}{D_{AB}}$ | **Sherwood number** (dimensionless mass-transfer coefficient) | — |
| 10 | $J_A = k_c \cdot (C^* - C_b)$ | **Mass flux** through boundary layer (Fick's 1st law analog) | kg/(m²·s) |

> **Legends used in formulas:**
> - $F$ = mass of feed (kg), $A$ = mass of insoluble inerts (kg), $B$ = mass of solute NaCl (kg)
> - $S_o$ = mass of fresh solvent (kg), $V$ = mass of overflow extract (kg), $L$ = mass of underflow (kg)
> - $y_V$ = mass fraction of salt in extract (kg salt / kg extract)
> - $x_L$ = mass fraction of salt in underflow solution (kg salt / kg underflow solution)
> - $R$ = underflow ratio, $k_c$ = mass-transfer coefficient, $D_{AB}$ = diffusivity of NaCl in water ($1.61 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s at 25 °C)
> - $N_{Sh}$ = Sherwood number, $d_p$ = particle diameter, $C^*$, $C_b$ = interfacial and bulk concentrations

## 2.4 Why Leaching is Industrially Important

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Leaching is the **backbone of the mineral, food, and pharmaceutical industries**:
> - **Mineral processing:** Extraction of copper, gold, and uranium from ores using acids/cyanide.
> - **Sugar industry:** Diffusion of sucrose from sugar-cane bags using hot water.
> - **Edible oils:** Solvent extraction of soybean oil using hexane.
> - **Pharmaceuticals:** Extraction of alkaloids (caffeine, quinine) from plant matrices.
> - **Environmental remediation:** Removal of heavy metals from contaminated soil.
>
> In the KTU 2024 Scheme, this experiment trains you in the **fundamentals** that all these industrial processes share — **material balances**, **equilibrium relations**, and **stage calculations**.

## 2.5 The Three Zones Inside the Leaching Vessel

For a well-stirred batch leaching, the slurry is divided into conceptual zones:

- **Zone 1 (Bulk Liquid):** Solvent-rich, low salt concentration at start, builds up as leaching proceeds.
- **Zone 2 (Boundary Layer / Nernst Film):** Thin stagnant film around each sand grain where diffusion resistance dominates.
- **Zone 3 (Solid Matrix / Pore Liquid):** Solution trapped inside the sand pores; this becomes the underflow.

> **Engineering Insight:** Even with **perfect mixing**, the **underflow retains solution** (typically $R = 0.3$ to $0.6$ kg solution / kg inerts for sand). This is why a **single stage** never gives 100 % recovery — some salt always escapes with the wet sand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📐 STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Overall Mass Balance for a Single-Stage Leaching

**Statement:** *Show that for a batch leaching process, the sum of inputs equals the sum of outputs.*

**Step 1 — Define the system boundary.**
A stirred tank is charged with feed (F) of salt–sand mixture and solvent (Sₒ) of pure water. After equilibration, we withdraw:
- Overflow (V) = clarified salt solution
- Underflow (L) = wet sand + retained solution

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of total mass.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Input} &= \text{Output} \\
F + S_o &= V + L \\
\therefore \quad L &= (F + S_o) - V
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **total mass balance** in its most reduced form.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Solute (Salt) Mass Balance

**Statement:** *Derive the recovery equation from first principles.*

**Step 1 — Salt in the feed.** Let the feed contain mass fraction $x_B$ of salt (kg salt / kg feed). Therefore salt in feed = $F \cdot x_B$.

**Step 2 — Salt in the products.** All the salt must distribute between the overflow (V) and the underflow solution (carried in L):

$$
\begin{aligned}
F \cdot x_B &= V \cdot y_V + (L - A) \cdot y_L
\end{aligned}
$$

where $y_V$ = salt mass fraction in extract, $y_L$ = salt mass fraction in underflow solution, and $(L - A)$ is the mass of solution retained in the underflow.

**Step 3 — Define the recovery $\eta$.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\eta &= \dfrac{\text{salt recovered in extract}}{\text{salt in feed}} \times 100 \\
\eta &= \dfrac{V \cdot y_V}{F \cdot x_B} \times 100 \quad (\%)
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Numerical Example (KTU Board Pattern):**
> Suppose 200 g of feed contains 30 % salt → $F \cdot x_B = 60$ g NaCl. After leaching with 300 g water and filtering, the extract volume is 290 g with $y_V = 0.190$ → $V \cdot y_V = 55.1$ g. Then:
>
> $$
> \eta = \dfrac{55.1}{60} \times 100 = 91.83 \,\%
> $$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Single-Stage Stage Equation (Operating Line)

**Statement:** *Derive the operating-line relation for a single-stage batch leaching where the underflow carries solution at constant underflow ratio R.*

**Step 1 — Inerts balance.** Sand is insoluble, so:
$$
A_{feed} = A_{underflow} = A
$$

**Step 2 — Define coordinates.**
- $x_N$ = mass of solute per mass of inerts in the solid phase (kg solute / kg inerts).
- $y_{N+1}$ = mass of solute per mass of solution in the extract leaving stage N.
- $L$ = mass of solution in underflow per kg of inerts = $R$.
- $V$ = mass of overflow per kg of inerts (per unit basis).
- $S$ = mass of solvent per kg of inerts entering.

**Step 3 — Solute balance over stage N (in per-unit-of-inerts form):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
S \cdot y_s + x_N &= V \cdot y_{N+1} + (L \cdot x_N) \\
\Rightarrow V \cdot y_{N+1} &= S \cdot y_s + x_N(1 - L)
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — For pure solvent ($y_s = 0$) and negligible solute in solvent:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
y_{N+1} &= \dfrac{(1 - L)}{V} \cdot x_N \\
\therefore \quad y_{N+1} &= \dfrac{L}{V} \cdot x_N \quad \text{(when L and V are expressed as kg soln / kg inerts)}
\end{aligned}
$$

This is the **operating line**, a straight line through the origin with slope $L/V$ on the $y$–$x$ diagram (analogous to McCabe–Thiele).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Equilibrium Tie-Line for Salt–Sand

**Statement:** *Show that the equilibrium relation for a fully-dissolved, non-adsorbing solute (like NaCl) reduces to $y^* = x/(x + R)$.*

**Step 1 — Assumption.** At equilibrium, the solution adhering to the sand grains has the **same composition** as the bulk extract (NaCl does not adsorb on SiO₂).

**Step 2 — Express $x$ and $y^*$ in terms of common basis.**
- $x$ = mass solute / mass inerts = $C$ kg NaCl / $A$ kg sand
- $y^*$ = mass solute / mass solution in underflow = $C$ / $(C + A \cdot R)$

**Step 3 — Form the ratio:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\dfrac{x}{y^*} &= \dfrac{C/A}{C/(C + AR)} = \dfrac{C + AR}{A} \\
\Rightarrow y^* &= \dfrac{x}{x + R}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Physical interpretation.**
As $R \to 0$ (perfectly drained sand) → $y^* \to 1$ (very concentrated solution possible).
As $R \to \infty$ (extremely wet sand) → $y^* \to 0$ (salt is diluted into retained solution).

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Mass-Transfer Rate (Diffusion-Controlled Regime)

**Statement:** *Derive the rate of salt dissolution assuming diffusion through a Nernst boundary film is rate-controlling.*

**Step 1 — Fick's First Law across the film:**

$$
J_A = -D_{AB} \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dz}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Linearize concentration profile across film of thickness $\delta$:**

$$
J_A = \dfrac{D_{AB}}{\delta} \cdot (C^* - C_b) = k_c \cdot (C^* - C_b)
$$

**Step 3 — Total moles transferred in time $t$ across area $A_s$ (surface of sand):**

$$
N_A = k_c \cdot A_s \cdot (C^* - C_b) \cdot t
$$

**Step 4 — Use the Sherwood correlation for spheres in agitated tanks (Frössling / Ranz–Marshall):**

$$
N_{Sh} = 2 + 0.6 \, N_{Re}^{1/2} \, N_{Sc}^{1/3}
$$

with
$$
N_{Re} = \dfrac{d_p^2 \cdot N \cdot \rho}{\mu}, \qquad N_{Sc} = \dfrac{\mu}{\rho \cdot D_{AB}}
$$

**Step 5 — Example calculation.**
For $d_p = 0.5$ mm, $N = 5$ rev/s, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³, $\mu = 10^{-3}$ Pa·s, $D_{AB} = 1.61 \times 10^{-9}$ m²/s:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N_{Re} &= \dfrac{(5 \times 10^{-4})^2 \times 5 \times 1000}{10^{-3}} = 1.25 \\
N_{Sc} &= \dfrac{10^{-3}}{1000 \times 1.61 \times 10^{-9}} = 621 \\
N_{Sh} &= 2 + 0.6 \cdot (1.25)^{0.5} \cdot (621)^{0.33} \\
       &= 2 + 0.6 \times 1.118 \times 8.49 = 7.69 \\
k_c &= \dfrac{N_{Sh} \cdot D_{AB}}{d_p} = \dfrac{7.69 \times 1.61 \times 10^{-9}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} \\
    &= 2.48 \times 10^{-5} \; \text{m/s}
\end{aligned}
$$

This value of $k_c$ is typically used to **predict the time required to reach equilibrium** in the lab.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 🏗️ STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 Process Flow Diagram of the Salt–Sand Leaching Experiment

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["FEED PREPARATION"] --> B["Salt Sand Mixture 200g"]
    B --> C["Weigh on Electronic Balance"]
    C --> D["Transfer to 1L Beaker"]
    E["SOLVENT PREPARATION"] --> F["Measure 400 mL Distilled Water"]
    F --> D
    D --> G["Stir at 500 rpm for 15 min on Magnetic Stirrer"]
    G --> H["Allow Slurry to Settle for 2 min"]
    H --> I["Vacuum Filtration through Buchner Funnel"]
    I --> J["Collect Filtrate Extract in Filter Flask"]
    I --> K["Collect Wet Sand Underflow on Filter Paper"]
    J --> L["Measure Volume and Density of Extract"]
    L --> M["Titrate Extract with AgNO3 to Find NaCl"]
    M --> N["Calculate Recovery and Plot y versus x"]
    K --> O["Dry Wet Sand in Oven at 110C for 2 hours"]
    O --> P["Weigh Dried Sand to Confirm Inerts Balance"]
    P --> N
    N --> Q["RESULT Recovery and Material Balance Closure"]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of the Leaching System (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUTS["INPUT STREAMS"]
        A1["Feed Stream F Salt plus Sand"]
        A2["Solvent Stream So Pure Water"]
    end
    subgraph PROCESS["LEACHING UNIT STIRRED TANK"]
        B1["Mixing Zone High Shear"]
        B2["Dissolution Zone NaCl dissolves in H2O"]
        B3["Diffusion Zone Boundary layer transport"]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUTS["OUTPUT STREAMS"]
        C1["Overflow V Salt Solution Extract"]
        C2["Underflow L Wet Sand plus Retained Solution"]
    end
    A1 --> B1
    A2 --> B1
    B1 --> B2
    B2 --> B3
    B3 --> C1
    B3 --> C2
    C1 --> D1["Downstream Crystallization or Evaporation"]
    C2 --> D2["Sand Disposal or Washing Stage 2"]
```

## 4.3 McCabe–Thiele Style Operating Diagram for Salt–Sand Leaching

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Start with Equilibrium Tie Line y star equals x divided by x plus R"] --> B["Draw Operating Line through origin with slope L over V"]
    B --> C["Locate Step from Feed Composition xF on x axis"]
    C --> D["Step 1 between Operating Line and Equilibrium Curve"]
    D --> E{"Number of Stages greater than 1?"}
    E -- "Yes" --> F["Continue Stepping to Reach yS equals 0"]
    E -- "No Single Stage" --> G["Read x1 from Diagram"]
    F --> H["Determine Number of Ideal Stages N"]
    G --> I["Report Single Stage Recovery"]
    H --> I
    I --> J["Final Report N stages and Recovery percent"]
```

## 4.4 Three-Resistance Model — Conceptual Block Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Solid NaCl Particle"] --> B["Step 1 Solvent Wetting External Surface"]
    B --> C["Step 2 Dissolution at Interface Fast"]
    C --> D["Step 3 Diffusion through Boundary Film Slow Rate Controlling"]
    D --> E["Step 4 Mixing into Bulk Solution"]
    E --> F["Bulk Solution Rich in NaCl"]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📝 KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model QP]**
**Define leaching. With the help of a suitable example, distinguish it from liquid–liquid extraction.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Leaching is a solid–liquid extraction process in which a soluble component is removed from a solid mixture by dissolving it into a suitable solvent.
- **Example (1 mark):** Extraction of NaCl from a sand–salt mixture using water; sugar diffusion from cane using hot water.
- **Distinction from LLE (1 mark):** In **leaching**, one phase is a **solid** matrix; in **liquid–liquid extraction**, both phases are **immiscible liquids**. Leaching requires a dissolution step which is absent in LLE.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP]**
**State any three factors that affect the rate of leaching of salt from a sand mixture.**

**Model Answer (3 marks, 1 each):**
1. **Particle size** of sand — smaller $d_p$ gives larger interfacial area and shorter diffusion path → faster leaching.
2. **Temperature** — higher $T$ increases both solubility of NaCl and diffusivity $D_{AB}$ → faster leaching.
3. **Agitation speed** — higher RPM thins the Nernst boundary layer → higher $k_c$ → faster leaching.
*(Other valid factors: solvent-to-solid ratio, initial salt concentration, viscosity of solvent.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP]**

**(a)** A feed of 250 kg containing 30 % NaCl and 70 % sand (by mass) is to be leached with 400 kg of water in a single stage. The underflow carries 0.5 kg of solution per kg of inerts. The solution adhering to the sand is in equilibrium with the overflow. **Calculate the mass and composition of the extract and underflow, and the percentage recovery of salt.** (7 marks)

**(b)** Discuss with a neat sketch the working of a **Hildebrandt extractor** for counter-current leaching. (7 marks)

#### Model Solution for (a):

**Given data:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F &= 250 \text{ kg}, \quad x_F = 0.30 \text{ (salt)}, \quad 1 - x_F = 0.70 \text{ (sand)} \\
S_o &= 400 \text{ kg water}, \quad R = 0.5 \text{ kg soln / kg inerts}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1 — Compute inerts and solute in feed.**

$$
A = 0.70 \times 250 = 175 \text{ kg (sand)}, \quad B = 0.30 \times 250 = 75 \text{ kg (salt)}
$$

**Step 2 — Total mass balance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
F + S_o &= V + L \\
250 + 400 &= V + L \\
\therefore V + L &= 650 \text{ kg} \quad \text{...(1)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Underflow in terms of inerts.**

The underflow mass = inerts + retained solution = $A + R \cdot A = A(1 + R)$:
$$
L = 175 \times (1 + 0.5) = 175 \times 1.5 = 262.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Overflow from (1).**

$$
V = 650 - 262.5 = 387.5 \text{ kg}
$$

**Step 5 — Equilibrium tie-line.**
For salt–sand, $y^* = y_V$ (extract composition) and $x_L$ is salt/inerts in the underflow. The retained solution has the same concentration as the bulk solution:

$$
y_V = y_L = y = \dfrac{\text{salt in retained soln}}{\text{mass of retained soln}} = \dfrac{x_L \cdot A}{R \cdot A} = \dfrac{x_L}{R}
$$

**Step 6 — Salt balance.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
B &= V \cdot y_V + R \cdot A \cdot y_L \\
75 &= 387.5 \cdot y + 0.5 \times 175 \cdot y \\
75 &= 387.5 \, y + 87.5 \, y = 475 \, y \\
\therefore y &= \dfrac{75}{475} = 0.1579 \; \text{(mass fraction of salt in extract)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 7 — Salt in extract and recovery.**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Salt in extract} &= V \cdot y = 387.5 \times 0.1579 = 61.18 \text{ kg} \\
\eta &= \dfrac{61.18}{75} \times 100 = 81.57 \,\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 8 — Salt in underflow (for verification).**

$$
\text{Salt in underflow} = R \cdot A \cdot y = 0.5 \times 175 \times 0.1579 = 13.82 \text{ kg}
$$

**Check:** $61.18 + 13.82 = 75$ kg ✓

#### Model Solution for (b):
- **Construction (3 marks):** A Hildebrandt extractor consists of a **vertical column** divided into three sections — top (slightly tapered), middle (wider), and bottom (conical). It is fitted with a **central rotating shaft** carrying **horizontal impeller blades** and **perforated trays** or **scoops**.
