## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation of First Order Frequency Response

**Starting Point:** The standard first order transfer function is

$$G(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:**

We replace the Laplace variable $s$ with the imaginary frequency $j\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p}{\tau(j\omega) + 1} = \frac{K_p}{1 + j\tau\omega}$$

**Step 2 — Rationalize the Denominator (Multiply by Complex Conjugate):**

To extract magnitude and phase, multiply numerator and denominator by the complex conjugate of the denominator, which is $1 - j\tau\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p}{1 + j\tau\omega} \cdot \frac{1 - j\tau\omega}{1 - j\tau\omega}$$

**Step 3 — Expand the Denominator (Difference of Squares):**

$$(1 + j\tau\omega)(1 - j\tau\omega) = 1^2 - (j\tau\omega)^2 = 1 - (j^2)(\tau\omega)^2$$

Since $j^2 = -1$:

$$= 1 - (-1)(\tau\omega)^2 = 1 + (\tau\omega)^2$$

**Step 4 — Form the Real and Imaginary Parts:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p(1 - j\tau\omega)}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2} = \frac{K_p}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2} - j\frac{K_p\tau\omega}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Extract Magnitude (Modulus):**

$$M(\omega) = |G(j\omega)| = \sqrt{\text{Re}^2 + \text{Im}^2} = \sqrt{\left(\frac{K_p}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}\right)^2 + \left(\frac{K_p\tau\omega}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}\right)^2}$$

Factor the common denominator $1/[\,1 + (\tau\omega)^2\,]^2$:

$$M(\omega) = \frac{K_p}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}\sqrt{1^2 + (\tau\omega)^2} = \frac{K_p\sqrt{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}$$

$$M(\omega) = \frac{K_p}{\sqrt{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Extract Phase Angle:**

$$\phi(\omega) = \angle G(j\omega) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\text{Im}}{\text{Re}}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{-K_p\tau\omega / [1 + (\tau\omega)^2]}{K_p / [1 + (\tau\omega)^2]}\right)$$

The common denominator cancels:

$$\phi(\omega) = \arctan(-\tau\omega) = -\arctan(\tau\omega)$$

**Step 7 — Asymptotic Behaviour Check:**

- At very low frequency ($\omega \to 0$): $M(0) = K_p$ and $\phi(0) = 0°$ — output follows input exactly.
- At very high frequency ($\omega \to \infty$): $M(\infty) \approx K_p / (\tau\omega) \to 0$ and $\phi(\infty) = -90°$ — system completely attenuates fast signals.
- At corner frequency $\omega_c = 1/\tau$: $M(\omega_c) = K_p/\sqrt{2} = 0.707\,K_p$ (i.e., **$-3$ dB point**) and $\phi(\omega_c) = -45°$.

### 3.2 Derivation of Second Order Frequency Response

**Starting Point:** The standard second order transfer function is

$$G(s) = \frac{K_p}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta\tau s + 1}$$

**Step 1 — Substitute $s = j\omega$:**

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p}{\tau^2(j\omega)^2 + 2\zeta\tau(j\omega) + 1}$$

Recognize that $(j\omega)^2 = -\omega^2$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p}{-\tau^2\omega^2 + j\,2\zeta\tau\omega + 1} = \frac{K_p}{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2) + j\,2\zeta\tau\omega}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the Real and Imaginary Components of the Denominator:**

- Real part: $a = 1 - \tau^2\omega^2$
- Imaginary part: $b = 2\zeta\tau\omega$

**Step 3 — Rationalize the Denominator:**

Multiply by the complex conjugate of the denominator, $(1 - \tau^2\omega^2) - j\,2\zeta\tau\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p\,[\,(1 - \tau^2\omega^2) - j\,2\zeta\tau\omega\,]}{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2}$$

**Step 4 — Form Real and Imaginary Parts of the Output:**

$$\text{Re} = \frac{K_p(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)}{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2}$$

$$\text{Im} = -\frac{2K_p\zeta\tau\omega}{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2}$$

**Step 5 — Extract Magnitude Ratio:**

$$M(\omega) = \sqrt{\text{Re}^2 + \text{Im}^2} = \frac{K_p\sqrt{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2}}{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2}$$

$$M(\omega) = \frac{K_p}{\sqrt{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2}}$$

**Step 6 — Extract Phase Angle:**

$$\phi(\omega) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{\text{Im}}{\text{Re}}\right) = \arctan\!\left(\frac{-2\zeta\tau\omega}{1 - \tau^2\omega^2}\right) = -\arctan\!\left(\frac{2\zeta\tau\omega}{1 - \tau^2\omega^2}\right)$$

**Step 7 — Derive the Resonance Frequency $\omega_r$:**

Resonance occurs when $M(\omega)$ is **maximum**, which is equivalent to minimizing the denominator $D(\omega) = (1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2$ with respect to $\omega$.

Differentiate $D$ with respect to $\omega$ and set to zero:

$$\frac{dD}{d\omega} = 2(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)(-2\tau^2\omega) + 2(2\zeta\tau\omega)(2\zeta\tau) = 0$$

Divide both sides by $4\tau\omega$ (assuming $\omega \neq 0$):

$$-(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)\tau + 2\zeta^2\tau = 0$$

Cancel $\tau$:

$$-(1 - \tau^2\omega^2) + 2\zeta^2 = 0$$

$$-1 + \tau^2\omega^2 + 2\zeta^2 = 0$$

$$\tau^2\omega^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$$

$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{\tau}\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

**Step 8 — Validity Condition for Resonance:**

The expression under the square root must be positive:

$$1 - 2\zeta^2 > 0 \implies \zeta^2 < \tfrac{1}{2} \implies \zeta < \tfrac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **If $\zeta \geq 0.707$, the magnitude curve has NO peak** — it monotonically decreases. This is critical: students often mistakenly report a resonance frequency for overdamped or near-critically damped systems.

**Step 9 — Derive the Maximum Magnitude Ratio $M_r$:**

Substitute $\omega_r$ back into $M(\omega)$:

$$(1 - \tau^2\omega_r^2) = 1 - (1 - 2\zeta^2) = 2\zeta^2$$

$$2\zeta\tau\omega_r = 2\zeta\tau \cdot \frac{1}{\tau}\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2} = 2\zeta\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

Therefore:

$$D(\omega_r) = (2\zeta^2)^2 + (2\zeta\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2})^2 = 4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2(1 - 2\zeta^2) = 4\zeta^4 + 4\zeta^2 - 8\zeta^4 = 4\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2)$$

$$M_r = \frac{K_p}{\sqrt{4\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2)}} = \frac{K_p}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case Check:** At $\zeta = 0$ (undamped), $M_r = K_p/0 \to \infty$ — the system resonates infinitely. At $\zeta = 0.707$ (threshold of resonance), $M_r = K_p/(2 \times 0.707 \times 0.707) = K_p/1 = K_p$ — exactly the DC gain, confirming no peak above the asymptote.

**Step 10 — Derive the Bandwidth $\omega_B$:**

The bandwidth is the frequency at which the magnitude ratio drops to $K_p/\sqrt{2}$ (i.e., the **$-3$ dB point**). Set $M(\omega_B) = K_p/\sqrt{2}$:

$$\frac{K_p}{\sqrt{(1 - \tau^2\omega_B^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega_B)^2}} = \frac{K_p}{\sqrt{2}}$$

Squaring both sides:

$$(1 - \tau^2\omega_B^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega_B)^2 = 2$$

Let $u = \tau^2\omega_B^2$. Expanding:

$$(1 - u)^2 + 4\zeta^2 u = 2$$

$$1 - 2u + u^2 + 4\zeta^2 u = 2$$

$$u^2 + (4\zeta^2 - 2)u - 1 = 0$$

Applying the quadratic formula $u = \dfrac{-(4\zeta^2 - 2) \pm \sqrt{(4\zeta^2 - 2)^2 + 4}}{2}$:

$$u = (1 - 2\zeta^2) + \sqrt{(1 - 2\zeta^2)^2 + 1} = (1 - 2\zeta^2) + \sqrt{2 - 4\zeta^2 + 4\zeta^4}$$

Therefore:

$$\omega_B = \frac{1}{\tau}\sqrt{(1 - 2\zeta^2) + \sqrt{2 - 4\zeta^2 + 4\zeta^4}}$$

### 3.3 Experimental Identification Procedure (Lab Perspective)

For the KTU 2024 PCSFL508 lab examination, the typical frequency-response experiment proceeds as follows:

- **Apparatus Setup:** First order apparatus (e.g., a single stirred tank with controlled inlet flow) or second order apparatus (e.g., two non-interacting tanks in series, or a U-tube manometer with a throttling valve).
- **Function Generator / Sine Pump:** A sinusoidal signal generator drives a pneumatic valve or a variable-speed pump to produce oscillating input (flow rate, voltage, or pressure).
- **Data Acquisition:** A pair of identical transducers (at input and output) feed a dual-channel chart recorder or computer-based DAQ system.
- **Frequency Sweep:** Starting at the lowest frequency (long period), the operator adjusts the signal generator to ten or more discrete frequencies spanning two decades. At each frequency, the system is allowed to reach steady state (at least five full cycles).
- **Data Reduction:** From the steady-state recordings, read $X$, $Y$, and the time delay $\Delta t$. Compute $M = Y/X$ and $\phi = -(\Delta t/T) \times 360°$.
- **Bode Plot:** Plot $\log M$ vs $\log \omega$ and $\phi$ vs $\log \omega$ on semi-log graph paper.
- **Parameter Extraction:**
  - **For first order:** Draw the asymptotic low-frequency horizontal line at $M = K_p$. The corner frequency $\omega_c$ is read where the magnitude curve deviates by $-3$ dB from $K_p$. Then $\tau = 1/\omega_c$.
  - **For second order:** Identify the resonance peak height $M_r$ from the plot. Then $\zeta$ is solved from $M_r = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$. The peak frequency $\omega_r$ gives $\omega_n = \omega_r / \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$, and $\tau = 1/\omega_n$.

### 3.4 Numerical Worked Example (Typical KTU Board Problem Style)

> **Problem:** A second order system has $K_p = 2$, $\tau = 5$ s, $\zeta = 0.3$. Find (a) the magnitude ratio at $\omega = 0.1$ rad/s, (b) the phase angle at the same frequency, (c) the resonance frequency, and (d) the maximum magnitude ratio.

**Solution:**

**(a) Magnitude Ratio at $\omega = 0.1$ rad/s:**

$$M = \frac{2}{\sqrt{(1 - (5)^2(0.1)^2)^2 + (2 \times 0.3 \times 5 \times 0.1)^2}}$$

$$\tau\omega = 0.5; \quad (\tau\omega)^2 = 0.25; \quad 1 - 0.25 = 0.75; \quad (0.75)^2 = 0.5625$$

$$2\zeta\tau\omega = 0.3; \quad (0.3)^2 = 0.09$$

$$M = \frac{2}{\sqrt{0.5625 + 0.09}} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{0.6525}} = \frac{2}{0.8078} = 2.476$$

**(b) Phase Angle:**

$$\phi = -\arctan\!\left(\frac{0.3}{0.75}\right) = -\arctan(0.4) = -21.8°$$

**(c) Resonance Frequency:**

Check validity: $\zeta = 0.3 < 0.707$ ✓

$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{5}\sqrt{1 - 2(0.3)^2} = 0.2 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.18} = 0.2 \times \sqrt{0.82} = 0.2 \times 0.9055 = 0.1811 \text{ rad/s}$$

**(d) Maximum Magnitude Ratio:**

$$M_r = \frac{1}{2 \times 0.3 \times \sqrt{1 - 0.09}} = \frac{1}{0.6 \times \sqrt{0.91}} = \frac{1}{0.6 \times 0.9539} = \frac{1}{0.5724} = 1.747$$

With $K_p = 2$ included: $M_{r,\text{actual}} = 2 \times 1.747 = 3.494$.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024] Define the term "frequency response" of a linear system. Why is it useful in process control?**

**Model Answer:**
The frequency response of a linear, time-invariant system is defined as the steady-state ratio of the output sinusoid to the input sinusoid when the input is varied over a range of frequencies. Mathematically, it is obtained by substituting $s = j\omega$ into the transfer function $G(s)$, yielding $G(j\omega) = M(\omega) e^{j\phi(\omega)}$, where $M(\omega)$ is the magnitude ratio and $\phi(\omega)$ is the phase angle. It is useful in process control because (i) it provides a non-parametric method to identify system dynamics experimentally without disturbing the process, (ii) it allows stability analysis of closed-loop systems via Bode and Nyquist criteria, and (iii) it forms the basis of controller tuning techniques such as the Ziegler–Nichols method. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023] Distinguish between the frequency response of a first order and a second order underdamped system. Mention any two distinguishing features.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | First Order | Second Order (Underdamped) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Magnitude ratio at $\omega \to \infty$ | Decays as $1/\omega$ ($-20$ dB/decade) | Decays as $1/\omega^2$ ($-40$ dB/decade) |
| Phase angle range | $0°$ to $-90°$ | $0°$ to $-180°$ |
| Resonance peak | Absent | Present if $\zeta < 0.707$ |
| Number of energy storages | One | Two |

**(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Question — Internal Choice (14 Marks)

#### **Question A — First Order Frequency Response Focus** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the frequency response function $G(j\omega)$ of a first order system with transfer function $G(s) = K_p / (\tau s + 1)$. Hence obtain expressions for the magnitude ratio $M(\omega)$ and phase angle $\phi(\omega)$. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A first order thermal system has a steady-state gain $K_p = 1.5$ and time constant $\tau = 10$ s. A sinusoidal temperature input of amplitude $X = 5$ °C and frequency $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s is applied. Calculate (i) the output amplitude, (ii) the phase lag in degrees, and (iii) the time lag between input and output peaks. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Start with $G(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau s + 1}$.

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p}{1 + j\tau\omega}$$

Rationalize by multiplying by the complex conjugate:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p(1 - j\tau\omega)}{(1 + j\tau\omega)(1 - j\tau\omega)} = \frac{K_p(1 - j\tau\omega)}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}$$

**Magnitude ratio:**

$$M(\omega) = \frac{K_p\sqrt{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}}{1 + (\tau\omega)^2} = \frac{K_p}{\sqrt{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}}$$

**Phase angle:**

$$\phi(\omega) = -\arctan(\tau\omega)$$

The phase decreases monotonically from $0°$ at $\omega = 0$ to $-90°$ at $\omega = \infty$, passing through $-45°$ at the corner frequency $\omega_c = 1/\tau$. The magnitude is flat at $K_p$ for $\omega \ll 1/\tau$ and rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade for $\omega \gg 1/\tau$. **(7 marks — 2 for substitution, 2 for magnitude, 2 for phase, 1 for behaviour description)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $K_p = 1.5$, $\tau = 10$ s, $X = 5$ °C, $\omega = 0.2$ rad/s. Therefore $\tau\omega = 10 \times 0.2 = 2$.

**(i) Output amplitude:**

$$M = \frac{1.5}{\sqrt{1 + (2)^2}} = \frac{1.5}{\sqrt{5}} = \frac{1.5}{2.2361} = 0.6708$$

$$Y = M \times X = 0.6708 \times 5 = 3.354 \text{ °C}$$

**(ii) Phase lag:**

$$\phi = -\arctan(2) = -63.43°$$

**(iii) Time lag:**

Period $T = 2\pi/\omega = 2\pi/0.2 = 31.416$ s.

$$\Delta t = \frac{|\phi|}{360°} \times T = \frac{63.43}{360} \times 31.416 = 5.535 \text{ s}$$

**(7 marks — 2 for (i), 2 for (ii), 3 for (iii))**

---

#### **Question B — Second Order Frequency Response Focus** [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Derive the magnitude ratio and phase angle expressions for a second order system. Hence, derive the condition for the existence of a resonance peak and the formula for the maximum magnitude ratio. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** For an underdamped second order system, the experimentally observed resonance frequency is $\omega_r = 2.5$ rad/s and the maximum magnitude ratio is $M_r = 4$. If the steady-state gain $K_p = 2$, determine (i) the damping ratio $\zeta$, (ii) the natural frequency $\omega_n$ and time constant $\tau$, and (iii) the magnitude ratio at $\omega = \omega_n$. **[7 marks]**

#### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Derivation:**

Start with $G(s) = \dfrac{K_p}{\tau^2 s^2 + 2\zeta\tau s + 1}$.

Substitute $s = j\omega$:

$$G(j\omega) = \frac{K_p}{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2) + j\,2\zeta\tau\omega}$$

**Magnitude ratio:**

$$M(\omega) = \frac{K_p}{\sqrt{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2}}$$

**Phase angle:**

$$\phi(\omega) = -\arctan\!\left(\frac{2\zeta\tau\omega}{1 - \tau^2\omega^2}\right)$$

**Resonance condition:** Differentiate the denominator $D(\omega) = (1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2$ and set $\dfrac{dD}{d\omega} = 0$:

$$\frac{dD}{d\omega} = 4\tau^2\omega(\tau^2\omega^2 - 1) + 8\zeta^2\tau^2\omega = 0$$

Dividing by $4\tau^2\omega$:

$$(\tau^2\omega^2 - 1) + 2\zeta^2 = 0 \implies \tau^2\omega^2 = 1 - 2\zeta^2$$

For a real positive solution, $1 - 2\zeta^2 > 0$, hence the **resonance existence condition** is:

$$\zeta < \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \approx 0.707$$

The resonance frequency is:

$$\omega_r = \frac{1}{\tau}\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2} = \omega_n\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$$

**Maximum magnitude ratio:** Substituting $\omega_r$ into $M(\omega)$:

$$M_r = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$$

(in normalised form, with $K_p = 1$). For general $K_p$, multiply by $K_p$. **(7 marks — 2 for magnitude/phase, 3 for resonance derivation, 2 for $M_r$)**

**Part (b) — Numerical Computation:**

Given: $\omega_r = 2.5$ rad/s, $M_r = 4$ (assuming $K_p$ absorbed), $K_p = 2$ so the normalised peak is $M_r/K_p = 4/2 = 2$.

**(i) Damping ratio:**

$$\frac{M_r}{K_p} = \frac{1}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} = 2$$

$$2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2} = \tfrac{1}{2} = 0.5$$

Square both sides:

$$4\zeta^2(1 - \zeta^2) = 0.25 \implies 4\zeta^2 - 4\zeta^4 = 0.25$$

Let $x = \zeta^2$:

$$4x^2 - 4x + 0.25 = 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$x = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{16 - 4}}{8} = \frac{4 \pm \sqrt{12}}{8} = \frac{4 \pm 3.464}{8}$$

Two solutions: $x_1 = 0.933$ → $\zeta = 0.966$ (rejected, no resonance possible since $\zeta > 0.707$); $x_2 = 0.067$ → $\zeta = 0.2589$. Valid: $\zeta = 0.259$.

**(ii) Natural frequency and time constant:**

$$\omega_n = \frac{\omega_r}{\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{1 - 2(0.0670)}} = \frac{2.5}{\sqrt{0.866}} = \frac{2.5}{0.9306} = 2.687 \text{ rad/s}$$

$$\tau = \frac{1}{\omega_n} = \frac{1}{2.687} = 0.372 \text{ s}$$

**(iii) Magnitude ratio at $\omega = \omega_n$:**

At $\omega = \omega_n$, the term $\tau^2\omega^2 = 1$, so:

$$M(\omega_n) = \frac{K_p}{\sqrt{0 + (2\zeta)^2}} = \frac{K_p}{2\zeta} = \frac{2}{2 \times 0.259} = \frac{2}{0.518} = 3.86$$

**(7 marks — 3 for (i), 2 for (ii), 2 for (iii))**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Forgetting to normalise by $K_p$:** When asked to find $\zeta$ from $M_r$ data, many students plug the raw $M_r$ into the formula $1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1-\zeta^2})$ and forget that the formula gives the *ratio* $M_r/K_p$. This is the single most common cause of full-mark loss in Part B.
> 2. **Reporting resonance frequency for overdamped systems:** If $\zeta \geq 0.707$, $\omega_r$ does not exist. Always verify the existence condition first.
> 3. **Sign convention for phase angle:** In frequency response, the phase is *negative* (output lags input) for stable minimum-phase systems. A positive phase answer is a red flag and will be marked wrong.
> 4. **Units:** $\omega$ must always be in rad/s. The natural frequency $\omega_n$ and corner frequency $\omega_c$ are NOT in Hz. Converting incorrectly costs 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Slope confusion:** First order rolls off at $-20$ dB/decade; second order at $-40$ dB/decade. Mixing these up in a Bode plot sketch loses 2 marks.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Frequency response** = substitution of $s = j\omega$ in $G(s)$ to obtain the **frequency response function (FRF)** $G(j\omega)$.
- The FRF is a **complex number** at each frequency $\omega$ — its modulus is the **magnitude ratio** $M(\omega)$ and its argument is the **phase angle** $\phi(\omega)$.
- **First order** $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K_p}{1 + j\tau\omega}$: magnitude $M = \dfrac{K_p}{\sqrt{1 + (\tau\omega)^2}}$; phase $\phi = -\arctan(\tau\omega)$.
- **Second order** $G(j\omega) = \dfrac{K_p}{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2) + j\,2\zeta\tau\omega}$: magnitude $M = \dfrac{K_p}{\sqrt{(1 - \tau^2\omega^2)^2 + (2\zeta\tau\omega)^2}}$; phase $\phi = -\arctan\!\left(\dfrac{2\zeta\tau\omega}{1 - \tau^2\omega^2}\right)$.
- **Resonance frequency** $\omega_r = \dfrac{1}{\tau}\sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$ exists **only if** $\zeta < 0.707$.
- **Maximum magnitude ratio** $M_r = \dfrac{K_p}{2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}$ at $\omega = \omega_r$.
- **Corner / natural frequency** is $\omega_c = 1/\tau$ (first order) or $\omega_n = 1/\tau$ (second order).
- **At $\omega = \omega_c$ (1st order)**: $M = K_p/\sqrt{2}$ ($\text{$-3$ dB point}$), $\phi = -45°$.
- **At $\omega = \omega_n$ (2nd order)**: $\phi = -90°$, $M = K_p/(2\zeta)$.
- **Asymptotic slopes:** First order: $-20$ dB/decade; Second order: $-40$ dB/decade.
- **Phase endpoints:** First order: $0°$ to $-90°$; Second order: $0°$ to $-180°$.
- **Experimental method:** Apply a sinusoidal input at multiple frequencies → record output amplitude $Y$ and time lag $\Delta t$ in steady state → compute $M = Y/X$ and $\phi = -360° \cdot \Delta t / T$ → plot Bode diagrams on semi-log paper.
- **Parameter identification:** From the Bode plot, read $K_p$ as the low-frequency plateau, $\tau = 1/\omega_c$ (1st order) or $1/\omega_n$ (2nd order), and $\zeta$ from the resonance peak height $M_r$ via $M_r = 1/(2\zeta\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2})$.
- **Lab apparatus examples:** Single mixing tank (1st order), two non-interacting tanks in series (2nd order), U-tube manometer with throttle (2nd order), RC circuit (1st order), RLC circuit (2nd order).
- **Why it matters in industry:** Stability analysis, controller tuning (Z-N ultimate gain method), filter design, and process identification for MPC (model predictive control).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Determination of pH, turbidity, total hardness, total solids and dissolved oxygen of water samples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of pH, turbidity, total hardness, total solids and dissolved oxygen of water samples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Water Quality Analysis

Water quality is a critical parameter in chemical, environmental, and public health engineering. The five core parameters studied in this experiment form the backbone of any standard water analysis report required by regulatory bodies such as the **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS: IS 10500)** and the **World Health Organization (WHO)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** This experiment is part of the *Water and Wastewater Analysis* cluster under the **Process Control & Safety / Fluid & Particle Mechanics** laboratory track. Students are expected to perform all five tests, tabulate observations, and report results with correct units and significant figures.

---

## 1.2 Parameter Definitions

### 1.2.1 pH (Potential of Hydrogen)

**Formal Definition:** pH is the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen ion (H⁺) activity (or molar concentration in dilute solutions) in an aqueous system. It quantifies the acidic or basic nature of a solution on a logarithmic scale ranging from **0 to 14**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Think of pH as a "spice meter" for water. Pure water (pH 7) is bland. Add lemon juice and the meter drops below 7 (sour/acidic). Add baking soda and it rises above 7 (bitter/basic). Each single step on the pH scale represents a **ten-fold (10×)** change in acidity — so going from pH 7 to pH 5 means the water is now **100 times more acidic** than before.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Reference:** Pure water at **25 °C (298.15 K)** has a pH of exactly **7.0** because [H⁺] = [OH⁻] = **1.0 × 10⁻⁷ mol/L**.

---

### 1.2.2 Turbidity

**Formal Definition:** Turbidity is an optical property of water that indicates the presence of suspended and colloidal particles (clay, silt, organic matter, algae, microorganisms). It is measured in **Nephelometric Turbidity Units (NTU)** using a nephelometer that detects scattered light at 90° to the incident beam.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Imagine shining a flashlight through a clean glass of water versus a glass of muddy water at night. In clean water, the beam is barely visible (low turbidity). In muddy water, you can clearly see the light beam because the suspended particles scatter the light in all directions (high turbidity). A nephelometer is essentially an "underwater flashlight and sensor" that measures exactly how much light is being scattered.

> [!NOTE]
> **BIS Drinking Water Limit:** Turbidity should be **≤ 1 NTU** (ideally) and **≤ 5 NTU** (maximum permissible) as per IS 10500:2012.

---

### 1.2.3 Total Hardness

**Formal Definition:** Total Hardness (TH) is the total concentration of divalent metallic cations — primarily **calcium (Ca²⁺)** and **magnesium (Mg²⁺)** ions — expressed as the equivalent concentration of **calcium carbonate (CaCO₃)** in mg/L.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Hard water is "difficult" water. When you wash clothes with hard water, soap doesn't lather well because the Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions "grab" the soap molecules and form a sticky scum (curd) instead of bubbles. The more of these ions present, the "harder" the water. In boilers and pipes, this hardness causes **scaling** — just like cholesterol clogging arteries in a human body.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hardness Classification (as CaCO₃):**
> - **Soft:** 0 – 60 mg/L
> - **Moderately Hard:** 61 – 120 mg/L
> - **Hard:** 121 – 180 mg/L
> - **Very Hard:** > 180 mg/L

---

### 1.2.4 Total Solids (TS, TDS, TSS)

**Formal Definition:**
- **Total Solids (TS):** The total residue left after evaporating a known volume of water to dryness at a specified temperature (103 – 105 °C).
- **Total Dissolved Solids (TDS):** The residue from the filtrate after passing water through a 1.2 µm glass-fiber filter.
- **Total Suspended Solids (TSS):** The residue retained on the filter after drying. Mathematically: **TSS = TS − TDS**.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Pour a cup of tea into a fine sieve. The wet leaves left on the sieve are the **suspended solids**. The dissolved sugar, milk particles, and tea compounds that pass through are the **dissolved solids**. The total mess (leaves + dissolved stuff) is the **total solids**. In water treatment, removing suspended solids is "filtering" and removing dissolved solids requires more advanced processes like **reverse osmosis**.

> [!NOTE]
> **BIS Drinking Water Limit:** TDS should be **≤ 500 mg/L** (acceptable) and **≤ 2000 mg/L** (permissible) per IS 10500.

---

### 1.2.5 Dissolved Oxygen (DO)

**Formal Definition:** Dissolved Oxygen is the amount of molecular oxygen (O₂) physically dissolved in water, expressed in **mg/L** (or ppm by mass). It is a vital indicator of the water body's ability to support aquatic life and its degree of pollution by organic matter.

**Conceptual Analogy / Intuition:** Fish "breathe" by extracting oxygen dissolved in water through their gills, just like humans extract oxygen from air. A fish tank without aeration quickly suffocates the fish as DO drops. Similarly, in a polluted river, bacteria consume the oxygen to decompose organic waste, starving the fish. **High DO = healthy ecosystem**; **Low DO = polluted, stressed water body**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **DO Reference Standards (BIS):**
> - **≥ 6 mg/L:** Good for aquatic life
> - **4 – 6 mg/L:** Acceptable
> - **< 4 mg/L:** Polluted / stressed
> - **< 2 mg/L:** Hypoxic — fish kills likely

---

## 1.3 Summary Snapshot

| # | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Unit | WHO/BIS Drinking Water Limit | Key Principle |
|---|-----------|--------|---------------|------------------------------|---------------|
| 1 | pH | pH | dimensionless | 6.5 – 8.5 | Electrochemical (glass electrode) |
| 2 | Turbidity | τ | NTU | ≤ 1 NTU (ideal) | Light scattering at 90° |
| 3 | Total Hardness | TH | mg/L as CaCO₃ | ≤ 200 mg/L | EDTA complexometric titration |
| 4 | Total Solids | TS / TDS / TSS | mg/L | TDS ≤ 500 mg/L | Gravimetric (evaporation) |
| 5 | Dissolved Oxygen | DO | mg/L O₂ | ≥ 6 mg/L | Winkler's iodometric method |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 pH — Theoretical Foundation

The pH of an aqueous solution is governed by the **auto-ionization constant of water (K_w)**:

$$K_w = [\text{H}^+][\text{OH}^-] = 1.0 \times 10^{-14} \text{ at } 25\,°C$$

Taking the negative logarithm of both sides:

$$\text{pH} + \text{pOH} = \text{p}K_w = 14 \text{ at } 25\,°C$$

### Measurement Principle (Glass Electrode pH Meter)
- A **glass membrane electrode** develops a potential proportional to log[H⁺] (Nernst equation).
- A **reference electrode** (saturated calomel or Ag/AgCl) provides a constant potential.
- The combined pH meter reads the **electromotive force (EMF)** and converts it to pH units.

**Nernst Equation (for the hydrogen electrode):**

$$E = E^0 - \frac{2.303\,RT}{nF} \log[\text{H}^+] = E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$$

Where at 25 °C, the slope is **59.16 mV per pH unit**.

### Calibration Procedure
1. Calibrate with **buffer solution pH 7.0** (neutral).
2. Calibrate with **buffer pH 4.0** (acidic) OR **pH 9.2** (basic).
3. Rinse the electrode with distilled water between buffers (no wiping — just blot).
4. Adjust **"Slope"** knob to 100% efficiency and **"Standardize"** knob to match buffer pH.

---

## 2.2 Turbidity — Light Scattering Theory

A nephelometer projects a beam of light (typically **860 nm near-IR LED**) through the water sample. Suspended particles scatter light. A photodetector placed at **90° to the incident beam** measures the intensity of scattered light (Rayleigh & Mie scattering regimes).

**Beer-Lambert Type Relation (qualitative):**

$$I_{\text{scattered}} \propto N \cdot d^6 \cdot \frac{I_0}{\lambda^4}$$

Where **N** = number of particles, **d** = particle diameter, **I₀** = incident intensity, **λ** = wavelength.

- **Formazin polymer** (a suspension of hydrazine sulfate + hexamethylenetetramine) is the **primary turbidity standard** (4000 NTU stock).
- Calibration curve: prepare standards of 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 NTU and plot NTU vs. scattered intensity.

---

## 2.3 Total Hardness — EDTA Complexometric Titration

### Chemical Principle
**EDTA (Ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid)** is a hexadentate ligand that forms a stable 1:1 complex with Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions:

$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{EDTA}^{4-} \rightleftharpoons [\text{Ca–EDTA}]^{2-} \quad (K_f \approx 10^{10})$$
$$\text{Mg}^{2+} + \text{EDTA}^{4-} \rightleftharpoons [\text{Mg–EDTA}]^{2-} \quad (K_f \approx 10^{9})$$

### Procedure Logic
1. Add **NH₃/NH₄Cl buffer (pH ≈ 10.0 ± 0.1)** to maintain alkaline pH so that Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ form stable EDTA complexes.
2. Add indicator **Eriochrome Black T (EBT)** — a triphenylmethane dye that forms a weak wine-red complex with Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺.
3. Titrate with **standardized 0.01 M EDTA solution** until the color changes from **wine-red → sky blue** (endpoint).
4. At the endpoint, free Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ are chelated, releasing the EBT indicator in its free blue form.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why NH₃/NH₄Cl buffer?** At lower pH, the H⁺ ions compete with Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺ for EDTA, weakening the complex. At higher pH, Ca²⁺ precipitates as Ca(OH)₂. The pH 10 buffer is the optimal compromise.

### Calculation Logic
The total moles of Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ = moles of EDTA consumed (since 1:1 stoichiometry). This is reported as **CaCO₃ equivalent**, where the equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = **50 g/eq** (MW/2 = 100/2).

---

## 2.4 Total Solids — Gravimetric Theory

### TS Measurement
1. Pre-dry the **evaporating dish** at 103 – 105 °C, cool in a **desiccator**, and weigh (**W₁**).
2. Transfer a known volume **V (mL)** of well-mixed sample into the dish.
3. Evaporate to dryness on a steam bath / hot plate, then dry in a hot-air oven at **103 – 105 °C for 1 hour**.
4. Cool in desiccator and weigh (**W₂**).

### TDS Measurement (using filtered sample)
1. Filter a known volume through a **pre-weighed 1.2 µm glass-fiber filter**; residue on filter = TSS.
2. Dry the filter at 103 – 105 °C, cool, and weigh (**W_F**).
3. Evaporate the **filtrate** to dryness, dry at 103 – 105 °C, cool, and weigh (**W_FD**).

### Mathematical Relationship

$$\text{TS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$$

$$\text{TDS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_{FD} - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$$

$$\text{TSS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_F - W_{\text{filter initial}}) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$$

**TSS = TS − TDS** (mass balance check — useful for lab validation).

---

## 2.5 Dissolved Oxygen — Winkler's Iodometric Method

### Theoretical Foundation
This is a **redox titration** that converts dissolved O₂ into an equivalent amount of **iodine (I₂)**, which is then back-titrated with **sodium thiosulfate (Na₂S₂O₃)**.

### The Four Reaction Stages

**Stage 1 — Fixation of O₂:**
$$\text{MnSO}_4 + 2\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow \text{Mn(OH)}_2 \downarrow \text{ (white)} + \text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$

**Stage 2 — Oxidation of Mn(II) to Mn(IV):**
$$2\,\text{Mn(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{MnO(OH)}_2 \downarrow \text{ (brown)}$$

**Stage 3 — Acidification releases I₂:**
$$\text{MnO(OH)}_2 + 2\,\text{KI} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{MnSO}_4 + \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{I}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Stage 4 — Back-titration of I₂ with thiosulfate:**
$$\text{I}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Stoichiometric Link:** 1 mole O₂ ≡ 2 moles MnO(OH)₂ ≡ 2 moles I₂ ≡ 4 moles Na₂S₂O₃
> Therefore, **1 mole Na₂S₂O₃ ≡ 0.25 mole O₂**, and the **equivalent weight of O₂ = 8 g/eq**.

### Calculation
$$\text{DO (mg/L)} = \frac{V \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$$

Where **V** = volume of Na₂S₂O₃ (mL), **N** = normality of Na₂S₂O₃, and **8** = equivalent weight of oxygen (32 ÷ 4 = 8 g/eq).

### Azide Modification (Winkler–Azide)
For samples containing **nitrite ions (NO₂⁻)** (common in biologically active waters), the azide modification is used. Sodium azide (**NaN₃**) destroys nitrite interference by converting it to N₂ gas before the MnSO₄ addition.

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Parameter | Formula | Variables | Units | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | pH | $\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$ | [H⁺] = H⁺ activity | dimensionless | At 25 °C, neutral pH = 7.0 |
| 2 | Nernst Slope | $E = E^0 + \frac{2.303\,RT}{F} \cdot \text{pH}$ | R = 8.314 J/mol·K, T in K, F = 96485 C | volts | Slope = 59.16 mV/pH at 25 °C |
| 3 | Total Hardness (EDTA) | $\text{TH} = \frac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times M_{\text{EDTA}} \times 100 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | V = volume EDTA, M = molarity, 100 = MW of CaCO₃ | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 50 |
| 4 | Total Hardness (Normality form) | $\text{TH} = \frac{V \times N \times 50{,}000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | V = mL EDTA, N = normality EDTA, 50000 = 50 × 1000 | mg/L as CaCO₃ | Quick KTU exam shortcut |
| 5 | Total Solids (TS) | $\text{TS} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | W₂ = dish+residue, W₁ = empty dish | mg/L | Drying at 103–105 °C |
| 6 | TDS | $\text{TDS} = \frac{(W_{FD} - W_1) \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | W_FD = dish + dried filtrate | mg/L | After filtration through 1.2 µm filter |
| 7 | TSS | $\text{TSS} = \text{TS} - \text{TDS}$ | Mass balance check | mg/L | Also obtainable by filter weighing |
| 8 | Dissolved Oxygen (DO) | $\text{DO} = \frac{V_{\text{thio}} \times N_{\text{thio}} \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | V = thiosulfate volume, N = normality, 8 = eq. wt. of O₂ | mg/L O₂ | Winkler's iodometric method |
| 9 | BOD (Related to DO) | $\text{BOD}_5 = D_1 - D_2$ | D₁ = initial DO, D₂ = DO after 5 days at 20 °C | mg/L | Not in this experiment but conceptually linked |
| 10 | % Saturation (DO) | $\%\text{ Sat} = \frac{\text{DO}_{\text{sample}}}{\text{DO}_{\text{saturated at T}}} \times 100$ | Compare with saturation table | % | Depends on temperature & pressure |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Utility:** These parameters are the **first-line indicators** in designing water treatment plants (coagulation, softening, filtration, aeration) and in monitoring effluent discharge compliance for industries such as pharmaceuticals, textiles, food processing, and petroleum refining.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of the Hardness-as-CaCO₃ Conversion

The hardness of water is caused by a mixture of Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺. Reporting this as **CaCO₃** requires converting the actual ion concentrations to their calcium carbonate equivalents based on equivalent weights.

**Step 1 — Express the equivalents of Ca²⁺:**

$$\text{eq. of Ca}^{2+} = \frac{\text{mass of Ca}^{2+}}{\text{eq. wt. of Ca}^{2+}} = \frac{\text{mass of Ca}^{2+}}{20\,\text{g/eq}}$$

Because the **equivalent weight of Ca = atomic mass / valency = 40 / 2 = 20**.

**Step 2 — Express the equivalents of Mg²⁺:**

$$\text{eq. of Mg}^{2+} = \frac{\text{mass of Mg}^{2+}}{12\,\text{g/eq}}$$

Because the **equivalent weight of Mg = 24 / 2 = 12**.

**Step 3 — Total equivalents of hardness cations:**

$$\text{Total eq. of hardness} = \text{eq. of Ca}^{2+} + \text{eq. of Mg}^{2+}$$

**Step 4 — Convert to CaCO₃ equivalent mass:**

$$\text{Mass of CaCO}_3 \text{ equivalent} = \text{Total eq.} \times 50\,\text{g/eq}$$

Where **equivalent weight of CaCO₃ = 100 / 2 = 50 g/eq**.

**Step 5 — Express in mg/L of original water sample:**

$$\text{TH (mg/L as CaCO}_3) = \frac{\text{Total eq.} \times 50{,}000}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$$

The factor **50,000** arises because 50 g/eq × 1000 mg/g = 50,000 mg/eq.

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — Total Hardness Calculation (Numerical)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 50 mL water sample required 18.4 mL of 0.01 M EDTA solution for the complete titration using EBT indicator. Calculate the total hardness in mg/L as CaCO₃.

**Step 1 — Moles of EDTA used:**

$$\text{Moles of EDTA} = M \times V = 0.01\,\text{mol/L} \times 0.0184\,\text{L} = 1.84 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol}$$

**Step 2 — Moles of Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ (= moles of EDTA, due to 1:1 stoichiometry):**

$$\text{Moles of (Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+}) = 1.84 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol}$$

**Step 3 — Mass of CaCO₃ equivalent (MW = 100 g/mol):**

$$\text{Mass of CaCO}_3 = 1.84 \times 10^{-4}\,\text{mol} \times 100\,\text{g/mol} = 0.0184\,\text{g} = 18.4\,\text{mg}$$

**Step 4 — Concentration in mg/L of water sample:**

$$\text{TH} = \frac{18.4\,\text{mg}}{0.050\,\text{L}} = 368\,\text{mg/L as CaCO}_3$$

**Step 5 — Classification:**

Since **TH = 368 mg/L > 180 mg/L**, the water is classified as **"Very Hard"**.

**Alternative Quick Formula Verification:**

$$\text{TH} = \frac{V \times M \times 100 \times 1000}{V_{\text{sample}}} = \frac{18.4 \times 0.01 \times 100 \times 1000}{50} = 368\,\text{mg/L} \quad \checkmark$$

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Winkler's DO Determination Procedure

**Step 1 — Sample Collection (Critical Step):**
Collect the water sample in a **300 mL BOD bottle** carefully, avoiding any air bubble entrainment. Add **2 mL of MnSO₄ solution** followed by **2 mL of alkali-iodide-azide reagent** at the surface by dipping the pipette tip just below the water level.

**Step 2 — Fixation of Oxygen:**
Stopper the bottle carefully (no air bubbles). Invert the bottle several times. A **brown precipitate** of MnO(OH)₂ forms. The intensity of brown color is **proportional to DO content**.

**Step 3 — Acidification:**
After the precipitate settles, add **2 mL of concentrated H₂SO₄** carefully. The brown precipitate dissolves, releasing **free iodine (I₂)** which turns the solution **yellow to brown** depending on DO.

**Step 4 — Back-Titration:**
Pipette out **200 mL** of this acidified solution into an Erlenmeyer flask. Titrate against **0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃** solution until a **pale straw-yellow** color remains. Add **1–2 mL of starch indicator** — the solution turns **deep blue**. Continue titration drop-by-drop until the **blue color just disappears** (endpoint). Record volume **V** of thiosulfate used.

**Step 5 — Calculation:**

$$\text{DO (mg/L)} = \frac{V_{\text{thio}} \times N_{\text{thio}} \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{\text{aliquot (mL)}}}$$

Note: Since the **whole bottle** (300 mL) was acidified but only **200 mL** was titrated, the DO result is **per 200 mL aliquot**, which equals the DO of the original sample (because DO is an intensive property, not extensive).

---

## 3.4 Worked Example — Dissolved Oxygen Calculation (Numerical)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 200 mL aliquot of acidified Winkler-treated water sample required 12.6 mL of 0.025 N sodium thiosulfate solution. Calculate the DO of the original sample in mg/L.

**Step 1 — Apply the formula:**

$$\text{DO} = \frac{V \times N \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{\text{aliquot}}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$\text{DO} = \frac{12.6 \times 0.025 \times 8 \times 1000}{200}$$

**Step 3 — Compute step by step:**

$$\text{Numerator} = 12.6 \times 0.025 = 0.315$$
$$0.315 \times 8 = 2.52$$
$$2.52 \times 1000 = 2520$$
$$\text{DO} = \frac{2520}{200} = 12.6\,\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 4 — Verify the water quality:**

Since **DO = 12.6 mg/L ≥ 6 mg/L**, the water is **well-oxygenated** and capable of supporting a healthy aquatic ecosystem.

---

## 3.5 Step-by-Step Total Solids Determination Procedure

**Step 1 — Evaporating Dish Preparation:**
- Wash a clean evaporating dish (or crucible).
- Heat in hot-air oven at **103 – 105 °C for 1 hour**.
- Cool in **desiccator** for 30 minutes.
- Weigh to a constant mass: **W₁ (g)**.

**Step 2 — Sample Transfer:**
- Stir the water sample thoroughly to ensure homogeneity.
- Measure **V mL** (typically 25 – 100 mL) using a graduated cylinder.
- Transfer into the pre-weighed dish.

**Step 3 — Evaporation:**
- Place the dish on a **steam bath** or hot plate in a fume hood.
- Allow the sample to evaporate **without boiling** (to prevent splashing losses).
- Continue until the sample is completely dry.

**Step 4 — Drying in Oven:**
- Transfer the dish to a **hot-air oven at 103 – 105 °C for 1 hour**.
- Cool in the **desiccator** to prevent moisture absorption.
- Weigh: **W₂ (g)**.

**Step 5 — Calculation:**

$$\text{TS (mg/L)} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 1000 \times 1000}{V_{\text{mL}}}$$

The factor 1000 × 1000 converts grams to mg and normalizes per liter of sample.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mass Balance Cross-Check:** For higher accuracy, run **TSS** (filter weight before–after) and **TDS** (evaporate filtrate) in parallel. Always verify that **TS = TDS + TSS**. A deviation > 5% indicates experimental error.

---

## 3.6 Worked Example — Total Solids Calculation (Numerical)

**Problem (KTU-style):** A 100 mL water sample was evaporated in a pre-weighed dish (W₁ = 47.8623 g). After drying at 105 °C, the dish + residue weighed W₂ = 47.9148 g. Calculate the TS in mg/L.

**Step 1 — Mass of residue:**

$$\Delta W = W_2 - W_1 = 47.9148 - 47.8623 = 0.0525\,\text{g} = 52.5\,\text{mg}$$

**Step 2 — Concentration in mg/L:**

$$\text{TS} = \frac{52.5\,\text{mg}}{0.100\,\text{L}} = 525\,\text{mg/L}$$

**Step 3 — Interpretation:**

Since **TDS = 525 mg/L > 500 mg/L** (BIS acceptable limit), the water is **slightly above the desirable limit** but still within the **permissible limit of 2000 mg/L**. Acceptable for drinking with minor treatment.

---

## 3.7 Turbidity Calibration & Measurement (Step-by-Step)

**Step 1 — Preparation of Formazin Stock Standard (4000 NTU):**
- Dissolve **1.000 g of hydrazine sulfate** in distilled water.
- Dissolve **10.000 g of hexamethylenetetramine** in distilled water.
- Mix the two, dilute to 100 mL, and let stand for **24 hours at 25 °C ± 3 °C**.

**Step 2 — Dilution to Working Standards:**
Prepare standards of 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 NTU by appropriate dilution of the 4000 NTU stock.

**Step 3 — Nephelometer Calibration:**
- Pour standard into the **clean, dry sample cell** (no fingerprints on the glass).
- Insert into the nephelometer.
- Adjust the reading to match the standard's NTU value.

**Step 4 — Sample Measurement:**
- Rinse the cell with the sample, then fill it.
- Place in nephelometer and read NTU directly (or via calibration curve).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfall:** Bubbles on the cell wall cause spurious high readings. Always tap the cell gently and let it sit for 30 seconds before reading.

---

## 3.8 Consolidated Analytical Matrix

| Parameter | Reagent(s) | Indicator | Endpoint Color | Key Formula | Final Units |
|-----------|-----------|-----------|----------------|-------------|-------------|
| pH | Buffer solutions pH 4, 7, 9.2 | — (electrode) | — (digital readout) | $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$ | dimensionless |
| Turbidity | Formazin standard | — (photo-detector) | — (NTU readout) | Calibrated against 4000 NTU stock | NTU |
| Total Hardness | EDTA (0.01 M) | Eriochrome Black T | Wine-red → Sky blue | $\text{TH} = \frac{V \times M \times 10^5}{V_{\text{sample}}}$ | mg/L as CaCO₃ |
| Total Solids | — (gravimetric) | — (mass balance) | Constant weight | $\text{TS} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 10^6}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$ | mg/L |
| Dissolved Oxygen | MnSO₄, KI, H₂SO₄, Na₂S₂O₃ | Starch (1%) | Blue → Colorless | $\text{DO} = \frac{V \times N \times 8 \times 10^3}{V_{\text{aliquot}}}$ | mg/L O₂ |

# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of the Water Quality Analysis Workflow

This Mermaid flowchart visualizes the complete experimental workflow — from sample collection to final data interpretation — used in the KTU Module Experiment 2.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    sampleA[Raw Water Sample] --> sampleQ{Sufficient Volume 500 mL?}
    sampleQ -->|No| sampleR[Collect More Sample]
    sampleR --> sampleA
    sampleQ -->|Yes| sampleB[Subdivide into 5 Aliquots]

    sampleB --> para1[Aliquot 1 pH Determination]
    sampleB --> para2[Aliquot 2 Turbidity Determination]
    sampleB --> para3[Aliquot 3 Total Hardness]
    sampleB --> para4[Aliquot 4 Total Solids]
    sampleB --> para5[Aliquot 5 Dissolved Oxygen]

    para1 --> ph1[Calibrate pH Meter with Buffers pH 4 and pH 7]
    ph1 --> ph2[Rinse Electrode Dip into Sample]
    ph2 --> ph3[Record Stable pH Reading]

    para2 --> tur1[Calibrate Nephelometer with Formazin Standards]
    tur1 --> tur2[Rinse Cuvette Fill with Sample]
    tur2 --> tur3[Record NTU Reading]

    para3 --> har1[Add Buffer pH 10 NH3 NH4Cl]
    har1 --> har2[Add Eriochrome Black T Indicator]
    har2 --> har3[Titrate with 0.01 M EDTA]
    har3 --> har4[Endpoint Color Change Wine Red to Sky Blue]
    har4 --> har5[Record Volume of EDTA and Calculate TH]

    para4 --> sol1[Weigh Empty Evaporating Dish W1]
    sol1 --> sol2[Transfer 100 mL Sample and Evaporate]
    sol2 --> sol3[Dry in Oven at 103 to 105 C for 1 Hour]
    sol3 --> sol4[Cool in Desiccator and Weigh W2]
    sol4 --> sol5[Calculate TS in mg per L]

    para5 --> do1[Collect in BOD Bottle Add MnSO4 and Alkali KI]
    do1 --> do2[Brown Precipitate Indicates O2 Fixation]
    do2 --> do3[Acidify with H2SO4 Liberate I2]
    do3 --> do4[Titrate 200 mL with 0.025 N Na2S2O3]
    do4 --> do5[Starch Indicator Endpoint Blue to Colorless]
    do5 --> do6[Record V and Calculate DO in mg per L]

    ph3 --> finalR[Compile Results Tabulate Compare with BIS Standards]
    tur3 --> finalR
    har5 --> finalR
    sol5 --> finalR
    do6 --> finalR

    finalR --> report1[Plot Bar Graph of 5 Parameters]
    finalR --> report2[Write Lab Report with Discussions]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** The flowchart is read top-to-bottom. The **central decision diamond** ensures adequate sample volume before subdivision. The five parallel branches represent the five independent analytical procedures. All branches converge to a final **reporting and interpretation** step — the most critical part of any KTU lab record.

---

## 4.2 Block Architecture of the Winkler DO Method (Subsystem Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    stage1[Stage 1 MnSO4 Addition] --> stage2[Stage 2 O2 Fixation MnO OH 2]
    stage2 --> stage3[Stage 3 Acidification H2SO4 Liberates I2]
    stage3 --> stage4[Stage 4 Thiosulfate Titration of I2]
    stage4 --> stage5[Stage 5 Starch Endpoint Blue to Colorless]
    stage5 --> stage6[Compute DO Using Formula]
```

## 4.3 EDTA Hardness Titration Sequence (Subsystem Detail)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    prep1[Weigh Water Sample 50 mL] --> prep2[Add 1 mL Buffer pH 10]
    prep2 --> prep3[Add EBT Indicator Wine Red Color]
    prep3 --> tit1[Titrate with 0.01 M EDTA]
    tit1 --> dec{Endpoint Sky Blue Reached?}
    dec -->|No| tit1
    dec -->|Yes| rec[Record Volume V of EDTA]
    rec --> calc[Calculate TH as mg per L CaCO3]
```

> [!TIP]
> **Visualization Note:** In an actual KTU lab examination, students should be able to draw both the **experimental setup** (BOD bottle, burette, conical flask, pH meter) and a **flowchart** of the procedure. The Mermaid diagrams above mimic the expected block-diagram representation. Note the deliberate use of simple alphanumeric node labels and plain-text labels to comply with mermaid syntax safety rules.

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1: Define pH. Mention the BIS acceptable range of pH for drinking water. Why is pH 7 called "neutral" only at 25 °C?**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**pH Definition:** pH is the negative base-10 logarithm of the hydrogen ion concentration (activity) in an aqueous solution.

$$\text{pH} = -\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$$

**BIS Range:** As per **IS 10500:2012**, the acceptable pH range for drinking water is **6.5 to 8.5**.

**Why neutral only at 25 °C:** Pure water is neutral when [H⁺] = [OH⁻]. The ionic product of water K_w is **temperature-dependent**:

$$\text{pH}_{\text{neutral}} = \frac{1}{2}\,\text{p}K_w$$

- At **5 °C**: pK_w ≈ 14.73 → pH neutral = **7.37** (basic in absolute sense)
- At **25 °C**: pK_w = 14.00 → pH neutral = **7.00**
- At **50 °C**: pK_w ≈ 13.26 → pH neutral = **6.63** (acidic in absolute sense)

So the value 7 is "neutral" only at the conventional reference temperature of 25 °C.

---

### **Question 2: Differentiate between Total Solids (TS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), and Total Suspended Solids (TSS). Give the BIS limit for TDS in drinking water.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Definition | Measurement Method | BIS Limit |
|-----------|-----------|-------------------|-----------|
| **TS** | Total residue after evaporation of unfiltered sample | Drying at 103 – 105 °C | — |
| **TDS** | Residue after evaporation of **filtered** sample (passes 1.2 µm filter) | Drying filtrate at 103 – 105 °C | ≤ 500 mg/L (acceptable); ≤ 2000 mg/L (permissible) |
| **TSS** | Residue **retained on the filter** | Drying filter + residue at 103 – 105 °C | ≤ 100 mg/L (effluent discharge) |

**Key Relationship:** **TS = TDS + TSS**

**Significance:**
- **High TDS** → brackish taste, scale in boilers, reduced crop yield
- **High TSS** → turbid appearance, harbor pathogens, clog filters
- **TS** → total pollution load indicator

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Module Internal Choice questions carry 14 marks each, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with KTU expecting descriptive answers, neat diagrams, and step-by-step calculations. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)**

#### **Part (a): Explain the EDTA complexometric method for the determination of total hardness of water in detail. Include the principle, reagents, procedure, calculation, and significance. (7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
EDTA (ethylenediaminetetraacetic acid) is a hexadentate ligand that forms a stable 1:1 soluble complex with Ca²⁺ and Mg²⁺ ions at pH 10.0 ± 0.1. Since the reaction is 1:1, the moles of Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ in the sample equal the moles of EDTA consumed, and the result is expressed as the equivalent CaCO₃.

$$\text{Ca}^{2+} + \text{Mg}^{2+} + 2\,\text{EDTA}^{4-} \rightarrow [\text{Ca–EDTA}]^{2-} + [\text{Mg–EDTA}]^{2-}$$

**2. Reagents Required (1 mark):**
- Standard EDTA solution (0.01 M)
- NH₃/NH₄Cl buffer (pH 10.0)
- Eriochrome Black T (EBT) indicator powder/tablet
- Standard CaCO₃ solution (for EDTA standardization)

**3. Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Pipette 50 mL of water sample into a clean conical flask.
2. Add 1 – 2 mL of NH₃/NH₄Cl buffer (pH 10) — solution turns slightly blue.
3. Add a pinch of EBT indicator — solution turns **wine-red** (Ca²⁺/Mg²⁺–EBT complex).
4. Titrate with 0.01 M EDTA from a burette, swirling continuously.
5. Endpoint: Color changes sharply from **wine-red → sky blue**.
6. Record the volume of EDTA used.

**4. Calculation (1 mark):**

$$\text{TH (mg/L as CaCO}_3) = \frac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times M_{\text{EDTA}} \times 100{,}000}{V_{\text{sample}}}$$

**5. Significance (1 mark):**
- **Boilers:** Prevents scale formation (CaCO₃ has low thermal conductivity, causes tube overheating).
- **Domestic:** Reduces soap consumption; prevents scaling in pipes and geysers.
- **Industrial:** Essential for textile, dyeing, food, and pharmaceutical industries.

---

#### **Part (b): Describe Winkler's iodometric method for the determination of dissolved oxygen in water. Give the reactions, procedure, and formula. (7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Principle (2 marks):**
Winkler's method is a redox titration in which dissolved O₂ first oxidizes Mn²⁺ to Mn⁴⁺ (MnO(OH)₂), which upon acidification liberates free I₂ from KI in equivalent amount. The liberated I₂ is then back-titrated with standard Na₂S₂O₃ using starch indicator.

**2. Reactions (2 marks):**

**Stage 1 — O₂ fixation:**
$$2\,\text{MnSO}_4 + 4\,\text{NaOH} \rightarrow 2\,\text{Mn(OH)}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{SO}_4$$
$$2\,\text{Mn(OH)}_2 + \text{O}_2 \rightarrow 2\,\text{MnO(OH)}_2 \downarrow$$

**Stage 2 — I₂ liberation:**
$$\text{MnO(OH)}_2 + 2\,\text{KI} + 2\,\text{H}_2\text{SO}_4 \rightarrow \text{MnSO}_4 + \text{K}_2\text{SO}_4 + \text{I}_2 + 3\,\text{H}_2\text{O}$$

**Stage 3 — Thiosulfate titration:**
$$\text{I}_2 + 2\,\text{Na}_2\text{S}_2\text{O}_3 \rightarrow 2\,\text{NaI} + \text{Na}_2\text{S}_4\text{O}_6$$

**3. Procedure (2 marks):**
1. Fill a **300 mL BOD bottle** carefully (no air bubbles).
2. Add **2 mL MnSO₄** + **2 mL alkali-iodide-azide** reagent; stopper; invert → brown precipitate.
3. After settling, add **2 mL conc. H₂SO₄**; brown precipitate dissolves → yellow-brown I₂.
4. Pipette **200 mL** of this solution; titrate with **0.025 N Na₂S₂O₃** to pale straw color.
5. Add **1 mL starch** indicator → blue color.
6. Continue titration until **blue color disappears** (endpoint).
7. Record volume **V**.

**4. Calculation (1 mark):**

$$\text{DO (mg/L)} = \frac{V_{\text{thio}} \times N_{\text{thio}} \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{\text{aliquot (mL)}}}$$

**Significance:** DO is a primary indicator of **water body health**, **BOD determination**, and **aquatic ecosystem viability**.

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks)** *(Alternative Choice)*

#### **Part (a): Explain the determination of Total Solids (TS), Total Dissolved Solids (TDS), and Total Suspended Solids (TSS) in a water sample. Give the procedure, formulas, and BIS limits. (7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. Definitions and Significance (1 mark):**
- **TS** = entire residue after evaporation of an unfiltered sample.
- **TDS** = residue from the filtrate (dissolved fraction only).
- **TSS** = residue retained on the filter (suspended fraction only).
- Relationship: **TS = TDS + TSS**

**2. Apparatus (1 mark):**
- Evaporating dishes (porcelain or borosilicate)
- Hot-air oven (103 – 105 °C)
- Desiccator with silica gel / CaCl₂ desiccant
- Analytical balance (0.0001 g accuracy)
- Glass-fiber filter (1.2 µm pore size) and filtration assembly
- Graduated cylinder, pipettes, beakers

**3. Procedure for TS (2 marks):**
1. Clean and dry evaporating dish in oven at 105 °C for 1 hr.
2. Cool in desiccator (30 min) → weigh to constant mass **W₁**.
3. Pipette **V mL** (50 – 100 mL) of well-mixed sample into dish.
4. Evaporate on steam bath (no boiling) → transfer to oven at 105 °C for 1 hr.
5. Cool in desiccator → weigh **W₂**.

**4. Procedure for TDS and TSS (1.5 marks):**
- **TSS:** Filter a separate **V mL** sample through a pre-weighed 1.2 µm filter; dry filter + residue at 105 °C; weigh. TSS mass = (Final weight − Initial filter weight).
- **TDS:** Evaporate the **filtrate** in a pre-weighed dish; dry at 105 °C; weigh.

**5. Formulas (1 mark):**

$$\text{TS} = \frac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 10^6}{V_{\text{mL}}}$$
$$\text{TSS} = \frac{W_{\text{final filter}} - W_{\text{initial filter}}}{V_{\text{sample (L)}}}$$
$$\text{TDS} = \text{TS} - \text{TSS}$$

**6. BIS Limits (0.5 mark):**
- TS: No specific limit (computed)
- TDS: ≤ 500 mg/L (acceptable); ≤ 2000 mg/L (permissible)
- TSS: ≤ 100 mg/L (industrial effluent discharge)

---

#### **Part (b): Define pH and turbidity. Explain how they are measured. Discuss the significance of each in water quality assessment. (7 marks)**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

**1. pH — Definition and Measurement (3 marks):**

**Definition:** pH is the negative logarithm of H⁺ ion concentration.

**Measurement Methods:**
- **(a) Electrometric (pH meter)** — most accurate; uses a glass electrode and a reference electrode (calomel or Ag/AgCl). The EMF across the glass membrane is proportional to pH (Nernst equation, slope 59.16 mV/pH at 25 °C). The instrument is calibrated with two buffer solutions (pH 7 and pH 4 or 9.2) before use.
- **(b) Colorimetric** — using universal indicator solution/paper; less accurate (±0.2 – 0.5 pH units) but useful for field tests.

**Significance:** pH controls:
- The **solubility** of metals and gases in water (low pH → heavy metals soluble → toxic).
- The effectiveness of **chlorination** (most effective at pH 6.5 – 7.5).
- The **corrosivity** of water (low pH attacks pipes; high pH causes scaling).
- **Aquatic life** — most fish survive in pH 6.5 – 9.0.

**2. Turbidity — Definition and Measurement (3 marks):**

**Definition:** Turbidity is the cloudiness or haziness of water caused by the presence of finely divided suspended and colloidal particles (clay, silt, organic matter, algae, microorganisms), measured in **NTU (Nephelometric Turbidity Units)**.

**Measurement Method — Nephelometer:**
- A nephelometer directs a beam of light (typically 860 nm near-IR) through the sample.
- A photodetector placed at **90°** to the incident beam measures the intensity of scattered light.
- Higher scattered intensity → higher turbidity.
- The instrument is calibrated using **Formazin polymer standards** (4000 NTU stock diluted to 0, 5, 10, 20, 50, 100 NTU).

**Significance:**
- **High turbidity shields pathogens** from disinfection (UV, chlorine).
- It is a **surrogate indicator** of microbiological contamination.
- Causes **aesthetic issues** — consumers reject turbid water.
- Indicates **inadequate filtration** in water treatment plants.
- BIS limit: **≤ 1 NTU (ideal), ≤ 5 NTU (max permissible)**.

**3. Comparative Significance (1 mark):**
Both pH and turbidity are **first-stage indicators** in water quality testing. They are quick, easy, and inexpensive, providing the **first warning** of contamination before more detailed chemical/biological analyses are undertaken. Together, they form the **initial screening protocol** for any water treatment facility.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls (Lose Marks Here!)**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to convert volumes to liters** in calculations — A frequent, costly mistake. Always show the unit conversion step explicitly.
> 2. **Confusing the equivalent weight of CaCO₃ (50) with its molecular weight (100)** — KTU examiners specifically check this. Always state that eq. wt. = MW / valency = 100 / 2 = 50.
> 3. **Using the wrong factor in DO calculation** — Some students forget the ×1000 (mg/L conversion) or the factor of **8** (equivalent weight of O₂). Show every step!
> 4. **Skipping the buffer role in EDTA titration** — Examiners want to know *why* pH 10 is essential (low pH: H⁺ competes; high pH: Ca(OH)₂ precipitates).
> 5. **Neglecting to write the four reactions in Winkler's method** — All four must be in sequence; partial writing = partial marks.
> 6. **Forgetting azide modification** — For samples with nitrite interference (sewage, biological wastewater), the **azide modification** is mandatory. Examiners often test this.
> 7. **In TS measurement, "boiling" instead of "evaporating"** — Boiling causes splashing losses and gives falsely low values. Steam bath evaporation is correct.
> 8. **No mention of desiccator use** — Hot samples absorb atmospheric moisture rapidly, leading to high erroneous weights. Always cool in a desiccator.

---

## 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER (Rapid Revision Checklist)

### 🎯 Critical Definitions (Must Memorize)
- ✅ **pH** = $-\log_{10}[\text{H}^+]$; at 25 °C, neutral = 7.0; range 0 – 14
- ✅ **Turbidity** = cloudiness from suspended particles; measured in **NTU**
- ✅ **Total Hardness** = Ca²⁺ + Mg²⁺ content, expressed as **mg/L CaCO₃**
- ✅ **TS** = TDS + TSS (mass balance identity)
- ✅ **DO** = dissolved O₂ in water; healthy ≥ 6 mg/L

### 🎯 Critical Formulas (Must Know)
- ✅ $\text{pH} = -\log[\text{H}^+]$
- ✅ $\text{TH (mg/L)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{EDTA}} \times M_{\text{EDTA}} \times 100{,}000}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$
- ✅ $\text{TS (mg/L)} = \dfrac{(W_2 - W_1) \times 10^6}{V_{\text{sample (mL)}}}$
- ✅ $\text{DO (mg/L)} = \dfrac{V_{\text{thio}} \times N_{\text{thio}} \times 8 \times 1000}{V_{\text{aliquot (mL)}}}$

### 🎯 Critical Reagents & Indicators
- ✅ **EDTA (0.01 M)** + **Eriochrome Black T (EBT)** for hardness; endpoint = **wine-red → sky blue**
- ✅ **MnSO₄ + alkali-iodide-azide + H₂SO₄ + Na₂S₂O₃ + starch** for DO; endpoint = **blue → colorless**
- ✅ **Formazin polymer** as turbidity standard (4000 NTU stock)
- ✅ **Buffer pH 10 (NH₃/NH₄Cl)** for hardness titration
- ✅ **Starch indicator** added near the endpoint (when pale yellow) to avoid starch–I₂ coagulation

### 🎯 Critical Procedure Steps (Must Mention in Lab Exam)
- ✅ Rinse **electrode** with distilled water (don't wipe — blot)
- ✅ Calibrate pH meter with **two buffers** (slope and standardize)
- ✅ Avoid **air bubbles** in BOD bottle during DO sample collection
- ✅ Cool all hot glassware in **desiccator** before weighing
- ✅ Evaporate (not boil) sample for TS determination
- ✅ Use **NH₃/NH₄Cl buffer** before EBT indicator in hardness titration
- ✅ For nitrite interference in DO, use **azide modification**

### 🎯 Critical Standards to Recall
- ✅ **BIS (IS 10500:2012) Drinking Water Limits:**
  - pH: 6.5 – 8.5
  - Turbidity: ≤ 1 NTU (ideal), ≤ 5 NTU (max)
  - Total Hardness: ≤ 200 mg/L
  - TDS: ≤ 500 mg/L (acceptable), ≤ 2000 mg/L (permissible)
  - DO: ≥ 6 mg/L (healthy aquatic life)

### 🎯 Common Mistake Patterns to Avoid
- ❌ Forgetting to mention the **stoichiometric ratio (1:1)** in EDTA and (1:4) in thiosulfate
- ❌ Using **MW of CaCO₃ (100)** instead of **eq. wt. (50)** in calculations
- ❌ Reporting turbidity in **ppm** (deprecated — use NTU)
- ❌ Mixing up **% saturation** vs. **absolute DO** (mg/L)
- ❌ Forgetting that **temperature affects pH neutrality** and **DO saturation**

### 🎯 Cross-Connections to Remember
- 🔗 **Hardness ↔ Scaling** (boilers, heat exchangers, cooling towers)
- 🔗 **Turbidity ↔ Disinfection efficacy** (high turbidity → chlorine demand ↑)
- 🔗 **DO ↔ BOD** (BOD₅ = DO_initial − DO_5days at 20 °C)
- 🔗 **TDS ↔ Conductivity** (TDS ≈ 0.55 × conductivity in µS/cm for natural waters)
- 🔗 **pH ↔ Coagulation** (optimum alum coagulation at pH 6.8 – 7.5)

### 🎯 High-Yield Numerical Practice Tips
- 💡 Always show the **unit conversion** (mL → L, g → mg) explicitly
- 💡 Maintain at least **3 significant figures** in final answers
- 💡 Include **blank correction** in gravimetric analysis
- 💡 For DO: if 200 mL aliquot is titrated, the DO result applies to the whole sample
- 💡 For hardness: report as **"mg/L as CaCO₃"** — never as "mg/L of Ca²⁺"

> [!TIP]
> **Final KTU Exam Strategy:** Always draw a **neat labeled diagram** (e.g., BOD bottle setup, burette arrangement) before writing the procedure. Examiners award **1–2 marks** for proper diagrams. End every answer with a **one-line significance/industrial relevance** statement — it is a guaranteed **bonus mark** that students often miss!

---

**END OF KTU MODULE EXPERIMENT 2 NOTES**
*Module: Determination of pH, turbidity, total hardness, total solids and dissolved oxygen of water samples*

topics.title: Determination of BOD and COD of waste water samples
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PCSFL508 - CHEMICAL ENGINEERING LAB
> - **Module:** Module Experiment 2
> - **Topic:** Determination of BOD and COD of waste water samples

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Water Quality Parameters

Water quality assessment is a foundational pillar of environmental engineering and industrial effluent management. Among the many analytical parameters used by chemists and environmental engineers worldwide, **Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** and **Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** are the two most critical indicators of organic pollution in water bodies.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (PCSFL508):** This experiment belongs to **Module 2 – Wastewater Characterization**. Students are expected to understand both the experimental determination and the underlying chemistry of oxygen-consuming reactions in wastewater.

## 1.2 Definition of BOD (Biochemical Oxygen Demand)

**Biochemical Oxygen Demand (BOD)** is formally defined as the **amount of dissolved oxygen (DO) required by aerobic microorganisms to biologically oxidize the organic matter present in a water sample under controlled conditions of temperature (20°C) and time (5 days)**.

The standard test is therefore universally referred to as **BOD₅** — the 5-day BOD measured at 20°C.

$$BOD_5 = \frac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2) \times f}{P}$$

Where each variable is defined later in the formula sheet (Section 2).

## 1.3 Definition of COD (Chemical Oxygen Demand)

**Chemical Oxygen Demand (COD)** is defined as the **amount of oxygen equivalent required to chemically oxidize the organic (and some inorganic) matter present in a water sample using a strong chemical oxidizing agent** (typically potassium dichromate, K₂Cr₂O₇) under acidic conditions.

$$COD \, (mg/L) = \frac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Concept Clarification:** COD measures **all chemically oxidizable matter** (both biodegradable and non-biodegradable), whereas BOD measures only the **biodegradable organic fraction**. Hence, **COD is always greater than or equal to BOD** for the same sample.

## 1.4 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Imagine a river flowing through a city. As it passes, it carries with it organic waste from households, food scraps, and industrial effluents. Now picture the river as a giant "lung" and the organic waste as "dirt clogging the lung."

- **BOD** is like measuring **how much air (oxygen) the river's tiny clean-up crew (aerobic bacteria) will gulp down over 5 days** while trying to digest the organic waste. A high BOD = a lot of waste = the river is suffocating.

- **COD** is like measuring **how much "dirt" the river contains in total**, by burning it chemically with a powerful oxidizer (dichromate). A high COD = a lot of stuff that *could* consume oxygen — including the biodegradable portion AND the non-biodegradable poisons.

> [!TIP]
> **Why do we need both?** Think of BOD as a "living" measurement and COD as a "total load" measurement. The ratio **COD/BOD** tells engineers how **biodegradable** the waste is:
> - Ratio ≈ 1.7 → Domestic sewage (easily biodegradable)
> - Ratio > 3.0 → Industrial effluent (often toxic / non-biodegradable)

## 1.5 Why BOD and COD Matter in Chemical Engineering

For a chemical engineer working in wastewater treatment plants, pharmaceutical industries, petrochemical refineries, or fertilizer plants, determining BOD and COD is essential because:

1. **Designing biological treatment units** (activated sludge, trickling filters, oxidation ponds) requires accurate BOD values to size aeration tanks.
2. **Monitoring regulatory compliance** — industries must discharge effluent with BOD < 30 mg/L and COD < 250 mg/L (per Indian CPCB/SPCB norms) into water bodies.
3. **Process control** in effluent treatment plants (ETPs) and common effluent treatment plants (CETPs).
4. **Assessing treatment efficiency** by measuring inlet and outlet BOD/COD to compute percentage removal.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Chemistry Behind BOD

### 2.1.1 Aerobic Oxidation Reaction

When organic matter (represented as C₆H₁₂O₆, glucose — a model substrate) is biodegraded by aerobic bacteria:

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 6 \, O_2 \xrightarrow{\text{aerobic bacteria}} 6 \, CO_2 + 6 \, H_2O + \text{energy}$$

**Key Insight:** For every mole of glucose, **6 moles of O₂** are consumed. The bacteria use this oxygen to "burn" the organic matter and release energy for their metabolism (cell growth + respiration).

### 2.1.2 The Role of Dissolved Oxygen (DO)

The DO is the limiting reactant in natural waters. If organic loading is high, dissolved oxygen plummets → fish and aquatic life suffocate → **eutrophication** and **fish kills** occur.

> [!NOTE]
> **Source of DO in water:** Atmospheric dissolution (slow), photosynthesis by algae (intermittent), and mechanical aeration (engineered). Cold water holds more DO than warm water. At 20°C, saturation DO ≈ 9.2 mg/L.

### 2.1.3 Why 5 Days?

The 5-day incubation period was historically chosen by the **Royal Commission on Sewage Disposal (UK, 1908)** because:
- Most biodegradable organic matter is oxidized within 5 days
- Results are obtainable in a useful timeframe for engineering decisions
- The ratio BOD₅ / BODₐ (ultimate BOD) is approximately 0.68 for domestic sewage

The ultimate BOD is given by:

$$BOD_u = \frac{L_0 \, (1 - e^{-k \cdot t})}{1}$$

Where:
- $L_0$ = ultimate carbonaceous oxygen demand (mg/L)
- $k$ = deoxygenation rate constant (day⁻¹), typically 0.23/day at 20°C for domestic sewage
- $t$ = time in days

## 2.2 The Chemistry Behind COD

### 2.2.1 The Dichromate Reflux Method (Standard Method 5220)

The dichromate ion ($Cr_2O_7^{2-}$) in strong sulfuric acid acts as a powerful oxidizing agent:

$$Cr_2O_7^{2-} + 14 \, H^+ + 6 \, e^- \rightarrow 2 \, Cr^{3+} + 7 \, H_2O$$

Each dichromate ion accepts **6 electrons**, accepting oxygen equivalents from the organic matter. The amount of dichromate consumed is proportional to the COD.

### 2.2.2 Overall Reaction for Glucose

$$C_6H_{12}O_6 + 4 \, Cr_2O_7^{2-} + 32 \, H^+ \rightarrow 6 \, CO_2 + 8 \, Cr^{3+} + 22 \, H_2O$$

Here, 4 dichromate ions (= 24 electrons) oxidize 1 glucose, equivalent to **8 moles of O₂** per mole of glucose (theoretically maximum).

### 2.2.3 The Catalyst and Interferences

- **Silver sulfate (Ag₂SO₄)** is added as a catalyst to oxidize straight-chain aliphatic compounds.
- **Mercuric sulfate (HgSO₄)** is added to complex chloride ions ($Cl^-$), preventing chloride interference (since $Cl^-$ is also oxidized by dichromate).

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Error:** Adding mercury salts is restricted in many countries due to environmental concerns. Always check whether your lab uses Hg-free or Hg-containing COD reagent.

## 2.3 Experimental Procedure — BOD (Winkler's Method)

### Step 1: Sample Preparation
- If sample BOD is expected > 7 mg/L, **dilute** with oxygen-saturated dilution water.
- Dilution water is seeded with buffer (phosphate buffer pH 7.2), MgSO₄, CaCl₂, FeCl₃, and acclimated bacterial seed.

### Step 2: Initial DO Measurement (D₁)
- Fill a BOD bottle (300 mL) with diluted sample.
- Determine DO immediately using Winkler's iodometric method → this is **D₁**.

### Step 3: Incubation
- Seal bottle and incubate at **20°C ± 1°C for 5 days** in a BOD incubator.

### Step 4: Final DO Measurement (D₂)
- After 5 days, measure DO again → this is **D₂**.

## 2.4 Experimental Procedure — COD (Open Reflux / Closed Reflux)

### Step 1: Digestion
- Take **V = 20 mL** of sample in a reflux flask.
- Add **10 mL of 0.25 N K₂Cr₂O₇** and **30 mL of H₂SO₄** (with Ag₂SO₄).
- Reflux for **2 hours** at 150°C.

### Step 2: Titration
- Cool, dilute, add ferroin indicator.
- Titrate excess dichromate with **Ferrous Ammonium Sulfate (FAS), 0.1 N** until color changes from blue-green to reddish-brown.

### Step 3: Blank Titration
- Repeat the entire procedure with **distilled water** instead of sample → gives blank reading **A**.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU exam question on this topic.**

| # | Formula / Concept | Mathematical Expression | Variables & Units | Applicable Test |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | BOD₅ (with seed correction) | $\dfrac{(D_1 - D_2) - (B_1 - B_2)f}{P}$ | $D_1, D_2$ = DO of sample; $B_1, B_2$ = DO of seed blank; $f$ = seed fraction; $P$ = sample fraction | BOD |
| 2 | BOD₅ (no seed) | $\dfrac{D_1 - D_2}{P}$ | When seeded dilution water not used | BOD |
| 3 | Dilution Factor (P) | $\dfrac{V_{sample}}{V_{sample} + V_{dilution \, water}}$ | Decimal fraction | BOD |
| 4 | COD | $\dfrac{(A - B) \times N \times 8000}{V}$ | $A$ = blank titer (mL); $B$ = sample titer (mL); $N$ = Normality of FAS; $V$ = sample volume (mL); 8000 = meq wt of O₂ × 1000 | COD |
| 5 | Seed Correction Factor | $f = \dfrac{\% \, seed \, in \, dilution \, water}{\% \, seed \, in \, B_1}$ | Ratio of seed volumes | BOD |
| 6 | Winkler's DO | $DO = \dfrac{(V_2 - V_1) \times N \times 8000}{V_{bottle}}$ | $V_1$ = sample titer; $V_2$ = blank titer | DO |
| 7 | Theoretical O₂ for Glucose | $8 \, \text{moles} \, O_2 \, / \, \text{mole} \, C_6H_{12}O_6$ | Complete oxidation | Stoichiometry |
| 8 | COD/BOD Ratio | $\dfrac{COD}{BOD_5}$ | Indicator of biodegradability | Comparison |
| 9 | Ultimate BOD | $L_t = L_0 \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$ | $k$ = rate constant (day⁻¹); $L_0$ = ultimate demand | Kinetics |
| 10 | Temperature Correction for k | $k_T = k_{20} \cdot (1.047)^{(T-20)}$ | Standard reference at 20°C | Kinetics |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for COD formula:** "**A minus B times N times 8 thousand over V**" — just remember that 8000 comes from the milliequivalent weight of oxygen (8) multiplied by 1000 (to convert equivalents to milliequivalents in mg/L when sample is in mL).
